## 121. Lady's Smock

**Species Name:** Lady's Smock
**Latin Name:** Cardamine pratensis
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** ladys-smock
**Seasons:** Mar, Apr, May, Jun

---

**Short Description:**
Lady's smock (*Cardamine pratensis*) is one of the first wild flowers to colour damp meadows in spring. The pale lilac-pink blooms and young leaves both taste of pepper and watercress. Pick the flowers as a garnish or scatter them through a salad. Pick the young leaves from March before the plant flowers.

---

**Full Description:**
Lady's smock belongs to the bittercress family, and the flavour gives it away immediately: a clean, sharp peppery heat close to rocket or watercress, with a slight bitterness in the older leaves. The flowers are milder than the leaves and more decorative. Both are good raw.

The plant grows in damp ground: wet meadows, riverbanks, roadsides prone to flooding, churchyards with heavy soil. It often appears in sheets across a damp field in April, the pale pink flowers catching the light. Pick the whole flowering stem and strip what you want back home, or pick individual flowers on site.

The leaves are available from late February onwards if the winter has been mild. Once the plant flowers in earnest through April and May, the leaves become more strongly flavoured. After flowering, the plant puts its energy into its long, narrow seedpods. The seedpods are edible too, with the same peppery character.

Lady's smock is also known as cuckooflower, a name tied to its habit of appearing around the time the first cuckoos are heard — typically mid to late April in southern England.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Lady's smock is a slender plant reaching 15–60cm, with a basal rosette of pinnate leaves and an upright flowering stem. Basal leaves have rounded leaflets 5–15mm wide; stem leaves are narrower and more lance-shaped. Flowers are four-petalled, pale lilac to near-white, 10–18mm across, carried in a loose cluster at the top of the stem. Petals have fine dark veining. The flowers appear before many other pink-flowered species and the habitat — damp, often flooded ground — is a strong indicator. Crush a leaf and smell it: the peppery bittercress scent is immediate. Seedpods are long (20–40mm) and narrow, standing upright as they develop.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Lady's smock has no dangerous lookalikes. Other *Cardamine* species growing in similar habitats — including wavy bittercress (*Cardamine flexuosa*) and hairy bittercress (*Cardamine hirsuta*) — are all edible and share the same peppery flavour. The flowers of lady's smock are distinctive: pale lilac, four-petalled, and carried at the top of an upright stem in damp grassland. No toxic pink-flowered plants share this precise combination of habitat, leaf shape, and smell.

Cuckoo pint / lords and ladies (*Arum maculatum*) sometimes grows nearby in hedgebanks at the field edge. Its arrow-shaped glossy leaves are quite different from the pinnate leaves of lady's smock. Crush a leaf: lady's smock smells of pepper; lords and ladies has no such smell. Lords and ladies is toxic.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Wet meadows, damp roadsides, riverbanks, floodplain grassland, and occasionally wet woodland rides. Common throughout Britain, though less frequent in areas of intensive agriculture where such meadows have been drained. Particularly abundant in the Welsh borders, northern England, and Scotland. Often grows alongside ragged robin, marsh marigold, and meadow buttercup. Absent from very acidic or very dry ground.

---

**In Season:**
Leaves from late February or early March; flowers from late March in the south to May in upland and northern areas. The main flowering window is April to May. Seedpods follow from May. By June the plant has largely died back. Pick leaves while they are young and the flavour is fresh. The flowers have a short window — a fortnight of peak bloom is typical in any given location.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw: scatter flowers over salads, egg dishes, or open sandwiches for flavour and colour. Add young leaves to green salads or to any dish that benefits from a peppery note. The flavour holds up alongside blue cheese, smoked fish, and cured meats. Cooked: the leaves wilt quickly in a hot pan and can be used in place of watercress or rocket in a warm salad. The peppery heat softens but does not disappear. Seedpods: eat raw — they have a good snap and a sharper flavour than the leaves.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking flowers and leaves from public land, roadsides, and commons for personal use is permitted under the Countryside and Rights of Way Act 2000. Do not uproot the plant. Lady's smock is a significant food plant for orange-tip butterfly caterpillars; avoid stripping an entire plant or picking from small isolated colonies.

---

**SEO Title:** Lady's Smock — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick lady's smock flowers and young leaves from April in damp meadows. Peppery, watercress-like flavour. Good raw in salads, as a garnish, or wilted.

---
---

## 122. Bistort

**Species Name:** Bistort
**Latin Name:** Bistorta officinalis
**Type:** Greens
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** bistort
**Seasons:** Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul

---

**Short Description:**
Bistort (*Bistorta officinalis*) is the green at the heart of dock pudding, the traditional Cumbrian dish eaten on the Lake District fells for centuries. Young leaves are available from March and carry a mild, slightly astringent flavour that responds well to blanching. Outside its northern stronghold it grows in damp meadows across much of Britain, often in quantity.

---

**Full Description:**
Bistort is a plant of damp ground: riverside meadows, wet pasture, churchyards, and upland hay meadows in the north and west of England. In parts of Cumbria and the Yorkshire Dales it grows in enough quantity to have sustained a genuine regional food tradition. Dock pudding — made with bistort leaves, nettles, oatmeal, and onion — is still made and eaten in the area around Kentmere and Grasmere, and was a staple food of fell farming families through the lean months of early spring.

The leaves are the edible part. Young leaves in March and April are the best picking: mild, slightly earthy, with a subtle astringency that blanching softens. As the season progresses the leaves become tougher and more strongly flavoured. The plant flowers in May and June, producing dense pink spikes on upright stems above the leaves. After flowering, leaf quality declines.

The name "bistort" comes from the Latin for "twice-twisted", referring to the shape of the rhizome underground — a gnarled, S-shaped rootstock. The rhizome is edible but is not usually picked. The leaves are the target.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Bistort forms loose colonies in damp ground. Leaves grow from the base on long stalks, reaching 10–20cm; they are broad, oval to triangular with a heart-shaped or squared base, smooth on top, slightly waxy below. The underside is often noticeably paler than the upper surface. Stem leaves are smaller and clasp the stem. The flowering stem reaches 40–80cm, topped by a dense cylindrical spike of pale pink flowers 2–5cm long. The combination of large, broadly oval basal leaves in damp meadow and the distinctive pink flower spike is unmistakeable. The leaves have no strong smell when crushed.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Bistort has no dangerous lookalikes in the habitats where it grows. Broad-leaved dock (*Rumex obtusifolius*) and other docks grow in similar ground and can be confused when both are in early leaf. Docks are edible, though tougher and more bitter. The leaves of dock are generally narrower and more pointed than bistort, and dock leaves have a more prominent midrib. Neither is dangerous.

Butterbur (*Petasites hybridus*) produces large leaves in similar wet habitats but its leaves are much larger — up to 90cm across — and appear after the flowers in late spring. Butterbur is not recommended for eating in quantity.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Damp meadows, river valleys, streamsides, and wet upland pasture. Common in the north and west of England, particularly Cumbria, Lancashire, and the Yorkshire Dales; less frequent in the south and east. Often found in churchyards and old water meadows where traditional management has continued. Grows in loose colonies, sometimes covering significant areas of wet grassland. Tolerates periodic flooding.

---

**In Season:**
Leaves emerge from late February in mild years, reaching a useful size by March. The best picking is March to May, before the plant flowers and while the leaves are still young. The plant flowers May to June; after this the leaves become increasingly tough and astringent. A second, lighter flush of young growth can appear after the plant is cut or grazed in late summer, but the spring growth is the main picking season.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Blanch before eating: drop leaves into boiling water for two to three minutes, drain, and squeeze out the water. The astringency softens and the flavour becomes earthy and mild. Dock pudding: blanch bistort with young nettles and any other available spring greens, chop finely, mix with oatmeal and fried onion, season, and fry in patties or press into a loaf and slice. It is filling and genuinely good. Soup: add blanched bistort to leek and potato soup in place of or alongside other greens. The leaves can also be used raw in very small amounts — add a few young leaves to a green salad for their mild, slightly unusual character.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking leaves from public meadows and commons for personal use is permitted. Bistort is not a protected species. In areas where dock pudding is a living tradition, established picking grounds on common land are well-known locally. Pick sustainably from large colonies; avoid stripping small patches. The plant regenerates reliably from its rhizome.

---

**SEO Title:** Bistort — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick bistort leaves from March in damp meadows. The key ingredient in Cumbrian dock pudding. Blanch and use in traditional spring greens recipes.

---
---

## 123. Scurvygrass

**Species Name:** Scurvygrass
**Latin Name:** Cochlearia officinalis
**Type:** Coastal
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** scurvygrass
**Seasons:** Mar, Apr, May, Jun

---

**Short Description:**
Scurvygrass (*Cochlearia officinalis*) grows along sea cliffs, saltmarshes, and upland streamsides. The small, fleshy leaves taste of hot horseradish and mustard. It was carried on ships for centuries as a remedy against scurvy, which is a reasonable use given the flavour and the vitamin content. Pick it fresh and eat it raw.

---

**Full Description:**
Scurvygrass is a small, sprawling plant of coastal and upland habitats. It belongs to the cabbage family, and the flavour makes this clear: a sharp, hot, horseradish-like punch from the first bite, followed by a clean bitterness. It is a strong flavour and a little goes a long way.

The name is earned. Sailors who lacked fresh vegetables on long voyages showed signs of scurvy within weeks. Scurvygrass, with its high vitamin C content and availability at many coastal ports, was one of the few fresh greens they could take on board or pick in harbour. The Dutch are said to have maintained entire scurvygrass gardens for this purpose.

Today the interest is the flavour, not the vitamin content. The leaves are best in spring, before and during flowering, when they are most tender and have their full peppery character. The plant produces small white four-petalled flowers from March to May, earlier than many coastal species. After flowering the leaves become tougher and the flavour more intense.

Scurvygrass has spread inland along salted roads and motorway verges in recent decades, where gritting in winter creates a salty strip that suits it. Roadside scurvygrass is best avoided: the leaves are close to the tarmac and subject to traffic pollution and road salt contamination.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Scurvygrass is a low-growing plant reaching 5–40cm, depending on exposure and growing conditions. Basal leaves are heart-shaped to kidney-shaped, fleshy, 1–3cm wide, bright green and slightly succulent. Stem leaves are smaller and clasp the stem. Flowers are white, four-petalled, 4–8mm across, in compact clusters that extend as pods develop. Pods are round to oval, 4–7mm. The combination of small fleshy rounded leaves, coastal or upland habitat, and hot horseradish taste is definitive. Crush a leaf: the pungent smell is immediate.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Scurvygrass has no dangerous lookalikes in the coastal habitats where it grows. Sea sandwort (*Honckenya peploides*) grows on sandy shores and has similar fleshy leaves, but its leaves are pointed at the tip and have no peppery smell or flavour. Shoreweed (*Littorella uniflora*) grows at the edge of freshwater lochs and has grass-like leaves: quite different.

Inland roadside scurvygrass is not dangerous but is not worth picking for the reasons above.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Sea cliffs, coastal rocks, saltmarshes, shingle above the strandline, and upland streamsides and mountain ledges. Tolerates salt spray and exposed conditions that most plants cannot. Found throughout the British coastline, from Kent to the Shetland Islands. Particularly abundant on the coasts of Scotland, Wales, and the west coast of England. Inland populations occur on mountain ledges and beside upland streams, particularly in Scotland and northern England. Roadside populations have expanded significantly since the widespread adoption of winter road salting.

---

**In Season:**
March to June, with flowering from March on sheltered southern coasts to May in exposed northern and upland sites. The leaves are available whenever the plant is in growth, which in mild coastal locations can mean much of the year. Spring growth is the most tender. After the plant sets seed in June and July, it begins to die back and the leaves become fibrous and intensely flavoured. Pick before and during flowering for the best eating.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw: use sparingly. A few leaves go well in any dish that needs a hit of pepper and acidity — green salads, oyster garnishes, grilled fish, or scattered over warm potatoes with good butter. The flavour is too intense for large quantities raw. Mixed into butter or a cream cheese: chop finely and combine; the dairy softens the heat. Used as a garnish it performs better than it does as a main green. The flowers are also edible and have a milder flavour than the leaves.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking leaves from coastal public land, cliffs, and commons is permitted for personal use. Do not uproot the plant. On National Trust or English/Natural Resources Wales coastal land, personal picking is generally permitted but commercial picking is not. Scurvygrass is not a protected species in Britain.

---

**SEO Title:** Scurvygrass — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick scurvygrass from sea cliffs and coastal rocks from March. Hot, horseradish-sharp flavour. Use sparingly raw as a garnish with fish or salad.

---
---

## 124. Sea Beet

**Species Name:** Sea Beet
**Latin Name:** Beta vulgaris subsp. maritima
**Type:** Coastal
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** sea-beet
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct

---

**Short Description:**
Sea beet (*Beta vulgaris* subsp. *maritima*) is the wild ancestor of beetroot, chard, and spinach beet. It grows in quantity along shingle beaches, sea walls, and cliff bases around most of the British coastline. The leaves are thick, dark, and glossy, with a rich flavour considerably better than shop-bought spinach. Cook them as you would chard: briefly, with butter.

---

**Full Description:**
Sea beet is a plant worth knowing. It grows in large patches on coastal shingle, among sea wall rubble, on the upper edges of saltmarshes, and at the base of sea cliffs. The leaves are available for much of the year on sheltered coasts; in practice, summer through early autumn gives the best combination of leaf size and flavour.

The leaves are thick and fleshy, with a waxy surface that holds up well to heat. The flavour is spinach-like but stronger, with a mild saline quality from growing in coastal soils, and a natural sweetness in the midribs and stems. The midribs are worth eating separately: slice them out and cook a minute or two longer than the leaf blades.

Every cultivated beetroot, chard, sugar beet, and spinach beet grown commercially traces its origins to this plant. The relationship is visible in the leaf texture and the deep red colouration sometimes present in the stems and veins of wild plants. Sea beet does not produce an enlarged taproot like cultivated beetroot — all the effort goes into the leaves.

Pick outer leaves from established plants, leaving the growing crown intact. Sea beet is perennial and tolerates repeated picking from the same plant across the season.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Sea beet is a sprawling to semi-upright plant reaching 30–100cm, with glossy, dark green leaves on long stalks. Leaves are 5–15cm, broadly triangular to diamond-shaped with a wavy margin; the surface is distinctly glossy compared to most coastal greens. Stems and leaf veins are often flushed red or pink. Flowers appear from June to September: tiny, greenish, densely packed on long branched spikes. The glossy triangular leaves, reddish stems, coastal habitat, and sprawling growth habit distinguish it from other coastal plants. No dangerous plants share this combination.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Sea beet has no dangerous lookalikes in British coastal habitats. Orache (*Atriplex* spp.) grows in similar coastal ground and can look broadly similar when young: it has similar triangular leaves but these are mealy or powdery on the underside (rub a finger across the back of an orache leaf and a white powder comes off), and the leaf surface is dull rather than glossy. Orache is edible.

Sea beet is not easily confused with anything harmful. The glossy, broad, reddish-stemmed leaves on coastal shingle are specific.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Shingle beaches, sea walls, cliff bases, coastal waste ground, and the upper edges of saltmarshes. Found around most of the British coastline, though less common on exposed sandy beaches and on the more extreme exposed Atlantic coasts of the north and west. Particularly abundant on the shingle coasts of Norfolk, Suffolk, Essex, Kent, and along the south coast. Often grows in the same zone as sea kale, sea rocket, and orache.

---

**In Season:**
Leaves are available for most of the year on sheltered coasts. The best picking is June to October, when the leaves are large and in good condition. Winter leaves become tough and strongly flavoured. New spring growth from March is tender and mild but leaves are small. By summer, established plants produce large leaves in quantity. Avoid picking once the plant is heavily in flower and the leaves begin to look tatty — usually by late September in the south, though some plants remain in good condition into October.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Cooked: sea beet is best cooked. Wash thoroughly to remove any salt or sand. Wilt in a hot pan with butter or olive oil: the leaves reduce significantly, like spinach, but the flavour is more robust. Season with salt, pepper, and a squeeze of lemon. Serve alongside fish — it is one of the best wild greens for pairing with any seafood. The midribs need slightly longer cooking: add them to the pan a minute before the leaf blades. As a gratin: layer with cream and hard cheese and bake at 180°C for 20–25 minutes. As a filling for pasta or pastry: wilt, squeeze dry, and season generously. Raw: very young, small leaves can be used raw in salads, but the texture is better cooked.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking leaves from coastal public land and foreshore for personal use is permitted. Do not uproot the plant. Sea beet is perennial and picks well repeatedly; taking outer leaves from large established plants is sustainable. Commercial picking without permission from the landowner is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Sea Beet — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick sea beet leaves from coastal shingle and sea walls from June. The wild ancestor of chard and spinach. Cook briefly with butter — excellent with fish.

---
---

## 125. Sea Aster

**Species Name:** Sea Aster
**Latin Name:** Tripolium pannonicum
**Type:** Coastal
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** sea-aster
**Seasons:** Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Sea aster (*Tripolium pannonicum*) grows in saltmarshes and tidal mudflats around the British coast. The succulent leaves and young stems are edible — mildly salty, slightly bitter, with a pleasant crunch. The purple daisy-like flowers arrive in late summer. Pick leaves from spring through to the end of summer; the plant is one of a small number of saltmarsh greens worth eating in quantity.

---

**Full Description:**
Sea aster is the dominant flowering plant of the middle and lower saltmarsh in Britain. In August and September it colours whole mudflats purple. The leaves and stems are what most foragers want: thick, fleshy, bright green, with a mild saline flavour and firm texture that cooks well or eats adequately raw.

The plant is a true halophyte — adapted to grow in ground regularly flooded by seawater. The fleshy leaves retain water and salts, which gives them their characteristic texture and flavour. Pick them and you need very little added salt in cooking.

Sea aster is the same genus as the michaelmas daisy (*Aster* species) grown in gardens, and the flavour connection is subtle but there. The leaves have a slight bitterness alongside the saltiness; blanching removes the bitterness and leaves a clean, mild green with good body. The stems, when young and still succulent, can be eaten too: strip them before the plant flowers, when they are still pliant and less fibrous.

This species was reclassified from *Aster tripolium* to *Tripolium pannonicum* but the older name appears frequently in field guides.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Sea aster is a succulent annual or short-lived perennial, reaching 15–100cm depending on conditions. It is shorter and stockier in exposed tidal marsh, taller in sheltered estuaries. Leaves are 4–12cm, narrow, lance-shaped, fleshy and smooth, bright green. The leaf edges are entire or very finely toothed. Stems are also fleshy and often branched near the top. Flowers arrive from July to October: daisy-like heads 15–25mm across with lilac to purple ray florets and a yellow disc centre. Some plants produce ray florets only (discoid form) and appear entirely yellow; this variant is less common. The combination of succulent narrow leaves, saltmarsh habitat, and purple daisy flowers is specific to this species.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Sea aster has no dangerous lookalikes in the saltmarsh habitats where it grows. Sea purslane (*Atriplex portulacoides*) grows in the same zone with similar succulent grey-green leaves, but its leaves are oval and covered in a mealy grey coating: clearly different in texture and colour. Sea arrow grass (*Triglochin maritima*), which also grows in saltmarsh, has very narrow grass-like leaves and does not resemble sea aster.

Common ragwort (*Jacobaea vulgaris*) grows on drier coastal grassland and has yellow flowers — quite different from sea aster's purple daisies. Ragwort leaves are also not succulent and smell unpleasant.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Saltmarshes, tidal mudflats, creek edges, and the upper saltmarsh zone. Found around most of the British coastline wherever saltmarsh develops: particularly abundant in the estuaries of Essex, Norfolk, Suffolk, Hampshire, and Scotland's west coast firths. Grows alongside sea purslane, glasswort, thrift, and sea lavender. Less common on exposed beaches or cliff habitats; this is a plant of sheltered tidal ground.

---

**In Season:**
Leaves are available from April, when new growth emerges from the base of overwintered plants. The best picking is May to July, before the plant flowers, when the leaves are most tender. Flowers arrive from July through September; after this the leaves become tougher. Pick leaves and young stems early in the season for best eating. The flowers themselves are edible and make a useful garnish.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Blanch first: drop leaves into boiling water for two minutes, drain, and cool. This removes excess bitterness and salt. Serve warm with butter, olive oil, or alongside grilled or baked fish. Sea aster pairs naturally with anything from the same coastal zone — crab, mussels, samphire, sea bass. Stir-fried: heat a little oil in a very hot pan, add the leaves, toss for two to three minutes. Season minimally given the natural salt content. The texture holds up better than spinach. Raw: very young leaves can be added in small amounts to coastal-themed salads, but the flavour is more useful once blanched.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking sea aster from public saltmarsh and foreshore for personal use is permitted. Many productive saltmarshes fall within nature reserves or SSSIs — picking for personal use is generally still permitted in these areas, but check any local bylaws before picking in quantity. Do not uproot the plant.

---

**SEO Title:** Sea Aster — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick sea aster leaves and stems from saltmarshes from spring. Mildly salty, slightly bitter. Blanch and serve with butter or alongside fish and shellfish.

---
---

## 126. Sea Rocket

**Species Name:** Sea Rocket
**Latin Name:** Cakile maritima
**Type:** Coastal
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** sea-rocket
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Sea rocket (*Cakile maritima*) grows along the upper edge of sandy and shingle beaches. It tastes exactly as the name suggests: peppery and sharp, like garden rocket with more heat. The leaves, flowers, and unripe seedpods are all edible raw. Find it above the strandline where the beach meets the dune or bank.

---

**Full Description:**
Sea rocket is a specialist of the beach strandline and the loose sand or shingle just above it. It grows where few other plants can: in the debris of the high tide mark, rooted in ground that shifts with every storm, tolerating salt spray and full exposure. The seeds are buoyant and disperse by sea. This is a plant adapted to some of the most difficult ground in Britain.

The flavour belongs firmly in the mustard and rocket family: a sharp, peppery heat with a slightly bitter edge. Raw leaves are the main event. They are small, succulent, and deeply lobed, with a pleasant crunch. The flowers — white to pale lilac, four-petalled — are milder than the leaves and make a useful garnish. The young seedpods, picked before they harden, are good too: they have the same peppery character with more snap.

Sea rocket is not abundant in the way that sea beet or sea purslane can be. The plants are smaller and more scattered, limited to the beach strand above the tide. It is worth picking when you find it: a handful of leaves is enough to lift any salad or fish dish. Do not strip a single plant; pick a few leaves from several.

The plant is an annual, completing its cycle from germination to seed in a single summer. The seedpods separate into two segments as they ripen: the upper segment floats away to seed new beaches, the lower remains at the plant.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Sea rocket is a low-growing sprawling plant reaching 15–45cm, with fleshy, deeply pinnately lobed leaves 2–7cm long. The leaves are bright to grey-green, succulent, and sometimes slightly waxy. Flowers are four-petalled, white or pale lilac to purple-pink, 10–14mm across, in tight clusters at the stem tips. Seedpods are distinctive: 1.5–2.5cm, two-jointed, the upper segment beaked. The plant grows exclusively on beaches, shingle, or dunes above the tide. The combination of habitat, fleshy lobed leaves, and peppery smell and taste is definitive.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Sea rocket has no dangerous lookalikes on British beaches. Prickly saltwort (*Salsola kali*) grows on the same beaches but has narrow, stiff, spine-tipped leaves: easy to distinguish. Sea sandwort (*Honckenya peploides*) forms mats on sandy shores but has oval, unlobed leaves and is much smaller. Neither is dangerous.

No toxic plants closely resemble sea rocket in its coastal strand habitat.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Sandy and shingle beaches, particularly the upper strandline and the foreshore immediately inland of the tide's reach. Also on mobile dune systems and coastal waste ground. Common around most of the British coastline on beaches where sand or fine shingle accumulates. Often grows with sea rocket, sea sandwort, sea purslane, and sea beet in the same upper beach zone. Less frequent on exposed rock or clay cliffs. Easily spotted while walking along a beach — look for spreading, slightly succulent plants above the strandline, often growing out of drift material.

---

**In Season:**
Germination and growth from May; main picking season June to September. The plant is in its best condition from June, when it is fully established but not yet heavily seeding. By late September, plants on exposed beaches are beginning to die back after setting seed. Pick leaves, flowers, and young pods through summer. The pods harden and become woody as the season progresses; pick them while they are still firm and green.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw: best raw. Add to green salads, scatter over grilled or baked fish, use as a garnish on oysters or ceviche. The peppery heat needs no dressing. The flowers soften the effect slightly and are worth using for colour. Young pods: eat as you go on the beach, or add to a beach salad. Wilted: a handful of leaves can be wilted in a hot pan with oil for 30–60 seconds; they collapse quickly and the flavour concentrates. Serve alongside any fish or seafood.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking leaves, flowers, and pods from public beaches and foreshore for personal use is permitted. Sea rocket grows on ground managed by local councils, the National Trust, and private landowners; personal picking is generally unproblematic. Do not remove whole plants. On beaches within nature reserves, check any local restrictions before picking in quantity.

---

**SEO Title:** Sea Rocket — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find sea rocket on sandy and shingle beaches from June. Peppery, mustard-sharp flavour. Pick leaves and young pods to eat raw with fish or in salads.

---
---

## 127. Wood Avens

**Species Name:** Wood Avens
**Latin Name:** Geum urbanum
**Type:** Greens
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** wood-avens
**Seasons:** Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug

---

**Short Description:**
Wood avens (*Geum urbanum*) is one of the most overlooked edible plants in British hedgerows and woodland edges. The roots smell strongly of cloves. The young leaves are edible in spring. Both have uses in the kitchen, but the roots are the main attraction: dig a small quantity, scrub them, and use them to infuse any drink, cream, or sauce that would benefit from warm spice.

---

**Full Description:**
Wood avens grows almost everywhere in Britain: hedgebanks, woodland edges, shaded roadsides, and garden borders. It tolerates deep shade and poor soil and tends to be ignored, partly because it looks unremarkable for much of the year and partly because it is one of those plants that requires processing rather than picking and eating.

The clove-like scent of the roots is the plant's best quality. Dig a small root, scrub it, and hold it to your nose: the smell is warm, aromatic, and genuinely reminiscent of cloves. This is because wood avens roots contain eugenol, the same volatile compound responsible for the flavour of cloves. The root can be used to infuse milk, cream, or custard; to steep in spirits; or to flavour a sauce. The flavour is more delicate than dried cloves and does not overpower.

The leaves are edible in spring when young: mild, slightly bitter, with a faint spice note. They are not the primary reason to pick wood avens, but small quantities of tender young leaves can go into a green salad.

Wood avens is known by several old English names, including herb bennet, reflecting its long history as a kitchen and medicinal herb. It was hung in houses to deter flies and used to flavour ale before hops became dominant. The plant hybridises with water avens (*Geum rivale*) where both species grow together, producing plants with intermediate characteristics and, confusingly, intermediate root quality.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Wood avens is a hairy perennial reaching 20–60cm. Basal leaves are pinnate with a large rounded terminal leaflet (up to 5cm) and two or more pairs of smaller unequal leaflets below. Stem leaves are simpler, often three-lobed. Flowers are small, yellow, five-petalled, 8–15mm across, carried on slender upright stems. The flowers lack the visual impact of buttercups. Seedheads are distinctive: rounded burrs of hooked red-tipped achenes that cling to clothing and animal fur. The roots are reddish-brown, fibrous, and thin; the clove smell is immediate when one is scraped. Habitat — shaded or semi-shaded ground — combined with the pinnate basal leaves, small yellow flowers, and clinging burrs identifies wood avens reliably.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Water avens (*Geum rivale*) is the most likely confusion, growing in similar habitats near streams and in damp woodland. It is also edible, with roots that smell of cloves, and the identification distinction does not affect safety. Water avens has larger, nodding flowers in purple-pink and cream; wood avens flowers are smaller and upright yellow. In practice the two species hybridise freely and intermediate plants are common. All *Geum* species in Britain are edible.

Silverweed (*Potentilla anserina*) has similar pinnate leaves but grows in open ground, not shade, and has much more conspicuously silvery undersides to the leaves. It is also edible.

Creeping cinquefoil (*Potentilla reptans*) has similar yellow flowers but five-lobed palmate leaves rather than pinnate. Also edible.

No dangerous plant closely resembles wood avens in woodland edge habitats.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Hedgebanks, woodland edges, shaded roadsides, churchyards, and the shaded edges of gardens. Common throughout Britain, including urban areas. Tolerates deep shade and is found at the base of north-facing walls and under dense tree canopy where few other plants survive. One of the most ubiquitous and under-exploited hedgerow plants in the country.

---

**In Season:**
Leaves: March to May, while young. Roots: available year-round, but best in spring before the plant flowers, or in autumn when the plant has died back and energy is concentrated in the root. The root is small — a single plant yields only a few thin rootlets. Dig only what you need and replant the crown if you want the plant to persist.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Root infusion: scrub two or three small roots (roughly 5–10cm total) and steep in warm milk, cream, or custard for 20 minutes. Remove the roots. Use the infused liquid in rice pudding, panna cotta, crème brûlée, or any baked custard. The flavour is mild clove with a slight bitter wood note. Steep in spirits: push two or three clean roots into a small bottle of vodka or whisky and leave for a week. The result is a gently spiced spirit. Sauce base: add a root to a reducing sauce alongside red wine and shallots; remove before serving. The leaves: raw, add small amounts to a mixed salad in spring. They are not the plant's best offering but worth knowing about.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking leaves and digging roots for personal use from public land, commons, and roadsides is permitted, though digging always requires the landowner's permission on private land. Pick roots sustainably: take from large established populations and leave the crown in the ground. Wood avens is not a protected species.

---

**SEO Title:** Wood Avens — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find wood avens in hedgebanks and woodland edges year-round. Clove-scented roots for infusing custards, cream, and spirits. Young leaves edible in spring.

---
---

## 128. Raspberry

**Species Name:** Raspberry
**Latin Name:** Rubus idaeus
**Type:** Berries
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** raspberry
**Seasons:** Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Wild raspberries (*Rubus idaeus*) fruit from July in woodland clearings, scrub, and upland hillsides. They are smaller than cultivated varieties and considerably better: more concentrated, more fragrant, with a sharpness that softens as the fruit darkens to deep pink. Pick them as you find them and eat them straight from the cane.

---

**Full Description:**
Raspberry is a native British shrub. The canes grow tall and arch over in their second year, bearing fruit along the whole length in summer. Find them in clearings where light has reached the woodland floor, on the edges of conifer plantations, on upland hillsides and moorland margins, and on roadside banks in the north and west. In Scotland in particular, wild raspberries grow in quantity in the right conditions.

The fruit is smaller than anything available in a shop, which is partly why it tastes better. The flavour is concentrated: intensely fragrant, sweet with a clean sharpness, the seeds noticeable but not unpleasant. A handful of wild raspberries eaten from the cane on a warm July afternoon is one of the better arguments for going outside.

The canes fruit on second-year growth. First-year canes (primocanes) are upright, unfruited, and covered in soft reddish bristles. Second-year canes (floricanes) are slightly woodier and will have the fruiting side shoots. Both can be present together in an established colony. The canes die after fruiting and are replaced by new growth from the root system.

The leaves can be picked in spring for a mild herbal tea: dry them and steep for five minutes. The flavour is gentle and grassy. Raspberry leaf tea has a long history of use in late pregnancy, though this is outside the scope of what this site covers.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Raspberry canes reach 1–1.5m, with upright then arching growth. Stems are round in cross-section, covered in fine reddish-brown bristles. Leaves are pinnate with three to seven leaflets, each 3–7cm, with a toothed margin and a distinctly white-felted underside. Flowers are white, five-petalled, 10–15mm, drooping slightly, carried in small clusters. Fruit is the familiar raspberry shape: a cluster of drupelets forming a domed thimble, pulling cleanly away from the conical white receptacle when ripe. Colour runs from pale pink at early ripeness through deep red-pink when fully ripe. The white underside of the leaves is a reliable field mark.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Wild raspberry has no dangerous lookalikes. Bramble (*Rubus fruticosus*) grows in similar habitats and also produces clusters of drupelet fruit, but the fruit is black when ripe, the canes have stronger, flattened thorns rather than bristles, and the leaves do not have a white underside. Bramble fruit is edible.

Dewberry (*Rubus caesius*) produces a bluer-black fruit with fewer, larger drupelets, on much lower-growing canes. Also edible.

No toxic species resembles raspberry closely enough to cause confusion.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Woodland clearings, the edges of conifer plantations, scrubby hillsides, moorland margins, upland streamsides, and roadside banks in the north and west. Prefers slightly acidic, reasonably well-drained soil with good light. Common in Scotland, Wales, northern England, and the west country; less frequent in the lowland arable east. Often found along old railway embankments and in the scrub that follows clear-felling in commercial forestry. Tends to grow in patches rather than single plants.

---

**In Season:**
Fruit ripens from July in the south, later in upland and northern sites — August is peak season in Scotland. By September the crop is finishing. Individual canes in a single colony ripen at slightly different times; visiting the same patch repeatedly through summer pays off. Pick when the fruit pulls easily from the receptacle and the colour is a deep, even pink-red. Under-ripe fruit is pale and does not pull cleanly; over-ripe fruit is very dark and soft, still edible but best used immediately.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw: eat straight from the cane. They keep poorly — better to eat them in the field than try to transport them. Take a small container if picking seriously. Jam: raspberry jam sets readily due to natural pectin, but wild fruit yields are usually too small for a full batch unless you find an exceptional patch. Mix with other summer fruit. Vinegar: steep a generous handful of ripe berries in white wine vinegar for a week, strain and sweeten lightly. Use on salads or with grilled duck or game. Sauce: simmer briefly with a little sugar and strain. Goes with vanilla ice cream, cheesecake, or strong blue cheese. Freeze: spread berries on a tray to freeze individually, then bag them. They freeze well and retain their flavour for months.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking wild raspberries from public land, woodland, and commons for personal use is permitted. In Forestry England and Forestry Commission Scotland managed woodland, personal picking of fruit is generally uncontested. Do not pick commercially without the landowner's permission.

---

**SEO Title:** Raspberry — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find wild raspberries in woodland clearings and upland scrub from July. Smaller and more intense than cultivated varieties. Eat fresh, make jam, or freeze.

---
---

## 129. Rosehip

**Species Name:** Rosehip
**Latin Name:** Rosa canina
**Type:** Berries
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** rosehip
**Seasons:** Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Rosehips are the fruit of the dog rose (*Rosa canina*), common in hedgerows across Britain. They ripen to a deep red in September and stay on the branch into winter. The flesh around the seeds is sweet and gently acidic, tasting of apples and rose. Use them for syrup, jelly, or tea. The seeds and hairs inside the hip are an irritant and must be removed before eating.

---

**Full Description:**
The dog rose is Britain's most common wild rose. It scrambles through hedgerows and scrub throughout the country, producing pale pink flowers in June and red hips from September. The hips are the fruit: oval to bottle-shaped, 1–2cm long, smooth and shiny, turning from green to orange to deep scarlet as autumn progresses.

The flavour of the flesh is good: sweet, slightly astringent, with a character that combines apple, rose water, and a gentle tartness. There is not a great deal of flesh relative to the seed mass inside, which is why rosehip syrup is the most efficient way to use them. A kilo of hips produces a useful quantity of syrup.

Inside each hip is a cluster of seeds surrounded by small, stiff, white hairs. These hairs are mechanically irritating to mucous membranes — they were used as itching powder by children for exactly this reason. They must be removed before eating rosehips in any form. When making syrup or jelly, straining through a fine cloth or muslin removes them. If eating raw, cut the hip open, scrape out the seeds and hairs, and eat the flesh walls only.

Rosehip syrup has a well-established history as a winter supplement for children in Britain. During the Second World War, the Ministry of Food ran national rosehip picking campaigns to offset the loss of citrus imports. The rosehip contains significant vitamin C, though the exact content varies and cooking reduces it. The flavour is the main reason to make the syrup.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Dog rose is a scrambling shrub, reaching 1–3m, with strong, recurved thorns on arching stems. Leaves are pinnate with five to seven leaflets, 1.5–4cm, oval with toothed margins. Flowers are 4–6cm across, five-petalled, pale to deep pink or occasionally white, with a prominent yellow stamen cluster, flowering June to July. Hips are 1–2cm, oval to flask-shaped, smooth, turning red in September. The five sepals often remain attached at the top of the ripe hip, giving it a distinctive capped appearance. Other wild rose species — field rose (*Rosa arvensis*), sweet briar (*Rosa rubiginosa*) — also produce edible hips and can be used in the same way.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Rosehips have no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British berry closely resembles the familiar red, smooth-skinned, oval hip of the dog rose. Black bryony (*Tamus communis*) produces small red berries in hedgerows, but these are in clusters on a climbing vine with large heart-shaped shiny leaves: nothing like a hip. Black bryony berries are toxic.

White bryony (*Bryonia dioica*) also produces small red berries; it is a climbing plant with lobed leaves and coiling tendrils, quite different from rose.

The internal hairs of the hip are the main practical hazard: they must be removed completely before eating.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Hedgerows, scrub, woodland edges, chalk downland, roadside banks, and coastal clifftops. Found throughout Britain, including Scotland, though more common in the south and east. Dog rose grows in almost any well-drained soil with reasonable light. It is one of the most widespread native shrubs in the country. Look for the distinctive scrambling canes with their recurved thorns in autumn; the red hips are visible from a distance.

---

**In Season:**
Hips colour from late August but are best picked from September, when they are fully ripe and the flesh has softened slightly. They remain on the bush through October and November, sweetening after the first frosts. Frost-softened hips are the best for syrup: the flesh is more easily extracted after freezing. By December, hips have usually shrivelled or been taken by birds. Fieldfare, redwing, and waxwing feed heavily on rosehips during winter irruptions; in a poor berry year, a good rosehip hedge can attract large flocks.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Syrup: wash and top-and-tail 1kg of hips. Simmer in 1.5 litres of water for 20 minutes. Strain through muslin overnight — do not squeeze or the hairs will pass through. Repeat the simmering with the pulp and a further litre of water; strain again. Combine the liquids, add 500g sugar per litre, dissolve over heat, and bottle while hot. The syrup keeps for several months unopened; refrigerate after opening. Use on porridge, yoghurt, or to make a long drink with sparkling water. Jelly: follow the same process but set with added pectin or combine with crab apple, which provides natural pectin. Tea: dry hips in a low oven (80°C for several hours), remove the seeds and hairs, and steep the dried flesh in boiling water for 10 minutes. Raw: cut a ripe hip lengthways, scrape out all seeds and hairs, and eat the flesh walls. The flavour is worth trying.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking rosehips from public land, roadsides, commons, and hedgerows for personal use is permitted. Dog rose is not protected. Do not cut or damage the plant. Hips on private land are the landowner's property. Large-scale or commercial picking without permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Rosehip — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick rosehips from hedgerows from September. Sweet, apple-flavoured flesh makes excellent syrup and jelly. Remove seeds and hairs before eating.

---
---

## 130. Crab Apple

**Species Name:** Crab Apple
**Latin Name:** Malus sylvestris
**Type:** Berries
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** crab-apple
**Seasons:** Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
The crab apple (*Malus sylvestris*) is Britain's only native wild apple. The fruit is small, hard, and very sour — inedible raw in any quantity but one of the best natural sources of pectin in the hedgerow, making it excellent for setting jellies and preserves. Find it in old hedgerows, ancient woodland, and scrubby grassland. Cultivated apple escapes (*Malus domestica*) are common in similar habitats; both are edible.

---

**Full Description:**
True crab apple is a spiny, slow-growing tree found in old hedgerows, ancient woodland edges, and semi-natural scrub across much of Britain. The fruit is small — typically 1.5–3cm across — and ranges from yellow-green to red-flushed, depending on the individual tree. It is hard, very tart, and occasionally almost unbearably bitter. Eating one raw is an experience rather than a pleasure.

Cooked and sweetened, the picture changes entirely. Crab apple makes one of the best hedgerow jellies: a clear, deep amber set with excellent flavour. The high pectin content means the jelly sets reliably without added pectin, and the natural acidity gives it a sharpness that flatters roast pork, lamb, cheese, and game.

The distinction between true crab apple (*Malus sylvestris*) and feral or escaped cultivated apples (*Malus domestica*) matters botanically but not for foraging purposes. Cultivated apple escapes grow freely from hedgerow and roadside, self-seeded from discarded cores or from old orchard stock. The fruit is variable but usually larger and less bitter than true crab apple. Both are entirely edible and both make good jelly. Where the two species grow together they hybridise, producing intermediates. For foraging, use whatever you find: small, hard, very sour apple-like fruit from a tree in a hedgerow or wood is going to be a crab apple or close enough to it.

In a good year, a single mature crab apple tree can produce a very large crop. The fruit falls from September; windfalls are easier to collect than fruit still on the tree, which requires a stick or a long reach.

---

**Identification Notes:**
True crab apple (*Malus sylvestris*) is a small to medium tree reaching 4–10m, often spreading and somewhat spiny, particularly on younger growth. The spines are short, stiff, and can be prominent on hedgerow trees. Leaves are 3–5cm, oval to elliptical, with a finely toothed margin and a smooth upper surface. Flowers are white or pink-flushed, 3–4cm across, in April to May. Fruit is 1.5–3cm, green to yellow-green, sometimes red-flushed, with a persistent calyx (the brown star-shaped remnant at the base of the apple). True *M. sylvestris* has hairless leaf stalks and leaf undersides; *Malus domestica* and its hybrids typically have some hairiness. In practice this distinction requires close examination and intermediate plants make it unreliable in the field. Feral cultivated apple fruit is usually larger and sweeter than true crab apple.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Crab apple has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic tree in Britain produces small apple-like fruit. Hawthorn (*Crataegus monogyna*) grows in the same hedgerow habitat and produces red berries (haws) in autumn; these are clustered on the branch, not solitary, and are quite different in appearance from crab apple fruit.

The only practical concern is eating the seeds in very large quantities. Apple seeds contain amygdalin, a compound that releases small amounts of cyanide when metabolised. The quantities present in crab apple seeds are not dangerous in normal use, but avoid eating large quantities of seeds deliberately.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Old hedgerows, ancient woodland edges, wood pasture, and scrubby grassland throughout England and Wales. Less common in Scotland, where it is largely restricted to southern and central areas. Grows on a range of soils but prefers well-drained ground. Often found growing alongside hawthorn, blackthorn, and hazel in traditional laid hedges. Feral cultivated apple grows more widely, particularly on roadsides, railway banks, and at the edges of old orchards.

---

**In Season:**
Fruit ripens from late August in warm years, with the main crop falling through September and October. Windfalls are the easiest source: check under the tree after a windy night. Fruit remaining on the branch into November is often the most developed. After the first hard frosts the fruit softens and begins to deteriorate. Pick or collect promptly once ripe. In a poor year some trees produce very little; others fruit heavily almost every year.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Jelly: the classic use. Wash and roughly chop 1kg of crab apples — no need to peel or core. Simmer in enough water to cover until soft, roughly 30–40 minutes. Strain through muslin overnight. Measure the juice and add 400g sugar per 500ml. Bring to a rolling boil and skim. Test for set on a cold plate after 10 minutes. Bottle in sterilised jars while hot. The jelly is clear amber and sets firm. Crab apple and rosehip jelly: combine in equal quantities for a jelly with more complex flavour. Crab apple and sloe: good pairing for a tart, deep-flavoured jelly. Crab apple butter: cook softened pulp with sugar, spice, and a little butter; pass through a sieve for a smooth apple butter. Fermented: crab apple makes good cider when combined with sweeter varieties, though the full fruit does not have enough sugar for a conventional cider on its own. Pickled: whole small crab apples can be pickled in spiced vinegar; they keep well and are good alongside cold meat and cheese.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking crab apples from public land, commons, and public rights of way for personal use is permitted. Fruit from trees on private land is the landowner's property. Windfalls from roadside trees are generally uncontested for personal use. Commercial picking without permission is not permitted. Crab apple is a native species and not subject to any restriction on personal picking.

---

**SEO Title:** Crab Apple — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find crab apples in old hedgerows from September. Very sour but high in pectin — excellent for jelly. Pairs well with rosehip, sloe, and hedgerow fruit.

---
