## 141. Wild Gooseberry

**Species Name:** Wild Gooseberry
**Latin Name:** Ribes uva-crispa
**Type:** Berries
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** wild-gooseberry
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug

---

**Short Description:**
Wild gooseberry (*Ribes uva-crispa*) grows in old hedgerows, woodland edges, and scrub throughout Britain. The fruit is identical to the cultivated gooseberry — tart, green-yellow when ripe, with the same familiar papery skin and seeds. It is uncommon enough to be a find worth noting. Pick it in July and use it exactly as you would a shop gooseberry.

---

**Full Description:**
Wild gooseberry is genuinely native to Britain, though much of what you find in hedgerows is likely garden escape or self-seeded from cultivated stock rather than truly wild stock. The distinction is botanical rather than practical: the fruit is the same, the plant looks the same, and it tastes the same. Find it and pick it.

The plant is spiny — single or triple spines at each node along the stem — which makes picking slightly effortful. The berries sit individually on short stalks from the leaf axils rather than in clusters. A productive plant can carry a good number of berries, but they tend to be scattered and the picking is slow.

Wild gooseberry fruit is smaller than most cultivated varieties: typically 1–2cm. The flavour is sharply tart before full ripeness, softening and sweetening as the fruit ripens to yellow-green or occasionally red-flushed. The best eating is when the berry gives slightly to the touch and the flavour has developed some sweetness alongside the acidity. For cooking — jam, crumble, fool — pick slightly under-ripe fruit for a cleaner, sharper result.

The plant flowers very early: small, drooping, greenish-pink flowers appear in March and April, often before most other hedgerow shrubs have broken bud. The fruit that follows takes until June or July to ripen.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Wild gooseberry is a dense, spiny shrub reaching 1–1.5m. Stems have single or triple spines at each node, 5–12mm. Leaves are 2–5cm, rounded with three to five lobes and a toothed margin, slightly hairy. Flowers are drooping, tubular, 8–12mm, greenish to pinkish, appearing singly or in pairs in March to April. Fruit is 1–2cm, oval to rounded, with fine hairs on the skin giving it a slightly downy texture, greenish-yellow to yellow when ripe, occasionally reddish. The papery ridges running from the base of the berry to the tip (remnants of the calyx) are visible on close inspection. The combination of spiny shrub, lobed leaves, and hairy-skinned gooseberry-shaped fruit is unmistakeable.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Wild gooseberry has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British plant produces a similar gooseberry-shaped, hairy-skinned berry on a spiny shrub with lobed leaves. Cultivated gooseberry escapes may be red or purple-fruited varieties; all are edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Old hedgerows, scrubby woodland edges, and rocky ground, particularly on limestone and chalk. Genuinely native plants are most likely in ancient woodland and old established hedgerows away from habitation. Garden escapes are found more widely, including in urban hedges and on railway banks. Found throughout England and Wales; less common in Scotland. Low search volume (110 on the master list) reflects its genuine scarcity rather than lack of interest.

---

**In Season:**
Flowers March to April. Fruit develops through spring, reaching pickable size from late June. The main ripening window is July, with some fruit persisting into August. Pick when the berry gives slightly to the touch and the flavour has some sweetness. Over-ripe fruit becomes soft and loses structure quickly.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Exactly as cultivated gooseberry. Fool: simmer 300g of fruit with 2–3 tablespoons of sugar until collapsed; cool completely, then fold through 300ml of whipped cream. Serve cold. Jam: gooseberry jam sets readily due to high pectin, particularly from under-ripe fruit. Simmer with equal weight of sugar until set. Crumble: mix with elderflower cordial and bake under a crumble top — the combination is excellent. Sauce: reduce with sugar, a splash of water, and a few elderflower heads for a sauce alongside mackerel or other oily fish.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking wild gooseberries from public land, hedgerows, and woodland for personal use is permitted. The plant is not protected. Fruit on private land is the landowner's property.

---

**SEO Title:** Wild Gooseberry — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find wild gooseberries in old hedgerows from July. Tart, sharply flavoured, identical to cultivated varieties. Good for fool, jam, crumble, and sauce with fish.

---
---

## 142. Guelder Rose

**Species Name:** Guelder Rose
**Latin Name:** Viburnum opulus
**Type:** Berries
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** guelder-rose
**Seasons:** Sep, Oct

---

**Short Description:**
Guelder rose (*Viburnum opulus*) produces vivid red berries in autumn, hanging in heavy clusters from a shrub common in hedgerows, damp woodland, and scrub throughout Britain. The raw berries are toxic: they contain compounds that cause vomiting, diarrhoea, and cramps. Cooked and strained, the juice makes an excellent sharp, cranberry-like jelly. Do not eat the raw fruit.

---

**Full Description:**
Guelder rose is not a rose at all — it belongs to the same family as elder. It is a widespread native shrub, found in damp hedgerows, wet woodland edges, and beside streams and ditches. In autumn the red berry clusters are among the most striking sights in the British hedgerow: vivid, translucent, lacquer-red, hanging in flat-topped clusters from a shrub that is simultaneously dropping its leaves in orange-red tones.

The berries are toxic raw. They contain viburnum glycosides and tannins that, in any significant quantity, cause gastrointestinal distress: nausea, vomiting, and stomach cramps. A single berry tasted is unlikely to cause serious problems, but eating more than a few raw is not advisable. Cooking destroys the problematic compounds. The strained juice, cooked with sugar, is safe and genuinely good.

The flavour of cooked guelder rose is sharp and distinctive: somewhere between cranberry and redcurrant, with a slight bitterness and an astringency that makes it an excellent accompaniment to rich meat. The juice is vivid red and the jelly sets easily, helped by the fruit's natural pectin.

The seeds inside each berry are flat and should be strained out during processing.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Guelder rose is a deciduous shrub reaching 2–5m. Bark is grey-brown and slightly corky on older stems. Leaves are opposite, 5–10cm, with three to five pointed lobes and a coarsely toothed margin, somewhat maple-like in outline; the leaf stalk has two small glands near the top. Autumn colour is a variable orange-red, sometimes excellent. Flowers appear May to June in flat-topped clusters 5–10cm across: the outer ring of flowers is large, white, and sterile; the inner flowers are smaller and fertile. This two-zone flower head is diagnostic. Berries develop from the inner flowers: 8–10mm, round, vivid red, translucent, with a flat seed inside, in flat-topped clusters. The maple-like lobed leaves, two-zone flower clusters, and vivid red translucent berry clusters together identify guelder rose reliably.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
The raw berries are toxic: do not eat guelder rose berries without cooking them first.

Rowan (*Sorbus aucuparia*) also produces vivid red berry clusters in autumn, but rowan is a tree rather than a shrub, and its leaves are pinnate with multiple leaflets, not lobed like guelder rose. Rowan berries are edible after cooking.

Elderberry (*Sambucus nigra*) produces black berries in flat-topped clusters on a shrub with pinnate leaves: different in colour and leaf shape. Raw elderberries also cause nausea; cook them too.

No other common British hedgerow shrub produces red berries in flat-topped clusters on a plant with maple-like lobed leaves.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Damp hedgerows, wet woodland edges, scrub beside streams and ditches, and the margins of fens and wet meadows. Common throughout England and Wales; less frequent in Scotland. Thrives on reasonably fertile, damp soils. Often grows alongside elder, hawthorn, and dogwood in hedgerows on clay or alluvial ground. Frequently planted in parks and gardens for its ornamental flower heads and autumn colour.

---

**In Season:**
Berries ripen from late August and are at their best through September and October. They often persist on the shrub well into November and occasionally into winter. After the first frosts the berries soften slightly but the juice remains usable. Fieldfare and redwing take guelder rose berries during winter irruptions, but established shrubs carry enough for both birds and foragers.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Jelly: strip berries from clusters and discard stalks. Rinse thoroughly. Simmer in just enough water to cover for 20–25 minutes until completely soft. Strain through muslin — do not squeeze. Measure the juice; add 400g sugar per 500ml. Bring to a boil, skim, and test for set after 10 minutes. Bottle while hot. The jelly is vivid red and sharp. Serve alongside venison, roast lamb, or strong hard cheese. Juice: strain the cooked pulp and sweeten lightly; use as a sharp cordial base, diluted with water or sparkling water. The flavour is excellent. Combine with apple juice for a milder drink. Do not eat the raw berries. Do not give raw berries to children.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking guelder rose berries from public land, hedgerows, and woodland for personal use is permitted. The plant is not protected. Cook the berries before consuming them in any quantity.

---

**SEO Title:** Guelder Rose — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick guelder rose berries from hedgerows in September. The raw fruit is toxic — cook before eating. Makes an excellent sharp cranberry-like jelly for game and lamb.

---
---

## 143. Dog Rose

**Species Name:** Dog Rose
**Latin Name:** Rosa canina
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** dog-rose
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul

---

**Short Description:**
Dog rose (*Rosa canina*) flowers from June in hedgerows throughout Britain. The pale pink petals are edible and fragrant, with a mild floral flavour that works in syrups, vinegars, and desserts. Pick petals in the morning when fully open. The hips that follow in autumn are a separate entry — both are worth knowing.

---

**Full Description:**
The dog rose is Britain's most common wild rose and one of the most familiar hedgerow plants in the country. Its pale pink flowers appear from June, typically lasting through to July, filling hedgerows and scrubby edges with a scent that is one of the characteristic smells of the British summer hedgerow.

The flowers are the edible part in summer. The petals have a delicate, slightly sweet floral flavour — genuinely rose-scented — that is more useful as an ingredient than eaten on their own. A handful of petals steeped in warm syrup or vinegar transfers the scent effectively. The flavour is subtle and works best in preparations where it is the only floral note.

The white base of each petal has a slightly bitter flavour; for syrup and delicate preparations, it is worth snipping it off before using. For vinegars and any strongly flavoured application, the difference is negligible.

Pick petals only when they are fully open and undamaged. Freshly opened flowers in the morning have the best scent. Avoid flowers that have already started to drop petals or show browning at the edges. The stamens and other flower parts can be removed or left depending on the use.

The hips that follow from September are covered separately.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Dog rose is a scrambling, arching shrub reaching 1–3m, with strongly recurved thorns on arching stems. Leaves are pinnate with five to seven leaflets, each 1.5–4cm, oval with a toothed margin. Flowers are 4–6cm across, five-petalled, pale pink to near-white, occasionally deeper pink, with a large central cluster of yellow stamens. The petals are notched at the tip. Flowers appear singly or in small clusters. The recurved thorns, pinnate leaves, and large five-petalled pale pink flowers on a scrambling hedgerow shrub are collectively specific to dog rose and its close relatives. Field rose (*Rosa arvensis*) has white flowers; sweet briar (*Rosa rubiginosa*) has a more intense apple scent from the leaves and smaller, brighter pink flowers. All three have edible petals and hips.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Dog rose has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British plant produces a similar five-petalled pink flower on a scrambling thorny shrub with pinnate leaves. Bramble (*Rubus fruticosus*) flowers are five-petalled and white or pale pink but the leaves are trifoliate or five-lobed palmate, not pinnate, and bramble stems have much stronger, flatter thorns. Bramble flowers are also edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Hedgerows, scrub, woodland edges, chalk downland, road banks, and coastal cliffs throughout Britain. One of the most common native shrubs in the country. Found on almost any well-drained soil with sufficient light. Particularly abundant in old laid hedges and on south-facing chalk banks. Scrambles through and over other shrubs, sometimes reaching several metres into a hawthorn hedge.

---

**In Season:**
Flowers from early June to mid-July in the south, slightly later in the north. The flowering period at any given location is typically three to four weeks. Individual flowers last only two to three days; the hedge as a whole flowers over a longer period as flowers open in succession. Pick petals on dry mornings when the scent is strongest. By late July, petals have largely fallen and the hips are beginning to develop.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Rose petal syrup: pack petals loosely into a measuring jug. For every 100ml of petals, make a syrup with 200ml water and 150g sugar. Pour the warm syrup over the petals and steep for 24 hours. Strain and bottle. Use over ice cream, pannacotta, or stirred into sparkling water. The flavour is delicate; use within a few weeks. Rose petal vinegar: pack petals into a jar of white wine vinegar and steep for two weeks in a cool dark place. Strain. Use in salad dressings where a floral note is wanted. Rose petal butter: mix a small number of petals (bases removed) with softened unsalted butter and a little icing sugar; refrigerate for a day to let the scent develop. Serve with plain scones or shortbread. Candied petals: paint individual petals with beaten egg white, dust with caster sugar, and dry on a wire rack at 50°C for an hour. Use to decorate cakes and desserts.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking petals from public land, roadsides, and commons for personal use is permitted under the Countryside and Rights of Way Act 2000. Do not uproot the plant. Dog rose is a significant wildlife plant — the flowers provide pollen for bees, the hips feed birds through winter. Pick from large established plants; avoid stripping any single plant of all its flowers.

---

**SEO Title:** Dog Rose — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick dog rose petals from hedgerows in June and July. Delicate floral flavour for syrups, vinegars, and desserts. The hips in autumn are a separate find.

---
---

## 144. Giant Puffball

**Species Name:** Giant Puffball
**Latin Name:** Calvatia gigantea
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** giant-puffball
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct

---

**Short Description:**
The giant puffball (*Calvatia gigantea*) is one of the most unmistakeable fungi in Britain: a smooth white sphere, sometimes the size of a football, sitting in grassland or woodland clearings from August. The flesh must be pure white all the way through when cut — this is the only safe test, and it must be done every time. Any hint of internal structure means do not eat it.

---

**Full Description:**
Giant puffball is a fungus that produces an immediate reaction from anyone who encounters it for the first time: a white sphere the size of a volleyball, sitting in a field as if placed there deliberately. It is not rare, but it is rarely seen because most people don't recognise it as food. Those who do find it are rewarded: the flesh is mild, creamy, and satisfying, with a texture close to firm tofu that takes on flavour from whatever it is cooked with.

The critical rule is the cut test, and it cannot be skipped or substituted. Cut the puffball in half from top to base before cooking or eating any part. The flesh must be uniformly, completely, and unambiguously white throughout — like the inside of a fresh bread roll. No zones, no discolouration, no hint of any developing structure inside. If any of these are present, do not eat it.

This rule exists because certain *Amanita* species — including death cap (*Amanita phalloides*) and destroying angel (*Amanita virosa*), both lethal — emerge from the ground as white egg-like bodies that can be superficially confused with a small puffball. Cut one of these in half and you will see a developing mushroom inside: cap, gills, stem. The developing structure is obvious. Cut a genuine giant puffball and there is nothing but white flesh. The test is reliable and takes five seconds.

Cut and do the test. Every time. No exceptions.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Giant puffball is round to flattened-round, 10–80cm across, occasionally larger. The outer surface is smooth, white to cream, with a very slight roughness that becomes more pronounced as the fungus ages. At the base there is a short attachment cord connecting it to the mycelium underground. The flesh of an edible specimen is uniformly white and firm, like cutting through a block of soft white cheese. As the puffball matures, the flesh turns yellow, then olive-green, then becomes a mass of brown spore dust — at any stage past white, the flesh is not edible. The sheer size, smooth white surface, and grassland habitat distinguish it from all other British fungi. Smaller puffballs (*Lycoperdon* spp.) are similar in form but much smaller (2–6cm) and also edible when flesh is fully white.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Cut every puffball in half before eating or cooking any part. The cut test is not optional.**

The only meaningful danger is confusing a young *Amanita* egg with a small puffball. Death cap (*Amanita phalloides*) and destroying angel (*Amanita virosa*) both emerge from the soil in a white egg-like stage that is several centimetres across. Cut the egg in half: you will see a fully developed mushroom in miniature, complete with developing cap, gills, and stem. This is entirely different from the solid white flesh of a true puffball. Giant puffball typically exceeds 10cm and is usually much larger; Amanita eggs are smaller. But size alone is not the test — cut and confirm.

Earthballs (*Scleroderma* spp.) are smaller, warty, and have dark purple-grey flesh inside from a very early stage — nothing like the pure white interior of a giant puffball.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Old permanent grassland, meadows, roadside verges, parkland, woodland clearings, and the margins of fields. Often appears in the same location year after year. Found throughout Britain, though not common. Tends to appear where old, undisturbed grassland exists alongside deciduous trees. Sometimes appears in gardens. The mycelium is perennial and the fruiting body appears annually in productive seasons; a good site is worth returning to.

---

**In Season:**
August through October. Most common in September in warm, damp conditions following summer heat. Individual fruiting bodies develop quickly — a puffball can appear and reach full size within a week. As the puffball ages, the flesh moves from white to yellow to olive to brown; the window for eating is when it is fully white inside. Check sites regularly once found. In a good year a single site can produce several large specimens; in a poor year nothing appears.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Slice thickly (at least 1.5cm) after cutting in half and confirming white flesh throughout. Remove any patches of skin if it has become leathery. Pan-fry in butter or olive oil over high heat: the flesh needs a hot pan or it stews rather than colours. Season generously. The mild flavour develops well with garlic, thyme, or sage. Large slices can be used as a base for other ingredients — top with sautéed mushrooms, wilted greens, or a fried egg. Breadcrumbed and fried: coat in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs and fry until golden. The texture and flavour of a large, well-cooked crumbed slice is genuinely excellent. Soup: chop into chunks and add to a mushroom soup base; the flesh holds its shape better than most soft fungi.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking giant puffballs from public land, meadows, and woodland for personal use is permitted. The fungus is not protected. Large-scale commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Giant puffball appears on land managed for nature conservation; personal picking is generally unproblematic but confirm for SSSIs and nature reserves.

---

**SEO Title:** Giant Puffball — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find giant puffballs in grassland from August. Cut in half first — flesh must be pure white throughout. Pan-fry in thick slices with butter. Excellent eating.

---
---

## 145. Hedgehog Mushroom

**Species Name:** Hedgehog Mushroom
**Latin Name:** Hydnum repandum
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** hedgehog-mushroom
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
The hedgehog mushroom (*Hydnum repandum*) is one of the safest fungi to pick for a beginner. Instead of gills or pores, the underside of the cap is covered in fine, pale, downward-pointing spines — these spines are the defining feature and no toxic British fungus shares them. The flavour is mild, slightly nutty, and peppery when raw. Cook them with butter and they are excellent.

---

**Full Description:**
Hedgehog mushroom is named for the spines on its underside, which look exactly as described: a dense covering of fine cream to pale buff spines, 2–6mm long, hanging downward. No dangerous British fungus has this feature. For a beginner who wants a reliable, safe, rewarding find, hedgehog mushroom is one of the first species to learn.

The flavour is mild and good: slightly nutty, with a faint pepperiness that disappears on cooking. The texture is firm and holds up well in the pan, better than most autumn fungi. Larger, older specimens can develop a bitterness; pick medium-sized caps in good condition for the best eating.

Hedgehog mushroom grows in deciduous and coniferous woodland and is associated with oak, beech, and pine. It often grows in groups, scattered across the woodland floor, and is sometimes found in large numbers on productive sites. Look for it on the same walks as chanterelle, cep, and winter chanterelle.

The spines are the identifier and the safety feature simultaneously. If there are spines on the underside — pale, fine, downward-pointing, attached to the cap — and the cap is pale cream to buff and the overall mushroom looks like the description below, eat it with confidence. No dangerous mushroom in Britain shares this structure.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 3–15cm across, irregularly rounded, often wavy or lobed at the margin, pale cream to ochre-buff, dry and smooth to slightly downy on the upper surface. The cap often curves downward at the margin. Underside: densely covered in fine spines (teeth) 2–6mm long, cream to pale buff, pointing straight downward. The spines run slightly onto the stem. Stem is 2–8cm tall, 1–3cm wide, solid, pale cream to buff, often off-centre or irregular. Flesh is white to pale cream, firm, and does not change colour when cut. Smell is mild, pleasantly mushroom-like. Spore print is white. The combination of pale cap, downward-pointing pale spines on the underside, and woodland habitat is definitive.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Hedgehog mushroom has no dangerous lookalikes in British woodland. Terracotta hedgehog (*Hydnum rufescens*) is a smaller, more orange-tinted species in the same genus, also edible. The two species grow in the same habitats and are sometimes found together; both are good to eat and the distinction is irrelevant for foraging purposes.

Shingled hedgehog (*Sarcodon imbricatus*) is a larger, darker species with a brown scaly cap and coarser spines; it is edible though more bitter and less common. It does not closely resemble *Hydnum repandum* in size or colour.

No toxic British fungus has fine, pale, downward-pointing spines on the cap underside.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Deciduous and mixed woodland, particularly under beech, oak, and occasionally pine. Found throughout Britain, most commonly in southern and central England and in the Scottish Highlands under ancient Scots pine. Tends to appear from August through to November, overlapping with chanterelle and cep seasons. Often found on the same site as other prized autumn fungi. Grows in scattered groups or loose rings in leaf litter; the pale cap blends into the woodland floor and rewards slow, careful searching.

---

**In Season:**
August through November. Most productive in September and October. The mushroom continues fruiting later into autumn than chanterelle, often appearing after the first frosts. Older specimens develop bitter flesh and darker, more brittle spines; pick fresh, medium-sized caps. In a good year, productive sites can yield large quantities. The fungus is much less picked than chanterelle or cep and good sites are less likely to have been stripped by other foragers.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Pan-fry in butter: the best preparation. Slice or leave whole if small. Cook over medium-high heat in plenty of butter, season, and add a little thyme or garlic in the final minute. The spines soften and the texture becomes tender. Serve on toast or alongside eggs. The spines hold the butter well and the eating is good. Mixed mushroom dishes: hedgehog mushroom combines well with cep, chanterelle, and other autumn fungi in risotto, pasta, or a mixed sauté. The firm texture holds up while softer fungi might break down. Dried: slice thinly and dry at 80°C for three to four hours. Rehydrate in warm water; use the soaking water in the dish.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking hedgehog mushrooms from public woodland and land for personal use is permitted. Large-scale commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Picking responsibly means not clearing a site entirely — leave small and undeveloped specimens and pick from several areas of a site rather than concentrating on one patch.

---

**SEO Title:** Hedgehog Mushroom — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find hedgehog mushrooms in woodland from August. The spined underside makes them one of the safest fungi to identify. Mild, nutty flavour — excellent fried in butter.

---
---

## 146. Oyster Mushroom

**Species Name:** Oyster Mushroom
**Latin Name:** Pleurotus ostreatus
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** oyster-mushroom
**Seasons:** Oct, Nov, Dec, Jan, Feb, Mar

---

**Short Description:**
Oyster mushroom (*Pleurotus ostreatus*) grows on dead and dying deciduous trees through autumn and winter. The grey to blue-grey shelving caps, white gills, and off-centre or absent stem on dead hardwood are the key identifiers. Distinguish it from angel wings (*Pleurocybella porrigens*), which is deadly and grows only on conifer wood. Habitat is the first and most reliable test.

---

**Full Description:**
Oyster mushroom is one of the most useful winter fungi in Britain. It fruits from October through to March — after most other edible species have finished — on dead and dying branches of deciduous trees, particularly beech, oak, and elm. A large shelf of oyster mushrooms on a fallen beech in November is a significant find.

The flavour is mild, slightly savoury, and better than any shop-bought oyster mushroom, which is typically grown on sawdust and lacks the depth of wild-grown specimens. The texture is silky when young, becoming tougher in older caps. Pick young, firm caps; old, pale, dried-out examples are woody and not worth eating.

Oyster mushroom is a saprotrophic fungus, breaking down dead wood. It grows in overlapping brackets or shelves, sometimes in large clusters, with the caps typically oriented upward and slightly cupped. In cold weather it can survive frost and continue fruiting — making it genuinely valuable as a late-season find.

The most important identification step is the tree. Oyster mushroom grows on deciduous wood. Angel wings, which is deadly, grows specifically on conifer wood — primarily pine. Confirm the host tree before picking anything from a pale, shelving fungus.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 5–20cm across, fan-shaped to oyster-shaped, smooth, grey to blue-grey (darkening in cold weather, paling in dry conditions), with a wavy, often inrolled margin. The surface is moist in wet weather. Gills are white to cream, running down the very short stem or the point of attachment; the gills are crowded and decurrent. Stem is very short (1–3cm) or absent, often off-centre, white. The fungus grows laterally from dead wood in overlapping brackets. Flesh is white, firm, and does not change colour when cut. Smell is mild and pleasant. Spore print is white to pale lilac. Growing in overlapping brackets on deciduous wood, with grey cap and white gills running down the stem, is the combination to look for.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Angel wings (*Pleurocybella porrigens*) is deadly and the critical lookalike for this species. It grows on conifer wood — almost always pine. It is much smaller and thinner than oyster mushroom, pure white throughout, and the cap is only 2–8cm across with a thin, almost translucent flesh. In 2004 a cluster of deaths in Japan was linked to angel wings, primarily in people with kidney disease. The distinctions from oyster mushroom are:

Oyster mushroom grows on deciduous wood and has grey to blue-grey caps with substantial flesh. Angel wings grows on conifer wood, is pure white throughout, and is thin and almost papery at the cap edge. Habitat is the definitive test: never pick a pale, shelving fungus from a conifer tree.

Crepidotus species (oysterlings) are small, white to cream, shell-shaped fungi growing on dead wood. They are much smaller — usually under 3cm — and have brown spores; the spore print is brown, not white. Crepidotus species are inedible but not deadly.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Dead and dying deciduous trees: beech, oak, elm, willow, ash, and occasionally other hardwoods. Grows on standing dead trees, fallen trunks, and cut stumps. Found throughout Britain wherever mature deciduous woodland exists. Most common in the south and midlands of England but widespread. Productive sites in beech woodland, particularly after a wet autumn, can yield large quantities. The fungus often reappears on the same log or stump year after year.

---

**In Season:**
October through to March, with peaks in October to November and again in January to February during mild spells. One of the few edible fungi that fruits through winter. In cold, dry weather the fruiting bodies freeze and may recover on thawing — frozen specimens are still edible if they have not deteriorated. Pick young, firm caps with the grey colour intact; avoid pale, dried-out, or heavily waterlogged specimens.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Pan-fry: slice large caps or leave smaller ones whole. Cook in butter or olive oil over medium-high heat; oyster mushrooms release significant moisture and need a hot pan to colour rather than stew. Season well and add garlic or thyme. The silky texture when young is excellent. Serve on toast, with eggs, alongside game, or in pasta. Soup: chop and add to a chicken or mushroom stock base; the flavour develops well in liquid. Stir-fried: cook quickly in very high heat with garlic, soy, and a little sesame oil. The texture holds up well. Tempura: coat in a light batter and fry at 180°C; serve immediately. Dried: dry at 80°C for several hours; rehydrate in warm water before using.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking oyster mushrooms from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Oyster mushroom is produced commercially at large scale; personal picking from the wild is not in competition with any commercial interest.

---

**SEO Title:** Oyster Mushroom — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find oyster mushrooms on dead deciduous trees from October through winter. Grey shelving caps on hardwood. Distinguish from deadly angel wings on conifers.

---
---

## 147. Chicken of the Woods

**Species Name:** Chicken of the Woods
**Latin Name:** Laetiporus sulphureus
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** chicken-of-the-woods
**Seasons:** May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct

---

**Short Description:**
Chicken of the woods (*Laetiporus sulphureus*) is a large, bright orange and yellow bracket fungus growing on living and dead deciduous trees. Young growth is tender and, when cooked thoroughly, has a texture genuinely close to chicken breast. The host tree matters: avoid specimens growing on yew, robinia (false acacia), and a handful of other trees, which are linked to reactions in some people. Always cook thoroughly — eating it raw or undercooked causes problems.

---

**Full Description:**
Chicken of the woods is among the most visually striking fungi in Britain. A mature specimen on an old oak or cherry tree can consist of dozens of overlapping, wavy brackets, each vivid orange on top and bright sulphur-yellow below, covering a square metre or more of trunk. It is visible from fifty metres.

The flavour and texture of the young, tender outer edges justify the reputation. Cooked thoroughly, the young growth has a fibrous, slightly chewy texture that genuinely resembles chicken breast in a way that no other fungus does. The flavour is mild and meaty. Only the tender outer margin of young brackets is worth eating; the inner flesh of larger, older brackets becomes woody and acrid.

Several caveats. Always cook thoroughly: chicken of the woods eaten raw or undercooked causes vomiting and stomach cramps, sometimes seriously. Even well-cooked specimens cause reactions in some people, particularly when grown on certain host trees. Avoid specimens growing on yew (*Taxus baccata*) — yew timber contains toxic compounds that may be taken up by the fungus. Specimens on robinia (false acacia, *Robinia pseudoacacia*) and sweet chestnut have also been linked to reactions in some people. Oak, cherry, and beech are the safest hosts.

On first eating: try a small amount before eating a full portion. Sensitivities vary.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Chicken of the woods grows in overlapping brackets of 5–40cm each, typically in large clusters. The upper surface is vivid orange to orange-yellow, smooth, and slightly wavy. The underside is bright sulphur-yellow with very fine pores — no gills. The flesh is pale yellow-white, firm, and fleshy when young; it becomes white and chalky with age. The stalk is absent or very short. Young margins are the softest and most tender. The intensely orange-yellow colouration on a tree trunk is unmistakeable; no toxic British fungus shares this combination. As the brackets age, the colour fades to buff and cream and the flesh becomes tough and inedible.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Chicken of the woods has no dangerous lookalikes among British fungi. The vibrant orange-yellow colour and the pored (not gilled) underside on a tree trunk are collectively specific to this species. Giant polypore (*Meripilus giganteus*) is a large, overlapping bracket fungus but is brown and cream in colour, not orange. It grows at the base of trees and stump roots, often beech. It is edible when young though less good than chicken of the woods.

The risks with chicken of the woods are not from misidentification but from the tree species it grows on and from undercooking. Check the host tree. Cook thoroughly. Eat a small amount on first trying.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Living and dead deciduous trees, particularly oak, cherry, sweet chestnut, beech, and willow. Also occasionally on conifers. Found throughout Britain, most common in southern and central England. Often appears on the same tree year after year. Ancient parkland oaks and old cherry trees in deciduous woodland are productive sites. The fungus is a parasite on living trees and a saprotroph on dead ones.

---

**In Season:**
May through to October, with most finds from July to September. Spring specimens on living trees tend to be particularly tender. Autumn specimens are typically larger. The brackets grow quickly and become tough within a week or two of appearing; visit productive sites regularly. Old, pale, dry, and crumbling brackets are past their use.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Trim carefully: cut away the tough inner flesh and woody attachment zone. Use only the tender outer margin — typically the outermost 3–5cm of young brackets. Cook thoroughly: fry in oil or butter over medium heat for at least 10 minutes until the flesh is fully cooked through. No pink or translucent flesh should remain. Stir into a sauce: the texture holds up in pasta or a creamy sauce. Use in any recipe calling for chicken breast: the substitution works genuinely well. Not suitable for gentle warming or quick cooking — it must be fully cooked.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking chicken of the woods from public woodland and trees for personal use is permitted. On ancient or veteran trees in parkland, picking the fungus is sometimes restricted to protect the host tree; check with the land manager. Commercial picking without permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Chicken of the Woods — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find chicken of the woods on oak and cherry from summer into autumn. Cook young growth thoroughly — the texture genuinely resembles chicken. Avoid yew and robinia hosts.

---
---

## 148. Beefsteak Fungus

**Species Name:** Beefsteak Fungus
**Latin Name:** Fistulina hepatica
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** beefsteak-fungus
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct

---

**Short Description:**
Beefsteak fungus (*Fistulina hepatica*) grows on oak and sweet chestnut in late summer and autumn. It is one of the most striking fungi in the British woodland: a thick, tongue-like bracket that oozes a reddish juice when cut and looks convincingly like a piece of raw liver. Young specimens are edible raw or cooked. The flavour is meaty with a sour edge. It has no dangerous lookalikes.

---

**Full Description:**
Beefsteak fungus deserves its name. Cut into a young specimen and you find marbled red-brown flesh, streaked with paler veining, that looks strikingly like raw beef. It oozes a dark, blood-like juice. The resemblance is close enough to be genuinely arresting.

The fungus grows on living and dead oak and sweet chestnut, emerging from wounds or the lower trunk as a tongue-shaped or fan-shaped bracket. Young specimens are the best eating: the flesh is firm, moist, and slightly acidic, with a meaty character and a sourness that is the fungus's main flavour characteristic. Older brackets are tougher and much more acidic, becoming unpleasant.

The acidity is the thing to manage in the kitchen. Soaking young slices in milk or water for 30 minutes before cooking reduces the sharpness. Alternatively, the sourness can be used as an asset: thin raw slices dressed with oil and seasoning eat like a carpaccio of sorts, the acidity functioning as lemon would.

The fungus causes a brown rot in oak that actually increases the quality of the timber for some uses — brown-rotted oak darkens and gains the character known to furniture makers as "brown oak". The fungus is not harming trees that wouldn't otherwise be affected by decay; it colonises already-compromised wood.

---

**Identification Notes:**
The bracket is 10–30cm across and 2–6cm thick, tongue or fan-shaped, deep red-orange to liver-red on the upper surface, pale cream to pink below. The upper surface is moist and slightly sticky. The underside has a cream to pale yellow pore surface (not gills) that bruises red when touched — individual tubes are visible and separable from each other, which is unique among bracket fungi. The flesh is deep red-brown, marbled, and exudes a dark reddish juice when cut. The stalk is short and thick, attached to the side. Grows from oak or sweet chestnut. The liver-like colour, juice, marbled flesh, and separable pore tubes together are entirely specific.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Beefsteak fungus has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British bracket fungus produces separable pore tubes, oozes reddish juice, or has marbled red-brown flesh. Chicken of the woods is vivid orange-yellow; giant polypore is brown; dryad's saddle is pale cream with a scaly brown cap. None of these resembles beefsteak fungus.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
At the base or lower trunk of living or dead oak and sweet chestnut. Occasionally on other hardwoods. Found throughout England and Wales in deciduous woodland, ancient parkland, and wood pasture. Most common in the south of England where mature oak is abundant. Ancient oaks in parkland are often highly productive sites. The fungus appears from the same tree year after year.

---

**In Season:**
August through October. Most common in September. Young specimens appear quickly and degrade over two to three weeks: pick when the upper surface is still vivid red and the flesh is firm and juicy. Older specimens become dry, leathery, and very sour. A large oak in a good site can produce multiple brackets in a season.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw: slice young specimens thinly across the grain of the flesh. Dress with good olive oil, salt, and a little black pepper. The sourness is present but works with the meaty character. Serve as an unusual first course. Reduce sourness: soak slices in milk for 30 minutes, rinse and cook. Pan-fry: cook in butter over high heat for four to five minutes per side. Season well. The texture holds up but the sourness concentrates slightly on cooking — balance with a little sweet onion. In a salad: slice thinly and add raw to a salad of bitter leaves, blue cheese, and walnuts. The tartness of the fungus works with the cheese.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking beefsteak fungus from public woodland for personal use is permitted. The fungus grows on trees in both public and private woodland; picking from private land requires the landowner's permission.

---

**SEO Title:** Beefsteak Fungus — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find beefsteak fungus on oak from August. Deep red marbled flesh that oozes juice like raw meat. Sour, meaty flavour — good raw in thin slices or pan-fried.

---
---

## 149. Velvet Shank

**Species Name:** Velvet Shank
**Latin Name:** Flammulina velutipes
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** velvet-shank
**Seasons:** Nov, Dec, Jan, Feb, Mar

---

**Short Description:**
Velvet shank (*Flammulina velutipes*) is a winter fungus, fruiting from November to March on dead and dying deciduous wood. The orange-brown caps are vivid against bare winter trees. The defining feature is the stem: distinctly velvety in texture, dark brown to almost black at the base, pale and smooth at the top. Distinguish it carefully from funeral bell (*Galerina marginata*), which is deadly and grows in the same habitat. The velvet texture on the lower stem is the key test.

---

**Full Description:**
Velvet shank is one of very few edible fungi that fruits reliably through the British winter. Finding a cluster of its vivid orange-brown caps on a dead elm or willow in January is a welcome surprise. The flavour is mild and the texture pleasant — it does not match cep or chanterelle for depth, but in a season with almost nothing else fruiting, it earns its place.

The stem is the safety identifier. The lower portion — typically half to two-thirds of the stem length — is covered in a distinctive velvety dark brown coating. Hold the stem and feel it: the velvet texture is immediately apparent and unlike anything on funeral bell, which is the deadly species sharing this habitat and season.

The caps are gelatinous in wet weather, which is normal and not a sign of deterioration. The mushroom is harvested commercially in East Asia as enoki, grown in the dark to produce the elongated, pale, tightly-clustered variety available in shops. Wild velvet shank looks quite different from enoki: stockier, more orange, and strongly velvety-stemmed.

Always confirm the velvet on the lower stem. Always check for a ring: funeral bell has a ring on the upper stem; velvet shank has no ring. Both tests take seconds.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 2–10cm, rounded when young becoming flatter and wavy, vivid orange-brown at the centre, paling to orange-yellow at the margin. The cap surface is slightly sticky or gelatinous in wet conditions. Gills are white to pale cream, broad, and widely spaced, running slightly down the stem. Stem is 3–8cm, 0.4–1cm wide, with a characteristic two-tone appearance: pale and smooth at the top, darkening to dark brown or near-black and distinctly velvety at the lower half to two-thirds. The velvet texture is the definitive field mark — run a finger down the stem. No ring present. Grows in clusters from dead wood, typically elm, willow, ash, or beech. Spore print is white.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Funeral bell (*Galerina marginata*) is deadly. It grows on the same dead deciduous wood through the same autumn and winter months. It contains the same amatoxins as death cap and eating it in any quantity causes liver and kidney failure. Several deaths in Britain and Europe have been attributed to this species.

The differences from velvet shank are clear and testable:

Velvet shank has a distinctly velvety, dark brown lower stem — this is immediately felt. Funeral bell has a smooth stem with no velvet texture. Velvet shank has no ring on the stem. Funeral bell has a prominent ring (skirt) on the upper stem, often visible as a ring or a ring-stain below the gills. Velvet shank caps are more vivid orange-yellow. Funeral bell caps are a more uniform honey-tawny brown. Velvet shank spore print is white. Funeral bell spore print is rusty orange-brown.

Check all of these. Check the velvet. Check for a ring. If there is any doubt at all, do not pick.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Dead and dying deciduous trees: elm, willow, ash, beech, and other hardwoods. Often grows on standing dead trees and on fallen logs, particularly around the base and on exposed roots. Found throughout Britain wherever dead deciduous wood is present. Most common in lowland deciduous woodland, hedgerows, and riverbanks where old willows and elms die back. Fruit bodies appear repeatedly on the same dead wood over multiple winters.

---

**In Season:**
November through to March. One of the most reliable winter fungi in Britain. Survives and recovers from hard frosts — frozen specimens thaw intact and remain edible. After prolonged freezing and thawing the caps become gelatinous but the flavour is unaffected. The winter fruiting window makes this a genuinely useful species when little else is available.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Remove the lower, tougher portions of the stems if they are very dark and fibrous. The caps and upper stems are the best eating. Sauté in butter over medium-high heat; the caps release moisture and need time to colour. Season with salt, pepper, and thyme. Good on toast, alongside winter game, or as a component of a mixed mushroom dish. Soup: add to a clear vegetable or chicken broth; the mild flavour works in a light broth. Not suited to very long cooking or heavy sauces, which overpower the delicate character.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking velvet shank from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Do not pick from any site where a ring-bearing species could be present without conducting the full identification checks above.

---

**SEO Title:** Velvet Shank — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find velvet shank on dead wood from November through winter. Velvety dark stem, no ring — check both before eating. Distinguish carefully from deadly funeral bell.

---
---

## 150. Cep

**Species Name:** Cep
**Latin Name:** Boletus edulis
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** cep
**Seasons:** Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Cep (*Boletus edulis*) is the finest edible fungus in Britain. The brown bun-shaped cap, white-to-cream pores on the underside, and distinctive pale net-like pattern on the stem identify it reliably. Two species require attention: bitter bolete, which ruins a dish but is not dangerous, and Satan's bolete, which is toxic. Neither is difficult to separate from cep once you know what to look for.

---

**Full Description:**
Cep, porcini, penny bun — the mushroom has names in every European language because every European culture that has woodland has prized it. The flavour is deep, complex, and unmistakeable: intensely savoury, nutty, with an earthy depth that concentrates further on drying. A handful of dried cep added to a risotto produces something quite different from any other mushroom.

The cap is the familiar brown bun — the colour of a well-baked bread roll, smooth, often slightly tacky in damp conditions. The underside has pores rather than gills: a spongy layer of tiny tubes that is white when the mushroom is young, becoming cream and then yellowish-green with age. The flesh is white throughout and does not change colour or stain blue when cut — this is one of the tests that separates it from dangerous boletes.

The stem is as important as the cap: pale brown to off-white, bulbous at the base, and covered with a pale, fine net-like raised pattern (reticulation) that is visible on close inspection. This net pattern is specific to *Boletus edulis* and its close relatives, all of which are edible.

Related species — bay bolete (*Imleria badia*), cep's close relative with a darker cap and similar flavour — are equally edible and treated identically in the kitchen. Pine bolete (*Boletus pinophilus*) is similarly good. For the purposes of a day's picking, any solid-fleshed, pale-pored, net-stemmed bolete that doesn't stain blue is almost certainly edible.

The two tests that matter: check the pore colour (white-cream, not red or pink), and cut the stem — the flesh should remain white, not stain dramatically blue.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 5–25cm across, rounded when young becoming flatter and broader; colour is mid-brown to dark brown, smooth, slightly sticky in wet conditions. Underside: a spongy pore layer (tubes), white when young, becoming cream, yellow, then olive-green with age. The pore surface does not stain when touched. Stem is 5–20cm tall, 2–8cm wide, often bulbous at the base, pale brown to off-white, with a fine, raised, pale net pattern covering at least the upper half. Flesh is white to pale cream throughout, firm, and does not change colour when cut. Smell is mild and pleasantly earthy. Spore print is olive-brown. Grows in woodland, singly or scattered, on soil among leaf litter.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Bitter bolete (*Tylopilus felleus*) is the most common confusion. It is not dangerous but is intensely, immediately, and persistently bitter — one piece ruins a whole dish. The test is simple and reliable: touch the tiniest fragment of raw bitter bolete to your tongue. The bitterness is unmistakeable within seconds. Bitter bolete pores have a pinkish tinge that develops with age, unlike the white-cream of cep. The stem net pattern of bitter bolete is coarser and darker. Bitter bolete tends to grow under conifers; cep grows under both deciduous trees and conifers.

Satan's bolete (*Rubroboletus satanas*) is toxic and causes severe vomiting and gastrointestinal distress. Fortunately it is visually dramatic and quite different from cep: the pores are strikingly red — the underside of the cap is vivid red to blood-orange, not white. The stem is mottled red and yellow. The flesh stains strongly and quickly blue when cut. Satan's bolete is uncommon and found mainly in southern England on chalky soils under beech and oak. It does not closely resemble cep for anyone paying attention, but the red pores are the critical field check.

The rule: white or cream pores, no staining to blue, no bitterness on a taste of raw flesh. All three clear: you have a cep.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Deciduous and coniferous woodland, particularly under oak, beech, birch, and pine. Found throughout Britain, most common in southern and central England, the New Forest, the Scottish Highlands, and parts of Wales. Grows on well-drained woodland soils, often near paths where soil compaction is slight. Tends to appear in the same locations year after year. Often found alongside chanterelle and hedgehog mushroom in good mixed woodland. Absent from very wet or very exposed ground.

---

**In Season:**
July through November, with peaks in August to September and often a second flush in October. The season varies considerably with weather: a warm wet August after a dry summer produces excellent conditions. In a poor year, productive sites may yield very little. Individual mushrooms grow quickly — a cep can reach full size in two to three days — and deteriorate equally fast: maggot damage is common in older specimens. Cut the stem clean and check for maggot tunnels. A slightly maggoty stem does not necessarily mean the cap is compromised; trim generously and inspect the flesh.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw: slice young, firm caps thinly and dress with olive oil, salt, and lemon — the flavour raw is excellent and rarely experienced outside of Italy and France. Sautéed: cook in butter over high heat; cep releases moisture and needs a hot pan to colour well. Season with garlic, thyme, and parsley. Risotto: the classic use. Use dried cep (see below) for the base flavour and add fresh if available. Dried: slice thinly and dry at 80°C for three to four hours, or use a dehydrator. Dried cep concentrates the flavour dramatically and keeps for a year in a sealed jar. The soaking water from rehydrating dried cep is intensely flavoured and should always be added to the dish. Soup: blend sautéed cep with onion, garlic, and stock; finish with a little cream. One of the best mushroom soups available from wild ingredients.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking cep from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Some Forestry England and National Trust sites operate informal limits on commercial picking; personal picking is unaffected. Large-scale commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted and is a growing concern at productive sites in southern England.

---

**SEO Title:** Cep — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find ceps in woodland from August. Brown cap, cream pores, pale net on stem. Check for bitterness and red pores before eating. The best wild mushroom in Britain.

---
---

*End of Batch 15. Entries 141–150 complete. All entries Draft status — Expert Review required before any entry goes Live.*
