## 151. Field Mushroom

**Species Name:** Field Mushroom
**Latin Name:** Agaricus campestris
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** field-mushroom
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct

---

**Short Description:**
The field mushroom (*Agaricus campestris*) grows in old permanent pasture from late summer. It is the wild original of the shop button mushroom and tastes considerably better. Two tests are essential before eating any Agaricus mushroom: the staining test to rule out yellow stainer, and checking the base of the stem to rule out death cap. Both tests take thirty seconds. Do not skip them.

---

**Full Description:**
Field mushroom was once a reliable sight in permanent grassland across Britain. Numbers have declined substantially as old pasture has been ploughed, fertilised, or reseeded — the fungus depends on undisturbed, traditionally managed grassland that has not been heavily treated. Where it still grows, it can fruit in quantity: a productive meadow after a warm August night can yield dozens of mushrooms across a wide area.

The flavour is everything the cultivated button mushroom gestures at and fails to deliver. The texture is firmer, the flavour more pronounced and genuinely savoury, the cap slightly smaller and more elegantly formed. Eaten simply — pan-fried in butter with salt and eaten on good bread — they require nothing else.

*Agaricus campestris* belongs to a genus that contains both excellent edible species and some genuinely dangerous ones. The identification tests for this genus are not optional. Two must be done before eating any mushroom in this group:

**Test one — the staining test:** cut or scratch the cap flesh, particularly at the edge, and scratch the base of the stem. Field mushroom flesh bruises pink to red-brown. Yellow stainer (*Agaricus xanthodermus*) turns immediately and dramatically bright chrome-yellow. This reaction is fast, vivid, and unmistakeable. Do this test every time.

**Test two — check the base:** pull or dig up the whole mushroom and examine the base of the stem. Field mushroom has no volva (no white egg-like sheath at the base). Death cap (*Amanita phalloides*), which is lethal and can appear as a young button-stage mushroom in grassland near trees, emerges from a white cup-like volva. If any white cup or sac is present at the base: do not eat it.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 3–12cm, rounded when young becoming flat, white to grey-white or very pale brown, smooth to slightly fibrous. The margin is straight when young. Gills are pink when young, turning deep brown to almost black as spores mature — never white. Stem is 3–8cm, 1–2cm wide, white, with a simple ring that often collapses or disappears on older specimens. The base of the stem tapers to a point with no volva. Flesh is white, bruising pink to red-brown. Smell is pleasant, mushroom-like. Spore print is dark brown to purple-brown. Growing in open grassland, well away from trees, with pink gills turning brown and no volva at the base, is the field mushroom profile.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Yellow stainer (*Agaricus xanthodermus*)** is toxic and the most important confusion species. It causes severe gastroenteritis, sometimes requiring hospitalisation, in a significant proportion of people who eat it. The test: cut or scratch the cap edge or stem base. Yellow stainer turns immediately bright chrome-yellow. The colour is instant and vivid. Field mushroom bruises pink to red-brown, not yellow. Yellow stainer also has a strong, unpleasant chemical or inky smell, distinct from the pleasant smell of field mushroom. Always do the staining test.

**Death cap (*Amanita phalloides*)** in its button stage can appear as a white, dome-capped mushroom in grassland near trees. Death cap gills are always white and remain white — they never turn pink. Death cap emerges from a white volva at the base of the stem; dig up the whole mushroom and check. Death cap grows near trees, particularly oak and beech; field mushroom grows in open grassland away from trees. Never pick a white-gilled mushroom near trees without examining the base for a volva.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Old permanent pasture, hay meadows, downland, parkland, racecourses, and old lawns. Found throughout Britain but declining with the loss of old pasture. Most productive on chalk and limestone grassland in the south of England. Often appears in rings or arcs — the fruiting bodies trace the expanding underground mycelium. A site that produces mushrooms in one year is likely to produce them again in subsequent years if the grassland management has not changed.

---

**In Season:**
August through October, with the main flush in late August and September following warm nights and rain. Individual fruiting bodies appear and deteriorate quickly — check productive sites every two to three days during the season. Pick before the gills have darkened completely; very dark, flat, wet-looking caps are past their best, though still edible if the flesh is firm and the smell is good.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Pan-fry: the best and simplest use. Slice thickly and cook in butter over high heat; field mushroom contains more moisture than cultivated varieties and needs a hot pan to colour rather than stew. Season with salt and pepper. Eat on toast or as a side to fried eggs. On the grill: whole caps, gills up, with a knob of butter and a clove of garlic; grill for five to seven minutes. The gills turn almost black and the flavour concentrates. Soup: chop and add to a clear chicken or beef stock. The flavour is strong enough not to need cream. The dark gills colour the soup deeply — not a problem for flavour, less welcome in a pale cream soup.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking field mushrooms from public pasture, commons, and open access land for personal use is permitted. Mushrooms on private farmland are technically the landowner's property, though personal picking from accessible field margins is rarely contested. Commercial picking without permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Field Mushroom — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find field mushrooms in old permanent pasture from August. Do the staining test and check the stem base before eating. The wild original of the button mushroom — far better.

---
---

## 152. Parasol Mushroom

**Species Name:** Parasol Mushroom
**Latin Name:** Macrolepiota procera
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** parasol-mushroom
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct

---

**Short Description:**
The parasol mushroom (*Macrolepiota procera*) is one of the largest edible fungi in Britain, with a cap reaching 30cm or more. The distinctive snakeskin-patterned stem, moveable double ring, and brown scaly cap on a tall upright mushroom in open grassland or woodland edge make it identifiable with confidence. Cut the stem: the flesh must stay white. If it turns orange-red, you have shaggy parasol, which causes reactions in some people. Check for a volva at the base for the destroying angel. Both tests matter.

---

**Full Description:**
Parasol mushroom is a genuinely impressive find. A fully open cap the size of a dinner plate on a stem 25–35cm tall, standing in a woodland clearing or field margin in September, is unmistakeable once you know what you are looking at. The flavour is good: mild, slightly nutty, with a pleasant texture that holds up well to cooking. The cap, not the stem, is the edible part — the stem is too fibrous to eat with pleasure.

The species is rated Intermediate not because it is difficult to identify in its mature form, but because two field tests must be completed before eating. Skipping either introduces genuine risk.

The first test rules out shaggy parasol (*Chlorophyllum rhacodes*), which causes gastrointestinal reactions in some people. Cut the stem: parasol mushroom flesh stays white. Shaggy parasol flesh turns orange-red within seconds of cutting. The reaction is visible and reliable.

The second test rules out destroying angel (*Amanita virosa*), which is lethal. Dig up the base of the stem: parasol mushroom has a tapering base with no volva (no white egg or cup). Destroying angel emerges from a clearly defined white volva in the soil. Young, still-closed parasol caps can be confused with large, white *Amanita* buttons. Digging up the base and finding no volva is the definitive test.

Both tests together take one minute.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Mature parasol mushroom: cap 15–30cm across, broadly convex to flat, pale cream to buff with a prominent brown central boss and concentric pale brown scales radiating outward. The cap surface has a distinctive shaggy-scale pattern. Gills are white to cream, free from the stem, broad and crowded. Stem is 15–35cm tall, 1–2cm wide, slender and straight, pale cream with a distinctive brown snakeskin-like mottled or banded pattern — this pattern is specific to *Macrolepiota procera* and one of the strongest field identifiers. A double ring is present on the upper stem; it is moveable and can be slid up and down the stem. The base tapers with no volva. Flesh is white throughout, not changing colour when cut. Spore print is white. Young mushrooms: the cap is egg-shaped and closed; the brown boss is visible at the top. Do not pick very young closed specimens until more features are visible.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Shaggy parasol (*Chlorophyllum rhacodes*)** is the primary confusion species. It causes gastrointestinal symptoms in a proportion of people who eat it, though some people tolerate it without problems. The test: cut the stem cleanly. Shaggy parasol flesh turns orange-red within seconds. Parasol mushroom flesh stays white. Shaggy parasol cap scales are coarser and chunkier than parasol mushroom; the stem does not have the characteristic snakeskin pattern of *Macrolepiota procera*, instead showing a smooth or streaked surface. Shaggy parasol grows in disturbed, composty ground — garden borders, compost heaps, roadsides — rather than open grassland.

**Destroying angel (*Amanita virosa*)** is deadly. It is pure white throughout. A young closed destroying angel could be confused with a very young parasol cap. The test: dig up the base of the stem. Destroying angel emerges from a white cup-like volva clearly visible in the soil. Parasol mushroom has no such structure. Destroying angel grows in woodland near trees; parasol mushroom grows in open grassland and woodland clearings. Never pick a large white mushroom in woodland without examining the base.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Open grassland, meadows, parkland, woodland clearings, and the grassy edges of deciduous woodland. Found throughout Britain, most common in southern and central England. Prefers well-drained, less fertile grassland; less common on heavy, fertilised, or recently improved land. Often grows at the edge of woodland where grassland meets shade. Appears singly or in small groups. Produces fruiting bodies in the same location year after year on productive sites.

---

**In Season:**
August through October, with most finds in September. Mature caps develop quickly and deteriorate over several days; pick before the cap has fully flattened and while the flesh is firm. Very large, flat, pale, and slightly translucent caps are old — the flesh becomes spongy and less pleasant. The young, half-open drum-shaped stage with the cap margin still inrolled is the best eating.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Use the cap only; the stem is too fibrous. Larger caps can be cut into slices across the diameter. Crumbed and fried: coat in flour, beaten egg, and fine breadcrumbs; fry in oil at 180°C for two to three minutes per side until golden. The texture is remarkably good — firm, substantial, with the flavour of the mushroom coming through the crust. This is the most rewarding way to cook a parasol. On the grill: whole cap, gills up, with butter and herbs; grill for five to eight minutes. The cap shrinks considerably. Pan-fry: slice and cook in butter over high heat; season well. Add garlic and thyme in the last minute.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking parasol mushrooms from public land, commons, and woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Do not pick every specimen from a site — leave some for spore dispersal and to allow the site to remain productive.

---

**SEO Title:** Parasol Mushroom — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find parasol mushrooms in grassland from August. Check flesh colour and stem base before eating. Excellent crumbed and fried. One of Britain's best wild mushrooms.

---
---

## 153. Shaggy Ink Cap

**Species Name:** Shaggy Ink Cap
**Latin Name:** Coprinus comatus
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** shaggy-ink-cap
**Seasons:** Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Shaggy ink cap (*Coprinus comatus*) appears in grass from September: a white, cylindrical, shaggy-scaled mushroom that dissolves from the base upward into black ink as it matures. Pick them when the cap is still firmly cylindrical and entirely white. They deteriorate within hours of picking. Do not eat within several hours of drinking alcohol: the closely related common ink cap contains a compound that causes a serious reaction with alcohol, and caution is warranted with shaggy ink cap too.

---

**Full Description:**
Shaggy ink cap is one of the most distinctive fungi in Britain and genuinely one of the easier species for a beginner to learn. The shape when young — a tall white cylinder covered in shaggy white and brown scales, standing in a lawn or roadside verge — is unlike almost anything else. The autodigestion process that follows, as the cap dissolves from the margin upward into black inky fluid, is one of the more extraordinary things in the natural world to watch in time-lapse.

The window for eating is narrow and non-negotiable. Only white, firm, cylindrical specimens with no sign of darkening at the base of the cap are worth picking. Once the lower edge of the cap begins to turn black — even slightly — the eating quality has dropped. Once black liquid appears, the mushroom is dissolving and is past use entirely.

The flavour is mild and pleasant, with a soft, slightly silky texture. It requires the simplest cooking. The main constraint is time: shaggy ink caps must be eaten on the day of picking, within a few hours where possible. They cannot be stored, transported seriously, or left for the next morning.

**The alcohol note:** common ink cap (*Coprinopsis atramentaria*), a related species, contains coprine, a compound that blocks aldehyde dehydrogenase in the same way as the drug disulfiram. Eating common ink cap within several hours of drinking alcohol — or drinking alcohol within several days of eating it — causes flushing, nausea, rapid heartbeat, and vomiting. Shaggy ink cap contains far less coprine and reactions are rare, but the cautious position is to avoid alcohol within a few hours of eating it.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 5–15cm tall and 2–5cm wide at the cylindrical stage, pure white with shaggy, recurved scales of white and pale brown. The cap is strictly cylindrical before autodigestion begins. Gills are white when very young, rapidly turning pink and then black from the cap margin inward as spores mature. Stem is 10–20cm, white, hollow, with a fragile ring near the base. The base of the stem has no volva. Flesh is white and soft. Grows in grass — lawns, roadsides, parks, rough grassland, disturbed ground — often in clusters. The shaggy white cylinder with dissolving black margin, in grassland, is unmistakeable.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Common ink cap (*Coprinopsis atramentaria*)** grows in clusters from buried wood or compacted ground and is the species linked to the alcohol reaction. It has a smooth to slightly scaly grey-brown cap, smaller and without the distinctive shaggy white scales of shaggy ink cap. Common ink cap caps are more bell-shaped from the start; shaggy ink cap caps are strictly cylindrical. The two look different in the field, but if in any doubt, avoid alcohol entirely for several days after eating any ink cap.

No dangerous species closely resembles a white, shaggy-cylindrical ink cap in open grassland.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Grassland, lawns, roadsides, rough verges, disturbed ground, compost heaps, and occasionally at the base of old stumps. Often appears in loose clusters rather than singly. Found throughout Britain in both urban and rural settings. One of the most common edible fungi in urban green spaces — it appears in parks, on playing fields, and on roadside verges where the grass is not heavily treated. A productive lawn can yield repeated flushes through autumn.

---

**In Season:**
September through November, occasionally into December. Appears quickly after rain following warm weather. The fruiting bodies develop and autodigest within 24–48 hours in warm conditions, somewhat more slowly in cold. Check productive sites every day during the season. Pick first thing in the morning when the cap is still fully white and closed; by evening the same mushroom may already be liquefying.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Cook immediately. Do not store. Slice thickly and pan-fry in butter over medium heat for three to four minutes — shaggy ink caps are more delicate than most field fungi and do not need high heat. Season with salt and pepper. Serve on toast or alongside eggs. The flavour is subtle; strong flavours overpower it. A light omelette or scrambled eggs is the ideal vehicle. Do not attempt to dry, pickle, or preserve: autodigestion is unstoppable and begins immediately after picking.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking shaggy ink caps from public grassland, commons, and parks for personal use is permitted. The species is not protected. Commercial picking is not practical given its extreme perishability.

---

**SEO Title:** Shaggy Ink Cap — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find shaggy ink caps in grassland from September. Pick only pure white cylindrical caps and cook within hours. Avoid alcohol when eating. Mild, excellent flavour.

---
---

## 154. Horn of Plenty

**Species Name:** Horn of Plenty
**Latin Name:** Craterellus cornucopioides
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** horn-of-plenty
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Horn of plenty (*Craterellus cornucopioides*) grows in clusters in deciduous woodland from August. It is one of the most distinctive edible fungi in Britain: a hollow, trumpet-shaped fungus, dark grey to near-black, with a frilled, wavy outer surface. The flavour is exceptional — deep, smoky, complex, and extraordinary when dried. It has no dangerous lookalikes. Finding a colony is a significant foraging event.

---

**Full Description:**
Horn of plenty is easy to overlook and hard to forget once found. The fungus is dark grey to sooty black, and it grows among dead leaves on the woodland floor where its colour gives near-perfect camouflage. Scan a productive beech wood in October and the first colony reveals itself as a darkening of the leaf litter that resolves into dozens of frilled black trumpets. It is the kind of find that makes a whole walk worthwhile.

The flavour is among the best of any edible British fungus. Fresh, it has a deep, savoury, almost smoky character quite different from most pale-fleshed woodland mushrooms. Dried, the flavour concentrates into something extraordinary: intense, complex, with a depth reminiscent of black truffle without the eye-watering price. A few grams of dried horn of plenty transforms a sauce or a risotto in the same way that dried cep does, but with a different character.

The fruiting body is hollow all the way through, like a tube. Cut one in half from top to base and the inside is empty: this is both an identification feature and a practical point for cleaning — debris collects inside.

Horn of plenty is also known as black chanterelle and trompette de la mort (trumpet of death), the latter being a French theatrical name that refers to its dark colouring, not its edibility. It is entirely safe.

---

**Identification Notes:**
The fruiting body is 4–12cm tall, hollow, trumpet or funnel-shaped, with the cap opening upward and the stem tapering downward. The inner surface (facing upward) is grey to dark grey, becoming darker and more sooty with age. The outer surface (facing outward and downward) is darker still — near-black to charcoal — wrinkled and frilled rather than smooth. There are no gills or pores on the outer surface: it is smooth to slightly veined. Flesh is thin, dark grey, and flexible. Grows in clusters from soil among leaf litter, usually under beech or oak. The combination of hollow black trumpet shape, frilled outer surface, no gills, and deciduous woodland habitat is entirely specific and unmistakeable.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Horn of plenty has no dangerous lookalikes in British deciduous woodland. No toxic British fungus produces a hollow black trumpet growing from leaf litter. Pig's ear (*Gomphus clavatus*) is a vaguely similar funnel shape but is distinctly purple to lilac and has decurrent gill-like folds on the outer surface; it is uncommon and edible. Some small dark *Craterellus* species, including (*Craterellus melanoxeros*), are also edible and grow in similar habitats.

The main reason for an Intermediate rating is that the camouflage makes initial identification harder than for more visually obvious species, and the hollow trumpet shape requires direct comparison with a good source before a beginner is confident. Once seen in context, there is nothing comparable growing in the same habitat.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Deciduous woodland, particularly under beech and oak on well-drained soil with a deep leaf litter layer. Often grows in the same spots year after year. Found throughout Britain, though most productive sites are in the south of England and in Welsh oak woodland. Less common in Scotland. Colonies can be large — hundreds of fruiting bodies in a small area — or small and scattered. The dark colour makes them almost invisible; look for a slight darkening of the leaf litter in likely habitat and investigate.

---

**In Season:**
August through November. Most common in September and October. The fruiting bodies tolerate cold and light frost; colonies can persist into November on sheltered sites. Individual fruiting bodies deteriorate over one to two weeks, becoming soggy and eventually breaking down. Pick firm, dark, frilled specimens; avoid any that have turned slimy or pale.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Clean carefully: the hollow interior collects debris. Use a brush or rinse briefly and dry. Fresh: sauté in butter over medium-high heat for four to five minutes. The flesh is thin and cooks quickly; do not over-cook. Excellent with pasta, on toast, or folded into scrambled eggs. The flavour is assertive enough to stand as the main ingredient. Dried: this is the best use. Spread on a tray and dry at 80°C for two to three hours until completely crisp. Store in a sealed jar — they keep for a year or more. Crumble or powder dried specimens and add to sauces, soups, and risotto. Rehydrate in warm water and add the soaking liquid to the dish. The dried flavour is extraordinary. Combine with dried cep for a mushroom powder that elevates any braise or sauce.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking horn of plenty from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Given the tendency to grow in concentrated colonies, pick sustainably from each site and leave a proportion of the fruiting bodies undisturbed.

---

**SEO Title:** Horn of Plenty — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find horn of plenty in beech woodland from August. Dark, hollow trumpet fungus with exceptional flavour. Extraordinary dried. No dangerous lookalikes.

---
---

## 155. Wood Blewit

**Species Name:** Wood Blewit
**Latin Name:** Lepista nuda
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** wood-blewit
**Seasons:** Oct, Nov, Dec, Jan

---

**Short Description:**
Wood blewit (*Lepista nuda*, also classified as *Clitocybe nuda*) is a late-season fungus fruiting from October into January in deciduous woodland and hedgerows. The lilac to violet-blue colouring throughout — cap, gills, and stem — is immediately striking. Two species require careful separation: webcap (*Cortinarius* species), which causes irreversible kidney failure with a two-to-three-week delay in symptoms, and lilac fibrecap (*Inocybe geophylla* var. *lilacina*), which is toxic. Take a spore print before eating any purple-tinted mushroom in woodland. This is not optional.

---

**Full Description:**
Wood blewit is genuinely beautiful. The young cap is a deep lilac-violet, the gills a similar hue, the stem pale blue-lavender — a colour combination unlike almost any other British fungus. Older specimens fade to buff-grey-lilac, but young, fresh wood blewits in October are among the most visually arresting fungi in British woodland.

The flavour is good: mild, faintly floral and perfumed, with a soft meaty texture. It requires thorough cooking; some people report mild reactions from eating it undercooked or raw. Always cook wood blewit before eating.

The safety concern is significant and specific. *Cortinarius* mushrooms, known as webcaps, include species that cause Orellanine poisoning — a form of irreversible kidney failure. The insidious aspect of Orellanine is the delay: symptoms typically begin two to three weeks after eating, by which point the kidneys have already sustained serious damage and the cause may not be immediately obvious. Several people have died in Britain from *Cortinarius* poisoning in recent decades.

Young webcaps can be violet or lilac in colouring. The definitive test is a spore print: wood blewit gives a pinkish-lilac print. Webcaps give a rusty orange-brown print. Take a spore print from every specimen before eating any lilac-gilled mushroom in woodland.

The taxonomy note: wood blewit is listed in some references as *Lepista nuda* and in others as *Clitocybe nuda*, reflecting an ongoing reclassification. Both names refer to the same species.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 5–15cm, rounded when young becoming broadly convex to flat, often wavy at the margin. Colour is lilac-violet when young, fading to buff-brown-lilac with age. The cap surface is smooth and slightly hygrophanous (colour changes with moisture level). Gills are blue-lilac, crowded, sinuate (notched where they meet the stem). Stem is 4–8cm, 1–2cm wide, solid, fibrous, pale blue-lilac to buff, with no ring and no volva. Flesh is pale lilac-grey, firm, and does not change colour when cut. Smell is pleasantly perfumed and floral, not unpleasant. Spore print is pinkish-lilac — take a print before eating. Grows in leaf litter in deciduous woodland and at the base of hedgerows, often in groups or loose rings.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Webcap species (*Cortinarius* spp.)** are the critical lookalike and the reason this entry carries a high caution level. Several *Cortinarius* species have violet, lilac, or purple tinges to the cap, gills, or stem. The distinguishing features are:

Young *Cortinarius* mushrooms have a cobweb-like veil — the cortina — between the cap margin and the stem; this leaves rusty-orange spore deposits on the stem as the mushroom matures. Wood blewit has no cortina and no such deposits. The spore print of *Cortinarius* is rusty orange-brown; wood blewit prints pinkish-lilac. *Cortinarius* species often have an earthy or radish-like smell; wood blewit smells pleasantly floral and perfumed. Eating a *Cortinarius* species causes irreversible kidney failure with a two-to-three-week delay in symptoms. Take a spore print. If it is orange-brown: do not eat it.

**Lilac fibrecap (*Inocybe geophylla* var. *lilacina*)** is toxic and smaller than wood blewit — cap 1–3cm versus 5–15cm for wood blewit. Lilac fibrecap has a silky, fibrous cap with a conical to bell shape and a more pointed centre. Its gills start pale lilac and turn brown as spores mature; spore print is brown. Much smaller than wood blewit in all dimensions.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Deciduous woodland, particularly under beech, oak, and ash, in deep leaf litter. Also at the base of old hedgerows and in the grassy margins of woodland. Found throughout Britain, most common in the south and midlands of England. The late season — October into January — makes it a valuable species when most other edible fungi have finished. Often grows in loose groups or scattered across a small area.

---

**In Season:**
October through January, occasionally later. One of the last edible fungi to appear in the autumn season and one of the few that persists into winter. Survives light frosts and continues fruiting during mild spells in December and January. Young, fresh-coloured specimens with full lilac tones are the best eating; older, fully faded buff specimens are past their prime.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Always cook thoroughly before eating. Pan-fry in butter over medium heat for at least five minutes; do not eat raw or undercooked. The floral flavour is delicate and benefits from simple treatment: garlic, parsley, and butter is the best accompaniment. Avoid strong flavours that overpower the perfumed character. Risotto: add to a simple butter and parmesan risotto — the colour fades on cooking but the flavour integrates well. Dried: the flavour concentrates well on drying; dry at 80°C for three hours and store in a sealed jar.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking wood blewit from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Take a spore print before eating any specimen. Do not pick any lilac-gilled mushroom without completing the identification checks above.

---

**SEO Title:** Wood Blewit — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find wood blewits in woodland from October. Striking lilac colouring. Take a spore print before eating — webcaps cause irreversible kidney failure with delayed symptoms.

---
---

## 156. Winter Chanterelle

**Species Name:** Winter Chanterelle
**Latin Name:** Craterellus tubaeformis
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** winter-chanterelle
**Seasons:** Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec

---

**Short Description:**
Winter chanterelle (*Craterellus tubaeformis*) grows in mossy deciduous and coniferous woodland from September through December. The small, funnel-shaped cap and grey, forking ridge-like structures on the underside — not true gills — identify it reliably. One species requires careful attention: funeral bell (*Galerina marginata*), which is deadly and grows on wood in the same season. The tests are specific: check for a ring on the stem, check what the mushroom is growing from, and look at the underside of the cap.

---

**Full Description:**
Winter chanterelle extends the mushroom season deep into autumn and early winter when almost everything else has finished. A mossy patch of woodland floor in November, scattered with the small olive-brown caps, is a significant find. The flavour is lighter than chanterelle proper but genuinely good: delicate, slightly fruity, with a clean woodland character that suits simple cooking.

The mushroom is also known as yellowfoot chanterelle or trumpet chanterelle. It often grows in groups or dense patches, which makes filling a bag more practical than with chanterelle, where individual mushrooms are more scattered. Productive sites under birch and pine with deep moss can yield large quantities.

The structural difference from true gilled mushrooms is key to both identification and safety. The underside of the winter chanterelle cap does not have blade-like gills. Instead, it has forking, blunt, vein-like ridges — pale grey to grey-yellow — that run partway down the stem and fork repeatedly. These ridges are rounded, not sharp. Run a finger along them: they feel blunt and wax-like, not like the edge of a blade. This ridge-not-gill structure is shared with chanterelle (*Cantharellus cibarius*) and horn of plenty. No toxic species that grows in the same autumn woodland habitat shares this structure.

Funeral bell (*Galerina marginata*), the deadly confusion species, has true sharp gills, grows from wood (not soil), and has a ring on its stem. The checks take seconds.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 2–6cm across, funnel or trumpet-shaped with a wavy, irregular margin, often developing a central hole through to the hollow stem. Colour is olive-brown to yellow-brown, darker at the centre, paler at the margin. The upper surface is slightly dry and matt. Underside: grey to grey-yellow forking ridges that are blunt and vein-like, running partway down the stem. Stem is 3–8cm, hollow, yellow to pale orange-yellow, slender. Grows from soil or from deep moss, not directly from wood. No ring on the stem. Grows in groups or patches, often under birch or pine with sphagnum or other moss. Spore print is white to cream.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Funeral bell (*Galerina marginata*)** is deadly. It grows in the same months — autumn through early winter — in the same woodland habitats, often on or near dead wood. Eating even a small amount causes liver failure from amatoxins. The same toxins present in death cap.

Three tests distinguish winter chanterelle from funeral bell, and all three should be checked:

Winter chanterelle has forking, blunt, vein-like ridges on the underside — not true gills. Funeral bell has true gills: blade-like, crowded, rust-brown. Funeral bell has a distinct ring (or ring-stain) on the upper stem. Winter chanterelle has no ring. Winter chanterelle grows from soil or moss. Funeral bell grows on or from decaying wood — check the base carefully; the wood may be buried beneath the soil or moss.

All three checks take one minute. If any of these tests gives the wrong answer: do not eat it.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Mossy deciduous and coniferous woodland, particularly under birch, oak, and pine with a deep moss layer. Also in mixed woodland with sphagnum ground cover. Found throughout Britain, most productive in Scotland, Wales, northern England, and the west, where mossy wet woodland is more common. Less frequent in the dry lowland east. Often found in the same woodland as chanterelle, appearing later in the season when chanterelle has finished.

---

**In Season:**
September through December, occasionally into January. The main season is October and November. Frost does not stop fruiting — winter chanterelle is one of the most cold-tolerant edible fungi in Britain and continues to appear during mild December spells. Pick firm, well-formed caps; avoid any that are soggy, pale, or heavily waterlogged.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Pan-fry in butter: the best preparation. Cook over medium heat for four to five minutes. The caps are small and cook quickly; add garlic and thyme in the final minute. Season well. Serve on toast, with eggs, or alongside game. Sauce: add to a white wine and cream sauce with shallots; reduce briefly. The delicate flavour holds in a cream sauce better than in strongly flavoured reductions. Dried: dry at 80°C for two hours; the small size means they dry quickly. Rehydrate in warm water. The flavour concentrates well. Mixed with dried horn of plenty or dried cep as a winter mushroom stock powder.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking winter chanterelle from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Do not strip a single patch completely; pick from several areas to allow the mycelium to continue fruiting.

---

**SEO Title:** Winter Chanterelle — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find winter chanterelles in mossy woodland from September through December. Check for ridges not gills, no ring, and growing from soil — not from wood like deadly funeral bell.

---
---

## 157. St George's Mushroom

**Species Name:** St George's Mushroom
**Latin Name:** Calocybe gambosa
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** st-georges-mushroom
**Seasons:** Apr, May, Jun

---

**Short Description:**
St George's mushroom (*Calocybe gambosa*) is the only significant edible fungus in Britain that fruits in spring, typically appearing around St George's Day (23 April). The mealy, fresh-bread smell is its most important identifier, and it applies throughout. Two lookalikes require attention: fool's funnel (*Clitocybe rivulosa*), which is toxic, and livid pinkgill (*Entoloma sinuatum*), which causes serious gastric poisoning. The smell test and a spore print separate all three.

---

**Full Description:**
Finding St George's mushroom in April is a disproportionate pleasure. After months of little to nothing in the woodland and hedgerow, a ring of cream-white mushrooms in short grassland or at a woodland edge is a genuine seasonal event. The species appears with striking reliability around the same date each year at productive sites, and experienced foragers note those sites and return annually.

The flesh is white and firm, with a distinctive and persistent smell of fresh flour or damp bread dough — a mealy scent that is present in every part of the mushroom and becomes stronger when the flesh is broken. This smell is the single most useful identifier. It does not weaken on cooking and, crucially, is entirely different from the unpleasant smells of both of the main lookalike species.

The flavour is good: mild, slightly floury, with a firm texture that suits simple cooking. The mushroom can appear in large numbers on productive sites and is worth returning to across the spring season, as multiple flushes sometimes occur into June.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 5–15cm across, broadly convex to flat, sometimes wavy at the margin, white to cream to pale buff. Surface is smooth and slightly greasy in wet conditions. Gills are white to cream, crowded, sinuate (notched where they meet the stem), and remain white throughout. Stem is 3–7cm, 1.5–3cm wide, short and stout relative to the cap, white to cream, solid, with no ring and no volva. Flesh is white, firm, and does not change colour when cut. Smell: mealy, distinctly of fresh bread or flour — the key identifier. Spore print is white. Grows in short grassland, chalk downland, hedgerow bases, and woodland edges in spring. The spring season alone is a useful distinguisher — most confused species appear later.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Fool's funnel (*Clitocybe rivulosa*, also *Clitocybe dealbata*)** grows in similar short grassland and produces pale, cream-white mushrooms of similar size. It is toxic, causing profuse sweating, salivation, and vomiting from muscarine. The differences: fool's funnel has a less distinctive smell — slightly sweet or faintly unpleasant, not mealy; its gills are decurrent, running down the stem (St George's gills are sinuate, notching where they meet the stem but not running down it); fool's funnel is typically smaller, 2–5cm cap diameter, less robust than St George's. The smell test is the fastest and most reliable check.

**Livid pinkgill (*Entoloma sinuatum*)** causes severe gastroenteritis — not usually fatal, but hospitalisation is common and the illness is serious. Livid pinkgill gills start pale then turn distinctly pinkish-salmon as the spores mature; spore print is pink. St George's gills remain white throughout. Livid pinkgill has a rancid or unpleasant mealy smell, distinct from the pleasant fresh-flour smell of St George's. Livid pinkgill appears in summer and autumn near trees, not in spring grassland.

The reliable test protocol: smell the mushroom (pleasant and floury — good; unpleasant or faint — investigate further), check the gills (white and sinuate — good; running down the stem or pinkish — stop), take a spore print (white — good; pink or brown — do not eat).

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Short grassland, chalk and limestone downland, the grassy bases of old hedgerows, parkland, woodland clearings, and roadside banks. Found throughout Britain, most productive on chalk and limestone in southern England: the North Downs, South Downs, Chilterns, and Cotswolds. Also found in old established grassland further north and in Wales. Grows in rings or arcs that expand outward slightly each year; the rings persist at the same location for many years.

---

**In Season:**
April through June, with peak fruiting around St George's Day (23 April) in the south of England. The season shifts later in the north and in upland sites — May is peak in many northern and Scottish locations. Occasional flushes occur into June. The spring timing is one of the most reliable identifiers: St George's mushroom is the only substantial edible white mushroom appearing in grassland this early in the year.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Pan-fry in butter over medium heat for five to six minutes. The mealy flavour mellows pleasantly on cooking, leaving a mild, clean mushroom character. Season with salt and black pepper; a little parsley at the end works well. Serve on toast or alongside eggs — the spring season makes both very natural accompaniments. Soup: make a simple cream of mushroom soup; the flavour is subtler than cep but pleasant. Dried: dries well at 80°C for three hours; the dried mushroom retains a mild, savoury character and reconstitutes cleanly.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking St George's mushrooms from public grassland, commons, and downland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without permission is not permitted. Productive rings on chalk downland often fall within nature reserves — personal picking is generally unproblematic but confirm with site management for commercial quantities.

---

**SEO Title:** St George's Mushroom — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find St George's mushrooms in spring grassland from April. The mealy bread smell is the key test. Distinguish from fool's funnel and livid pinkgill before eating.

---
---

## 158. Morel

**Species Name:** Morel
**Latin Name:** Morchella esculenta agg.
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** morel
**Seasons:** Mar, Apr, May

---

**Short Description:**
The morel (*Morchella esculenta* agg.) is one of the most prized edible fungi in the world. It grows in spring — one of very few edible fungi to do so — in woodland clearings, old orchards, disturbed ground, and occasionally on bonfire sites. The distinctive honeycomb cap is unmistakeable. One species requires careful separation: false morel (*Gyromitra esculenta*), which is deadly. Cut every morel in half before eating: a true morel is completely hollow inside.

---

**Full Description:**
Morels are difficult to find and exceptional to eat. The spring season — March to May — is narrow, the growing conditions specific, and productive sites are hard won and closely guarded by the foragers who know them. Finding a morel is one of the more significant moments in British foraging.

The flavour is remarkable: intensely earthy and complex, with a savoury depth that dried morels make even more concentrated. Fresh morels have a texture that holds up to cooking in a way most fungi do not; they can be stuffed, braised, pan-fried, or added to cream sauces. The flavour is deep enough to dominate any dish.

*Morchella esculenta* is now understood to be an aggregate — a group of closely related species rather than a single species. British populations include *M. esculenta*, *M. vulgaris*, *M. elata*, and others. The distinction is taxonomic and irrelevant for eating purposes; all are excellent.

Morels must be cooked before eating. Raw morels contain thermolabile toxins that can cause gastrointestinal symptoms. Thorough cooking removes them. Never eat raw morels.

The hollow test is essential. Cut every morel in half from top to base before cooking. The entire interior — cap and stem — must be completely hollow. If there is any chambered structure, cottony fill, or developing flesh inside, you do not have a true morel.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 3–10cm tall, honeycomb-like in structure — a network of irregular, deep pits and ridges giving the surface a sponge-like or latticed appearance. Colour is pale yellow to ochre-brown to grey-brown. The cap is attached to the stem all the way down its length. Stem is 2–8cm, cream to white or pale buff, somewhat granular or ribbed on the surface. The entire fungus — cap and stem — is completely hollow when cut in half. This hollow interior is the definitive safety test. Grows from soil, singly or in small groups. No ring. No volva. The spring season and the honeycomb cap together are unmistakeable in context.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**False morel (*Gyromitra esculenta*)** is deadly and the reason this entry carries an Intermediate rating despite the visual distinctiveness of the true morel. False morel cap is irregularly brain-like or saddle-shaped — convoluted, folded, and lobed rather than pitted in a regular honeycomb pattern. The surface looks more like a wrinkled brain than a sponge. Cut the false morel in half: the interior is chambered or has cottony, irregular structure — not completely hollow. The cap of false morel is attached to the stem only at the top, with the cap lobes hanging free; the true morel cap is attached to the stem all the way down.

False morel contains gyromitrin, which converts to monomethylhydrazine in the body — a compound used in rocket fuel. It causes liver and kidney failure and is lethal in sufficient quantity. False morel is not common in Britain, being most frequent on sandy soils under conifers, but it occurs and it is dangerous.

The hollow test rules it out definitively. Cut every morel in half: hollow throughout — proceed. Any other internal structure — stop.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Woodland clearings, old orchards, the margins of elm woodland, disturbed ground, sand dunes, and occasionally the sites of old bonfires or burn piles. Grows on a range of soils but tends to favour calcareous or disturbed ground. Found throughout Britain, though genuinely uncommon. The most productive sites are old established orchards in southern England and the margins of dying or recently dead elm. Also found on recently burned or disturbed ground — populations appear on newly laid bark mulch in parks and gardens. Elusive and unpredictable; a productive site one year may produce nothing the following year.

---

**In Season:**
March through May, with peak fruiting in April in the south of England. The season shifts later northward. The entire spring window can be as short as two to three weeks at a given site. Morels appear quickly, fruit for a period, and then are gone. Check productive sites regularly through April.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Always cook thoroughly before eating. Never eat raw. Clean carefully: the honeycomb cap surface traps soil and debris. Slice in half, rinse briefly, and dry. Pan-fry in butter over medium heat for five to six minutes, turning once. Season with salt. The flavour needs nothing more, but a splash of cream added to the pan at the end and reduced briefly is classic and excellent. With cream sauce: reduce a good stock and cream together, add cooked morels, and serve with pasta or with roast chicken. Stuffed: halve large morels and fill with a mixture of breadcrumbs, garlic, herbs, and butter; bake at 180°C for 12–15 minutes. Dried: dry at 80°C for three to four hours or in a dehydrator. The flavour concentrates dramatically. Dried morels are among the most intensely flavoured dried fungi available. Rehydrate in warm water and use the soaking water in the dish.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking morels from public woodland and land for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Morel populations are localised and often small; pick a proportion of any colony and leave the remainder.

---

**SEO Title:** Morel — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find morels in woodland clearings and orchards from March. Cut in half before eating — must be completely hollow. Deadly false morel has brain-like cap and chambered interior.

---
---

## 159. Cauliflower Fungus

**Species Name:** Cauliflower Fungus
**Latin Name:** Sparassis crispa
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** cauliflower-fungus
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Cauliflower fungus (*Sparassis crispa*) grows at the base of pine trees from late summer. It is one of the most visually distinctive edible fungi in Britain: a large, cream-coloured mass of tightly packed, frilly, brain-like lobes, sometimes the size of a football. It has no dangerous lookalikes in Britain. Clean it carefully — the lobes trap debris — and cook with butter. The flavour is mild, nutty, and good.

---

**Full Description:**
Cauliflower fungus is one of those species that bypasses any beginner anxiety about mushroom identification. It looks like nothing else in British woodland. A mature specimen at the base of a Scots pine — cream to pale yellow, layered and frilled, 20–50cm across — is unmistakeable on first encounter and on every subsequent one.

The flavour is mild: slightly nutty, with a gentle earthiness, and a firm-but-yielding texture that holds up well in the pan. It is not as intensely flavoured as cep or horn of plenty, but its size makes it a substantial find, and it stores better than most fresh fungi if kept cool and dry.

The main challenge is cleaning. The tightly packed frilly lobes trap soil, conifer needles, insects, and other debris from the woodland floor. Separate the lobes gently and work through with a dry brush before cutting into more manageable pieces. A brief rinse in cold water, followed by drying on a cloth, is usually sufficient for the remainder.

Cauliflower fungus is a parasitic species attacking the roots of pine trees, causing a butt rot. It appears from the same tree year after year — sometimes for decades — until the tree dies. Productive trees are worth noting and revisiting.

---

**Identification Notes:**
The fruiting body is 15–50cm across, a dense, multi-branched mass of flat, frilly lobes arranged in overlapping tiers from a short, thick, whitish base attached to tree roots. Colour is cream to pale yellow-buff when fresh, darkening to tan or brown with age. The lobes are 2–6cm wide, flat, and wavy to frilled at the edges, with a firm, slightly rubbery texture. The whole structure has a rough resemblance to a cauliflower or a large, complex sea creature. Grows at the base of pine trees, typically Scots pine, occasionally other conifers. No gills, pores, or cap. The species is entirely specific in Britain: nothing else resembles it.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Cauliflower fungus has no dangerous lookalikes in British woodland. No toxic British fungus produces a similar cream, multi-lobed, frilly mass at the base of a pine tree. Hen of the woods (*Grifola frondosa*) is a bracket fungus growing at the base of oak trees with a different structure — overlapping fan-shaped brackets, grey-brown above, white-pored below. It is edible and bears only a passing resemblance to cauliflower fungus.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
At the base of pine trees, particularly Scots pine, in woodland and plantation throughout Britain. Also occasionally on other conifers. Found wherever mature pine woodland exists: the Scottish Highlands, the New Forest, Breckland in East Anglia, and planted conifer woodland throughout Britain. Grows from the same tree year after year, often appearing in the same month within a narrow seasonal window. An old, large Scots pine with a rough base and visible roots is the habitat to search.

---

**In Season:**
August through November, with most finds in September and October. Appears from the same tree annually; note the tree and return. Fresh specimens are cream-white and firm; older ones yellow and increasingly tough. Pick while the lobes are still pale and the flesh is firm. Large specimens can weigh several kilograms — more than any reasonable forager needs in a single picking.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Clean first: separate lobes, brush away debris, rinse briefly, and dry. Cut into manageable pieces — palm-sized sections work well. Pan-fry in butter over medium-high heat for five to six minutes until the edges colour. Season well with salt and pepper. Garlic and thyme work particularly well alongside. The texture is good — firm enough to fry without collapsing, but not tough. Tempura: coat small pieces in a light tempura batter and fry at 180°C; the frilly texture holds the batter well and the result is excellent. Soup: chop and add to a chicken or mushroom stock; the flavour is mild enough to work in a light broth. Dried: slice and dry at 80°C for three to four hours. The mild flavour concentrates usefully and the dried product stores well.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking cauliflower fungus from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Where the fungus grows on trees in managed forestry, consider the tree owner — it is a parasitic species, but removing the fruiting body does not meaningfully harm the tree or slow the rot.

---

**SEO Title:** Cauliflower Fungus — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find cauliflower fungus at the base of pine trees from August. Large, frilly, cream-white mass with no dangerous lookalikes. Clean carefully and fry in butter.

---
---

## 160. Dryad's Saddle

**Species Name:** Dryad's Saddle
**Latin Name:** Cerioporus squamosus
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** dryads-saddle
**Seasons:** May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Dryad's saddle (*Cerioporus squamosus*, formerly *Polyporus squamosus*) is a large, bracket fungus growing on dead and dying deciduous trees from spring. The concentric brown scales on a cream background, the pore surface below, and the distinctive melon-like smell identify it immediately. Only young, pale, tender specimens are worth eating — older brackets become tough and unpleasant. It has no dangerous lookalikes.

---

**Full Description:**
Dryad's saddle is one of the most commonly encountered bracket fungi in Britain and one of the easiest to identify. It appears from April on dead and dying elms, beeches, sycamores, and other hardwoods, sometimes in large overlapping tiers that cover a significant length of trunk or fallen log. The smell is instantly recognisable: fresh, melon-like, slightly sweet, reminiscent of watermelon rind. It is a pleasant smell that carries at a distance.

The edibility is entirely age-dependent. Very young, pale specimens — those that are still creamy-white, with soft flexible flesh and small pores — are genuinely good: mild, slightly melon-flavoured, with a pleasant firm texture. Brackets that have turned dark and tough are unpleasant to eat and not worth the effort. The window between young-and-edible and old-and-tough is short, often measured in days.

This is a species that rewards knowing productive trees and visiting them frequently in spring. A dead elm that produced good young brackets in May is likely to do so again in following years. The species reappears on the same timber repeatedly.

The reclassification from *Polyporus squamosus* to *Cerioporus squamosus* is recent; the older name appears in most British field guides published before 2020.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 10–60cm across, fan or kidney-shaped, cream to pale buff with concentric zones of dark brown fibrous scales arranged in a regular pattern across the surface. The overall impression is of a large, pale, scaly bracket. The underside has angular white pores, 1–3 per mm, clearly visible to the naked eye. The stem is short, thick, and lateral or off-centre, pale above, darkening to black at the base where it attaches to the wood. Flesh is white, firm in young specimens, becoming tough and fibrous with age. Smell is distinctly of fresh melon or watermelon rind. Grows from dead or dying deciduous wood, singly or in overlapping tiers. The brown-scaled cream cap, white pores, melon smell, and hardwood host are collectively specific.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Dryad's saddle has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British bracket fungus has the same pattern of brown scales on a cream cap with white angular pores and a melon smell. Giant polypore (*Meripilus giganteus*) is a large bracket fungus at the base of beech trees, brown and cream with small pores, but lacks the scale pattern and the melon smell. Hen of the woods (*Grifola frondosa*) also grows at the base of oak, is more irregularly branched, and lacks scales. Both are edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Dead and dying deciduous trees, particularly elm, beech, sycamore, ash, and willow. Found throughout Britain wherever mature deciduous trees exist. Common in hedgerows, parkland, and woodland. One of the first bracket fungi to appear in spring — it can be found as early as April, considerably earlier than most edible fungi. Often grows on trees that have recently died or that have significant wounds and decay. Reappears on the same timber over multiple seasons.

---

**In Season:**
April through September, with the best young growth in May and June. New flushes can appear after summer rain on productive trees. The edible window is the earliest growth — pale, cream-white, with flexible flesh and small fine pores. By summer, most brackets have toughened. Seek out trees with recent new growth rather than established, darkened brackets.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Use young growth only: cut away any tough or darkened flesh and use only the pale, firm outer margin. Slice into strips about 1cm thick. Pan-fry in butter or oil over medium-high heat for four to five minutes; the texture firms pleasantly and the melon flavour mellows to a mild, savoury character. Season well. Good alongside eggs, in stir-fries, or in a spring vegetable dish. The melon smell largely disappears on cooking. Slice thinly for a raw addition to salads — only from very young, fresh specimens. The texture holds raw better than most fungi.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking dryad's saddle from public woodland and trees for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. The fungus grows on dead or stressed trees; picking the fruiting body has no meaningful impact on the underlying timber decay.

---

**SEO Title:** Dryad's Saddle — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find dryad's saddle on dead hardwood trees from April. Brown-scaled cream bracket with a melon smell. Only young pale growth is worth eating — older brackets are tough.

---
