## 161. Jelly Ear

**Species Name:** Jelly Ear
**Latin Name:** Auricularia auricula-judae
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** jelly-ear
**Seasons:** Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec

---

**Short Description:**
Jelly ear (*Auricularia auricula-judae*) grows on elder throughout the year and is one of the easiest edible fungi to identify in Britain. The brown, rubbery, ear-shaped fruiting body on dead elder wood is unmistakeable. The flavour is very mild; the texture — gelatinous and slightly crunchy — is the point. Used widely in Chinese and East Asian cooking, where it is sold dried as wood ear or cloud ear mushroom.

---

**Full Description:**
Jelly ear is a year-round fungus, fruiting on elder in every month when conditions are not extreme. On mild winter days when almost nothing else is available, a dead elder branch covered in clusters of brown rubbery ears is a reliable find. It is not the most exciting edible fungus in flavour terms, but it is consistent, safe, and useful.

The connection to elder is strong. *Auricularia auricula-judae* grows primarily on elder (*Sambucus nigra*) in Britain, occasionally on other hardwoods — sycamore, beech, and sometimes elm — but elder is where to look first. The name "judae" refers to Judas Iscariot, whose legendary hanging from an elder tree embedded itself in the mushroom's English name across centuries of folklore.

The texture is the selling point. Raw or briefly cooked, jelly ear has a satisfying, slightly gelatinous crunch — nothing like the soft or silky texture of most edible fungi. This texture survives cooking well, particularly stir-frying at high heat and soaking in soups. Dried, the mushroom shrivels to a fraction of its fresh size and rehydrates completely, returning the texture intact. The flavour is very mild — clean and slightly earthy — which makes it useful as a texture element in any dish rather than as a flavour driver.

In Chinese cooking it is a staple: dried wood ear or cloud ear mushroom is the same species, added to soups, stir-fries, and hot and sour dishes for texture. Finding it fresh in Britain gives access to exactly the same ingredient without the drying and rehydration step.

---

**Identification Notes:**
The fruiting body is 3–10cm across, ear or cup-shaped, with a lobed or irregular margin. The outer surface is brown to red-brown, slightly velvety when dry, smooth and rubbery when moist. The inner surface (the concave side) is slightly paler and smoother. Texture is gelatinous and rubbery — flexible, not brittle. Attached to dead or dying elder wood, often in clusters. When dry, the fungus shrivels to a dark, much smaller, rigid form; it rehydrates and returns to full size in water. Growing on dead elder, with the ear shape and gelatinous texture, leaves no practical room for confusion.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Jelly ear has no dangerous lookalikes in the British habitat where it grows. Yellow brain fungus (*Tremella mesenterica*) is bright yellow to orange and grows on dead wood; it is edible and entirely different in colour. Witches' butter (*Exidia glandulosa*) is black, irregular, and blobby, growing on dead oak; also edible. No toxic British fungus produces a brown ear-shaped rubbery fruiting body on dead elder.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Dead and dying elder throughout Britain. Also occasionally on sycamore, beech, and other hardwoods. Grows in hedgerows, woodland edges, gardens, and anywhere elder is present — which is almost everywhere. Elder is one of the most common woody plants in Britain, and jelly ear follows it reliably. Productive in any season when the weather is not extremely dry or extremely cold. After rain in mild weather, clusters appear quickly on suitable wood.

---

**In Season:**
Year-round, with peaks in autumn and during mild winter spells. Best avoided during prolonged summer drought (the fruiting bodies dry out and become unpleasant) and during hard frosts (they freeze and become mushy on thawing, though the flavour is unaffected). A dead elder branch after a mild, wet November day is one of the most reliable foraging finds in Britain.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Clean briefly: wipe or rinse. Slice into strips if large. Stir-fried: cook over very high heat in oil for two to three minutes; jelly ear can withstand temperatures that would destroy most fungi and the texture holds. Add to any stir-fry in the last two minutes. Hot and sour soup: add sliced jelly ear to a chicken or vegetable broth with rice vinegar, chilli, and soy; simmer for five minutes. The texture in soup is excellent. Dried: spread on a tray and dry at 80°C for two to three hours until completely rigid. The volume reduces dramatically. Rehydrate in cold water for 20 minutes before use — the texture returns fully. Dried jelly ear keeps for a year in a sealed jar.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking jelly ear from public land and woodland for personal use is permitted. The fungus grows on elder, which is among the most common hedgerow plants in Britain; picking is unproblematic in almost any context. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Jelly Ear — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find jelly ear on dead elder year-round. Brown, rubbery, ear-shaped fungus with no dangerous lookalikes. Same species as dried wood ear. Good in stir-fries and soup.

---
---

## 162. Scarlet Elf Cup

**Species Name:** Scarlet Elf Cup
**Latin Name:** Sarcoscypha austriaca
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** scarlet-elf-cup
**Seasons:** Jan, Feb, Mar

---

**Short Description:**
Scarlet elf cup (*Sarcoscypha austriaca*) grows on dead wood in damp woodland from January to March. The vivid scarlet interior of the cup-shaped fruiting body against the grey winter woodland floor is one of the most striking sights in British foraging. The flavour is mild and the main interest is the colour. It has no dangerous lookalikes.

---

**Full Description:**
Finding scarlet elf cup on a grey February morning in damp woodland is a genuine lift. A cluster of vivid crimson cups, 2–6cm across, sitting on a mossy dead branch in otherwise monochrome winter woodland, looks placed rather than grown. Nothing else in the winter British landscape looks remotely similar.

Two species produce the same scarlet cup fruiting body and are effectively indistinguishable without microscopy: *Sarcoscypha austriaca* and *Sarcoscypha coccinea*. Both occur in Britain; *S. austriaca* is now considered more common, particularly in the north and west. Both are edible and treated identically for foraging purposes. The entry uses *S. austriaca* following current British mycological consensus, but references to *S. coccinea* in older guides refer to the same foraging encounter.

The flavour is mild — slightly earthy, with a clean freshness — and the texture is firm and cup-like. It is not among the most intensely flavoured fungi on this site, but as a winter ingredient it is genuinely useful. The vivid colour makes it an outstanding garnish: it holds on cooking and transforms any pale dish.

Scarlet elf cup grows on buried or partially buried dead sticks and branches, often of hazel, willow, or elm, in damp deciduous woodland. Seek it in valley bottoms, beside streams, and in wet woodland where the humidity is high and the wood decays slowly.

---

**Identification Notes:**
The fruiting body is 2–6cm across, cup or disc-shaped, with the inner (upper) surface vivid scarlet to crimson and the outer surface pale pink to whitish, covered in fine, short hairs visible under magnification. The margin is inrolled when young, becoming flat or slightly wavy when mature. The cup sits on a short, sometimes branched stalk attached to dead wood, which may be buried in leaf litter. No gills, pores, or cap — the entire spore-producing surface is the smooth inner cup. Growing on dead wood in damp winter woodland, the scarlet colour is entirely specific to this species and its close relatives.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Scarlet elf cup has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British fungus produces a vivid scarlet, smooth-faced cup on dead wood in winter. Orange peel fungus (*Aleuria aurantia*) is bright orange rather than scarlet and grows directly from bare soil rather than from wood; it is also edible. Eyelash cup (*Scutellinia scutellata*) is much smaller (under 1cm) and has conspicuous dark hairs fringing the margin; also edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Damp deciduous woodland, particularly in valley bottoms, beside streams, and in wet woodland where dead wood decays slowly. Grows on dead sticks and branches, often partially buried, of hazel, willow, alder, and elm. Found throughout Britain, with the most productive sites in Wales, northern England, Scotland, and the west of England where high rainfall maintains the humidity the fungus requires. Often appears in the same location year after year on the same rotting wood.

---

**In Season:**
January through March, occasionally into April in cool years. A winter and very early spring fungus, appearing when almost nothing else edible is fruiting. The cups are at their best when fully open and vivid; older cups fade to orange-red and the margin begins to deteriorate. Pick freshly opened cups in good condition and use promptly.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
The colour is the primary asset. The flavour is mild and the texture firm but not remarkable. Use as a garnish: place whole cups on pale dishes — poached eggs, ricotta on toast, white bean soups, goat's cheese — where the scarlet reads as a deliberate splash of colour. The colour holds on brief cooking. Briefly sautéed in butter for two to three minutes, the cup softens slightly and the scarlet deepens. Add to a salad of winter leaves (chicory, endive) for visual contrast. Stuffed: larger cups can hold a small amount of soft cheese, cream, or a single quail's egg yolk. Mild in flavour but striking in presentation.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking scarlet elf cup from public woodland and damp ground for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Scarlet Elf Cup — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find scarlet elf cups on dead wood in damp woodland from January. Vivid crimson cups with no dangerous lookalikes. Mild flavour — exceptional for colour in winter dishes.

---
---

## 163. Bay Bolete

**Species Name:** Bay Bolete
**Latin Name:** Imleria badia
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** bay-bolete
**Seasons:** Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Bay bolete (*Imleria badia*, formerly *Boletus badius*) is one of the most common and reliable edible boletes in British woodland. The dark chocolate-brown cap, white to pale yellow pores that turn distinctly blue when pressed, and woodland habitat identify it clearly. The flavour is close to cep — rich, earthy, and excellent dried. One species requires ruling out: bitter bolete (*Tylopilus felleus*). The taste test is fast and definitive.

---

**Full Description:**
Bay bolete is a consistent autumn find in British woodland. It is not as celebrated as cep, but experienced foragers often prefer it — partly because the flavour is genuinely very good and partly because it is considerably more common than cep on many sites, meaning a productive woodland can yield a useful quantity even in an average year.

The flavour when cooked is rich and savoury, close to cep with a slightly more pronounced earthiness. Dried, the bay bolete concentrates beautifully, producing a powder or flake that rivals dried cep for depth of flavour in sauces and risottos. For foragers building a store of dried wild mushrooms, bay bolete is among the most practical species to work with.

The blue staining reaction is one of the more distinctive features of this species and useful as an identification check. Press the pore surface or cut the flesh: within seconds to a minute, both turn distinctly blue. This is not the dramatic, almost instant flash of blue seen in some toxic boletes, but a clear, definite colour change. The flesh staining blue is not a danger indicator for bay bolete — it is simply characteristic of this species.

The reclassification from *Boletus badius* to *Imleria badia* is relatively recent; older field guides use the former name.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 4–15cm across, broadly convex to flat, deep chocolate to bay-brown, smooth and slightly tacky in wet conditions, drying more matt. The margin is often slightly paler. Underside: pore surface, cream when young, turning pale yellow as the mushroom matures. Pores are angular, 1–2 per mm. Press the pore surface: it stains distinctly blue within seconds to a minute. Stem is 5–12cm, 1–3cm wide, pale brown to ochre-brown with fine longitudinal streaking but no net-like pattern (unlike cep). Flesh is pale cream to white, staining blue on cutting, most strongly at the junction of cap and stem. Smell is pleasant and mushroomy. Spore print is olive-brown. Grows in deciduous and coniferous woodland, often under pine and birch.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Bitter bolete (*Tylopilus felleus*)** is the key lookalike and is inedible — intensely, immediately bitter, ruining any dish it enters even in small quantities. The test: touch a tiny fragment of raw flesh to your tongue. Bitter bolete is unmistakeably, shockingly bitter within seconds. Bay bolete is mild. This test takes three seconds and is entirely reliable. Bitter bolete pore surface has a pinkish tinge and does not stain blue when pressed; bay bolete pores are cream to yellow and stain distinctly blue. Bitter bolete stem has a coarser, darker net pattern; bay bolete stem shows only longitudinal streaking, no net.

No dangerous (as opposed to inedible) species closely resembles bay bolete. Satan's bolete (*Rubroboletus satanas*) has vivid red pores — entirely different from the cream-yellow pores of bay bolete.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Deciduous and coniferous woodland, particularly under pine, birch, and beech. Often abundant at the edges of conifer plantations and in mixed woodland. Found throughout Britain, most common in Scotland, northern England, and Wales under pine and birch, and in southern England under beech and oak. One of the most frequently encountered boletes in British woodland during autumn. Often fruits heavily on the same site year after year.

---

**In Season:**
July through November, with the main flush in September and October. Often fruits more abundantly and later into the season than cep on the same site. Check after rain following a warm spell. Bay bolete at the cap-button stage, when the cap is still convex and the pores are cream-white, is the best eating; fully flat, older caps with yellowed pores are still good but check for maggot damage.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Treat identically to cep. Pan-fry in butter over high heat; the blue staining disappears on cooking and the colour of the cooked flesh is unremarkable — pale brown. Season with garlic and thyme. Risotto: add fresh or rehydrated dried bay bolete to a standard mushroom risotto. Dried: slice thinly and dry at 80°C for three to four hours. The flavour concentrates significantly and is excellent. Powder the dried slices in a spice grinder for a mushroom seasoning. Soup: blend with onion, garlic, and stock; finish with cream. Pickle: young, firm caps can be lightly pickled in seasoned white wine vinegar for a Scandinavian-style preserved mushroom.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking bay bolete from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Bay Bolete — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find bay boletes in woodland from July. Dark brown cap, pores blue when pressed. Rule out bitter bolete with a taste test. Excellent eating — close to cep in flavour.

---
---

## 164. Slippery Jack

**Species Name:** Slippery Jack
**Latin Name:** Suillus luteus
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** slippery-jack
**Seasons:** Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Slippery jack (*Suillus luteus*) grows exclusively under Scots pine from September. The slimy, sticky, chocolate-brown cap, yellow pore surface, and ring on the stem identify it reliably. Peel the slimy cap skin before cooking — it causes digestive upset in some people and the texture is unpleasant regardless. The flavour underneath is mild and good. Has no dangerous lookalikes under pine.

---

**Full Description:**
Slippery jack is easy to find and easy to identify, but requires one preparation step that is genuinely non-optional: peel the cap skin before cooking. The slimy outer skin of the cap has a slightly laxative effect in some people when eaten and the texture is unappealing in any case. Peeling is straightforward — the skin comes away from the cap edge in strips with minimal effort, particularly when the mushroom is fresh.

The underlying cap flesh is mild and pleasant. The flavour is softer than cep, without the depth and complexity, but serviceable. Slippery jack fruits abundantly under Scots pine in autumn and can produce large quantities on productive sites. Where better-flavoured boletes are absent, it is worth picking in quantity.

The association with Scots pine is absolute. Slippery jack is a mycorrhizal species specific to pine, and it does not occur under any other tree. If there is no Scots pine within a reasonable distance, the mushroom is something else. This habitat specificity is a strong identification tool.

The ring on the stem is another reliable feature: a distinct, membranous skirt hanging around the upper stem, often stained purple-brown below from falling spores. This ring, combined with the slimy cap and pine habitat, is the identification picture.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 5–15cm, broadly convex to flat, deep chocolate to red-brown, with a distinctly slimy or sticky surface in moist conditions (drying to a glossy finish in dry weather). Underside: pore surface, pale yellow to lemon-yellow, fine-pored, 2–3 per mm. Stem is 4–9cm, 1–2cm wide, pale yellow above the ring, paler below, with a distinct membranous ring that often hangs skirt-like around the upper stem and turns purple-brown from spore deposits below. Flesh is pale yellow, not changing colour significantly when cut. Smell is mild and pleasant. Grows under Scots pine. Spore print is cinnamon-brown. Peel the cap skin before using.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Slippery jack has no dangerous lookalikes under Scots pine. Other *Suillus* species — including larch bolete (*Suillus grevillei*) and brown birch bolete (*Leccinum scabrum*) — grow under different trees and are not found under Scots pine. The combination of slimy brown cap, yellow pores, ring on the stem, and Scots pine habitat is specific to *Suillus luteus* and its immediate relatives, all of which are edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Under Scots pine (*Pinus sylvestris*), in both native Caledonian pine woodland and plantation pine. Found throughout Britain wherever Scots pine grows: the Scottish Highlands, Breckland in East Anglia, the New Forest, and conifer plantations across Wales and northern England. Often abundant on sandy, acidic soils under mature trees. Does not occur under spruce, larch, or other conifers; Scots pine specifically.

---

**In Season:**
September through November. Fruits prolifically in good conditions after rain. The slimy cap is more manageable to peel when the mushroom is fresh and slightly dry; very wet conditions make the slime difficult to handle. Pick into a separate bag to avoid contaminating drier mushrooms with slime.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Peel the cap skin first: grip the skin at the cap edge and pull toward the centre in strips. It comes away cleanly from a fresh mushroom. Discard the skin. Pan-fry in butter over medium-high heat; the flesh is soft and cooks quickly. The yellow pore layer can also be peeled away if preferred — it absorbs a lot of butter and becomes slightly slimy on cooking, though it is edible. Season with salt, pepper, and parsley. Soup: slice and add to a mushroom soup base — the flavour integrates well in liquid. Pickled: a traditional preparation in central and eastern Europe; slice and pickle in seasoned vinegar after brief blanching. Dried: slice and dry at 80°C; the flavour concentrates moderately.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking slippery jack from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Slippery Jack — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find slippery jacks under Scots pine from September. Peel the slimy cap skin before cooking — it causes upset in some people. Mild, reliable flavour. No dangerous lookalikes.

---
---

## 165. Saffron Milk Cap

**Species Name:** Saffron Milk Cap
**Latin Name:** Lactarius deliciosus
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** saffron-milk-cap
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Saffron milk cap (*Lactarius deliciosus*) grows under conifers — particularly pine and spruce — from late summer. The orange cap with concentric paler zones, the bright orange latex (milk) that oozes when the gills are cut, and the orange-staining flesh together identify it reliably. One species to rule out: woolly milk cap (*Lactarius torminosus*), which is toxic. The milk colour and cap texture are the decisive tests.

---

**Full Description:**
Saffron milk cap is a prized edible fungus across southern Europe, where it is sold in markets and treated as a serious culinary ingredient. In Britain it is less celebrated, partly because it is less common than in Mediterranean pine forests, and partly because British foragers have tended to focus on boletes and chanterelles. It is genuinely good eating: firm, meaty, with a distinctive flavour that has a faint carrot-like sweetness alongside an earthy, resinous depth. The orange colour fades to green with age and on cooking, which is normal and not a sign of deterioration.

The defining feature of the *Lactarius* genus is the latex — the "milk" — that oozes from the gills when they are cut or broken. In saffron milk cap, this latex is bright orange, turning slowly deeper orange-red on exposure to air. This is the key identifier and the key safety test: the milk must be orange, not white. Woolly milk cap, the toxic species, produces white milk.

The orange staining extends throughout the flesh: cut the cap and the flesh is pale to deep orange, staining green-grey with age or on bruising. This green staining in older specimens is normal and harmless; it does not indicate the mushroom is past eating, provided the flesh is still firm.

Saffron milk cap is primarily found under pine (*Pinus* spp.) and spruce (*Picea abies*) in Britain. Its distribution is patchy — most reliable in Scotland under Scots pine, and in planted spruce woodland throughout Britain. It is not a species to encounter routinely in lowland deciduous woodland.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 4–14cm, convex when young becoming depressed or funnel-shaped with age, orange to orange-red with concentric zones of slightly paler or darker colour — these zones are subtle but visible on close inspection. The cap surface is smooth, slightly waxy, not slimy. Older caps develop green-grey patches or overall greenish tinges, which is characteristic of this species. Gills are crowded, orange, and ooze bright orange latex when cut or damaged. Stem is 3–8cm, 1.5–3cm wide, orange, often with small darker orange pits on the surface. Flesh is orange to pale orange, firm, staining slowly green-grey when cut or bruised. Smell is mild and slightly fruity-resinous. Spore print is pale cream to yellowish. Grows under conifers.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Woolly milk cap (*Lactarius torminosus*)** is the critical lookalike. It is toxic, causing severe gastroenteritis. The tests are clear and reliable:

Woolly milk cap produces white milk — cut the gills and the latex that oozes is white. Saffron milk cap produces orange milk. This is the primary and fastest test. Woolly milk cap has a distinctly woolly or shaggy margin — the cap edge is visibly hairy and rolled inward. Saffron milk cap has a smooth cap margin. Woolly milk cap grows exclusively under birch trees. Saffron milk cap grows under pine and spruce. Habitat is a reliable secondary check.

Cut the gills. Orange milk: saffron milk cap. White milk: do not eat.

No other orange milk cap with orange latex in British conifer woodland is toxic.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Under conifers, primarily Scots pine and spruce, in both native and planted woodland. Found throughout Britain wherever productive conifer woodland exists: the Scottish Highlands, northern England, Wales, and conifer plantations across the country. Most reliable in Caledonian pine woodland in Scotland and in older spruce plantations. Less common in southern deciduous woodland. Often found in the same sites as slippery jack and other conifer-associated fungi.

---

**In Season:**
August through November. Most productive in September and October. Fresh, orange caps in good condition are the target; older, heavily green-stained caps are past their best. The green staining of older caps is harmless but the flesh becomes softer and less pleasant.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Pan-fry in olive oil (butter browns too fast against the cooking time needed) over medium heat for five to six minutes. Season with salt and a little garlic. The colour shifts from orange toward a more muted green-brown on cooking — normal and unavoidable. The flavour is good: earthy, slightly sweet, meaty. Serve alongside game or as part of a mixed mushroom dish where the saffron milk cap provides body. In Spain and France it is frequently cooked on a griddle with olive oil, salt, and little else. Pickled: a traditional preparation in eastern Europe — blanch briefly, cool, and pack in seasoned vinegar with peppercorns and bay. Dried: dries adequately but the colour goes fully grey-green; the flavour is better fresh.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking saffron milk caps from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted. Many productive sites are in Forestry Commission or privately owned conifer woodland; personal picking is generally unproblematic.

---

**SEO Title:** Saffron Milk Cap — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find saffron milk caps under pine and spruce from August. Orange milk from the gills is the key test — white milk means woolly milk cap, which is toxic. Excellent pan-fried.

---
---

## 166. Amethyst Deceiver

**Species Name:** Amethyst Deceiver
**Latin Name:** Laccaria amethystina
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** amethyst-deceiver
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Amethyst deceiver (*Laccaria amethystina*) is one of the most intensely coloured fungi in British woodland. Young specimens are a deep violet-purple throughout — cap, gills, and stem. The colour fades significantly as the mushroom dries out, which is the source of the "deceiver" name. One group requires ruling out: *Cortinarius* species (webcaps), some of which are violet or purple and deadly. A spore print is the definitive test. Take one before eating any purple-tinted woodland mushroom.

---

**Full Description:**
Amethyst deceiver is common in deciduous woodland from late summer and is hard to miss when fresh. The young caps are an intense violet-purple — a colour that stands out sharply against autumn leaf litter. Finding a cluster of them under beech is a striking sight.

The "deceiver" part of the name refers to the variable appearance as the mushroom ages and dries. Fresh, well-hydrated specimens are deeply violet. As they dry — in warm weather or as they age — the colour fades to a dull lilac-buff or pale grey-brown, making them look like a different fungus entirely. Rewet a dry cap with a damp cloth and the violet colour returns. This colour change catches people out repeatedly and is the reason for the name.

The flavour is mild and the mushroom is worth eating, though it is not among the most intensely flavoured edible species. Its value is partly practical — it is abundant — and partly visual, where a few intensely violet specimens used as a garnish or scattered through a pale dish are striking.

The safety concern is not complex but it is serious. Several *Cortinarius* (webcap) species also show violet or purple colouring. Some of these are responsible for Orellanine poisoning, which causes irreversible kidney failure with a delay of two to three weeks before symptoms appear. The spore print test separates them reliably: amethyst deceiver prints white to pale lilac. *Cortinarius* species print rusty orange-brown. Take a print before eating any purple-tinted woodland mushroom you cannot identify with complete confidence.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 1–5cm, convex when young becoming flat or slightly depressed, deep violet-purple when fresh and moist, fading to pale lilac-buff or grey-brown when dry. The surface is dry and slightly mealy. Gills are broadly spaced, thick, violet-purple when fresh, fading with the cap. Stem is 3–8cm, slender, fibrous, violet-purple matching the cap, often with a slightly twisted appearance. Flesh is thin, pale violet, and does not change colour when cut. No ring. No volva. Spore print is white to very pale lilac. Grows from soil in deciduous woodland, particularly under beech and oak, singly or in scattered groups. The violet colouring throughout, woodland habitat, and white spore print identify it. Dried, faded specimens look entirely different — rewet to confirm the violet.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Cortinarius species (webcaps)** — some show violet, purple, or blue-violet colouring in the cap, gills, or stem. Cortinarius species have a cobweb-like veil (cortina) between the cap margin and the stem when young, leaving rusty-orange spore deposits on the stem as they mature. Amethyst deceiver has no cortina and no rust-coloured spore deposits on the stem. The spore print of Cortinarius is rusty orange-brown. Amethyst deceiver prints white to very pale lilac. Cortinarius species are typically more robust, with a thicker stem and larger cap. Eating a Cortinarius species causes irreversible kidney failure with a two-to-three-week delay in symptoms.

**Lilac fibrecap (*Inocybe geophylla* var. *lilacina*)** is toxic. It is smaller than amethyst deceiver (cap 1–3cm), has a silky, fibrous, conical cap with a pointed centre, and a brown spore print. Its gills start pale lilac and darken as spores mature.

Take a spore print before eating. White to pale lilac: amethyst deceiver is consistent. Orange-brown: do not eat.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Deciduous woodland, particularly under beech, oak, and birch, in deep leaf litter. Found throughout Britain, common in southern and central England and in Wales. Often grows in the same sites as other autumn woodland fungi. One of the more frequently encountered small woodland mushrooms in autumn.

---

**In Season:**
August through November. Fresh violet specimens appear after rain; dry conditions cause rapid colour fade. Check a damp cloth test on any pale-coloured specimen you are uncertain about. Most productive in September and October.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Pan-fry in butter over medium heat for four to five minutes. The intense violet colour fades almost entirely on cooking — the cooked mushroom is a pale grey-buff, which is a significant visual disappointment after the raw intensity. Use raw as a garnish on pale dishes where the colour will have maximum impact: scatter over ricotta, soft cheese, or a pale soup just before serving. The flavour is mild; simple seasoning is all it needs. Mix with other autumn fungi in a pan of mixed woodland mushrooms where size and flavour variety are the goal.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking amethyst deceiver from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without permission is not permitted. Take a spore print before eating any purple-tinted mushroom collected from woodland.

---

**SEO Title:** Amethyst Deceiver — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find amethyst deceivers in beech and oak woodland from August. Intense violet colour fades with drying. Take a spore print — webcaps are deadly and also violet.

---
---

## 167. Larch Bolete

**Species Name:** Larch Bolete
**Latin Name:** Suillus grevillei
**Type:** Fungi
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** larch-bolete
**Seasons:** Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov

---

**Short Description:**
Larch bolete (*Suillus grevillei*) grows exclusively under larch and is one of the most abundant edible fungi in larch woodland from late summer. The bright yellow to orange-brown slimy cap, yellow pore surface, and ring on the stem identify it clearly. Peel the cap skin before cooking. The flavour is mild but serviceable. Has no dangerous lookalikes under larch.

---

**Full Description:**
Larch bolete is to larch woodland what slippery jack is to Scots pine: the dominant bolete of that specific habitat, abundant in good conditions, and reliable enough to fill a bag when other species are scarce. The mycorrhizal association with larch (*Larix* spp.) is specific — this species does not occur under any other tree, which makes the identification picture straightforward.

The cap is more vivid than slippery jack: bright yellow to golden-orange-brown, slimy in the same way, and similarly needing the cap skin peeled before cooking. The pore surface is yellow. The ring on the stem is present as in slippery jack but tends to be less prominent.

The flavour is mild. It is not a species to seek out over cep or chanterelle, but in larch woodland in October, when it can be genuinely abundant, it fills the forager's bag with something edible and useful. The mild flavour suits preparations where the mushroom is a texture and body component rather than the flavour driver — soups, mixed mushroom dishes, pickles.

Larch is a deciduous conifer, unusual in dropping its needles in autumn. The bright green of new larch needles in spring and the golden colour of dying needles in October make it easy to identify. Where larch grows — in plantations throughout Britain, in parkland, and in shelterbelts — larch bolete grows beneath it.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Cap is 4–10cm, convex to broadly flat, bright yellow to golden-orange-brown, distinctly slimy or sticky in moist conditions. Underside: pore surface is yellow to golden-yellow, fine-pored, 1–2 per mm. Stem is 4–8cm, pale yellow to buff, with a ring that may be white above and yellow-stained below from spores, sometimes fragmenting to leave only a ring-zone. Flesh is pale yellow, not changing colour significantly when cut. Grows under larch. Spore print is cinnamon-brown. Peel the cap skin before use.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Larch bolete has no dangerous lookalikes under larch trees. Other *Suillus* species grow under different conifers — slippery jack under Scots pine, *Suillus bovinus* under pine — and do not occur under larch. The slimy yellow to orange-brown cap, yellow pores, ring, and larch association are collectively specific to *Suillus grevillei* and immediately separate it from any dangerous species.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Exclusively under larch (*Larix* spp.), in plantations, parkland, and shelterbelts throughout Britain. Larch is widely planted across the country; wherever mature larch trees grow, this species may be found in autumn. Often produces large numbers of fruiting bodies under productive trees. Look for the golden or pale green needles of larch overhead as confirmation of the host tree.

---

**In Season:**
August through November. Most productive in September and October after rain. The slimy cap is most manageable to peel when the mushroom is fresh. Very wet conditions make the slime more pronounced and handling more difficult. Pick into a separate bag or bag each mushroom individually to prevent slime transferring to other species.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Peel the cap skin before use: grip the skin at the cap edge and pull toward the centre in strips. The yellow pore layer can also be removed if preferred. Pan-fry in butter or oil over medium heat. Season with salt and pepper. The mild flavour suits mixed mushroom dishes, soups, and pickles. Pickled larch bolete is a traditional preparation in central European cooking: blanch briefly, cool, and pack in seasoned vinegar with dill, peppercorns, and bay. Dried: slice and dry at 80°C; the flavour concentrates moderately.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking larch bolete from public woodland for personal use is permitted. Commercial picking without landowner permission is not permitted.

---

**SEO Title:** Larch Bolete — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find larch boletes under larch trees from August. Slimy yellow-orange cap — peel before cooking. Abundant in larch woodland. No dangerous lookalikes. Mild, useful flavour.

---
---

## 168. Common Mallow

**Species Name:** Common Mallow
**Latin Name:** Malva sylvestris
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** common-mallow
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Common mallow (*Malva sylvestris*) grows on roadsides, waste ground, and field margins throughout Britain from June. The flowers, leaves, and seedpods are all edible. The flowers are the most attractive ingredient: purple-veined, good raw, and striking as a garnish. Young leaves are mucilaginous when cooked and have been used as a thickener. The seedpods, known as cheeses, are edible raw.

---

**Full Description:**
Common mallow is one of the most widespread wild flowers in Britain, growing on roadsides, rubble, waste ground, and disturbed field margins throughout the country. It has a long history of use as a food plant: in times of scarcity it was eaten in quantity as a pot herb, and the mucilaginous quality of the cooked leaves was valued for its thickening properties in soups and stews. Today the interest is primarily in the flowers and seedpods.

The flowers are the best part. They are large — 2.5–4cm across — with five petals deeply veined in purple-pink against a pale lilac background. Raw, they have a mild, slightly nutty flavour with a pleasant soft texture. Scattered over a salad or used as a garnish they are one of the more visually effective edible flowers available in the summer hedgerow.

The small, disc-shaped seedpods are known as cheeses — named for the shape, which resembles a round of cheese sliced into segments. They are edible raw when green and tender, with a mild flavour and a slight chewiness. Children have eaten them as a hedgerow snack for centuries.

Young leaves cooked are edible but the texture — sticky and slightly mucilaginous, like okra — is an acquired taste. They work as a thickener in soups but are not the most appealing green on their own.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Common mallow is a bushy, sprawling plant reaching 45–90cm, with upright to spreading stems covered in soft hairs. Leaves are 4–7cm, rounded with five to seven shallow lobes and a toothed margin, slightly wrinkled in texture. Flowers are 2.5–4cm across, five-petalled, lilac to purple-pink with darker purple veining on each petal — the veining is striking and diagnostic. Flowers appear in clusters in the leaf axils. Seedpods are 8–12mm across, flattened, disc-shaped, divided into ten to twelve segments — the cheese shape. The combination of the distinctly veined purple flowers and the cheese-shaped seedpods on a bushy roadside plant is unmistakeable.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Common mallow has no dangerous lookalikes. Dwarf mallow (*Malva neglecta*) is a smaller, more prostrate species with smaller flowers; it is also edible and the two species are used identically. Tree mallow (*Malva arborea*) is taller (up to 3m), woody at the base, and also edible. No toxic British plant produces the same distinctly veined purple flowers on a bushy hairy plant with cheese-shaped seedpods.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Roadsides, waste ground, disturbed field margins, rubble, and the bases of walls. Found throughout Britain, most common in the south and east of England. Tends to grow in open, disturbed ground rather than in managed grassland or dense vegetation. Often found on chalk and limestone soils. A roadside or building site plant more than a hedgerow or woodland plant.

---

**In Season:**
Flowers from June through September. Leaves available from spring, but best while young and before the plant flowers. Seedpods develop from July onward as each flower fades. The cheeses are best while still green and tender; they harden and become unpleasant as they dry. Pick flowers on dry mornings when fully open.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Flowers: scatter over green salads or grain salads for colour and a mild flavour. Use as a garnish on summer desserts or open sandwiches. Freeze in ice cubes for drinks. The petals can be used to make a tea: steep a small handful in hot water for five minutes — the liquid turns slightly blue-purple. Leaves: cook briefly in boiling water, drain, and use as a thickening agent in soup. The mucilaginous texture is pronounced — good for a thick, hearty broth, less appropriate in dishes where texture should be light. Cheeses: eat raw as a snack from the plant. Add to salads whole for visual interest.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking common mallow from public roadsides and waste ground for personal use is permitted. The plant is not protected. It grows prolifically on disturbed ground and picking is in no way harmful to the population.

---

**SEO Title:** Common Mallow — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find common mallow on roadsides and waste ground from June. The purple-veined flowers, young leaves, and green seedpods are all edible. Good for salads and garnishes.

---
---

## 169. Yarrow

**Species Name:** Yarrow
**Latin Name:** Achillea millefolium
**Type:** Greens
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** yarrow
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Yarrow (*Achillea millefolium*) grows in grassland, meadows, and roadsides throughout Britain. The feathery, finely divided leaves and flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers are the identifiers. The leaves have a strong, complex, slightly bitter aromatic flavour — use sparingly. Two species require ruling out before picking: hemlock (*Conium maculatum*), which is deadly, and fool's parsley (*Aethusa cynapium*), which is toxic. The smell test is immediate and reliable.

---

**Full Description:**
Yarrow is one of the most common flowering plants in Britain, growing on roadsides, old pasture, meadows, and disturbed ground throughout the country. The feathery, finely divided leaves and flat-topped white flower clusters are familiar to anyone who has walked through summer grassland. The leaves have been used medicinally and as a food flavouring for centuries.

As a food ingredient, yarrow is best used with restraint. The flavour is complex: aromatic, slightly medicinal, bitter, with warm spicy undertones. A few young leaves chopped into a compound butter or added to a grain salad introduce something genuinely interesting. A large amount overpowers everything around it. Think of it as an aromatic herb rather than a cooking green.

The flat-topped flower heads — technically a corymb of many small composite flower heads — are also edible and milder than the leaves. They make a striking garnish and can be used to infuse oils and vinegars.

Yarrow is rated Intermediate because of two dangerous plants that can grow in similar habitats and share some superficial visual similarities with the compound-leaved white-flowered look of the umbellifers. Neither closely resembles yarrow on careful inspection, but the smell test must be done before picking any finely divided-leaved plant from a roadside or meadow.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Yarrow grows to 20–80cm, with upright, slightly woolly stems. Leaves are 5–15cm, very finely bipinnate — divided and redivided into tiny, feathery segments, giving a lacy appearance. The leaves are arranged alternately along the stem, with a characteristic feathery texture that is distinct from the broader leaflets of umbellifers. Flowers are in flat-topped clusters (corymbs) of many tiny composite heads, each 3–5mm across, white (occasionally pale pink). The flower heads are daisy-like at close range — small ray and disc florets — not umbels. Crush any part of the plant: yarrow smells strongly and distinctly aromatic, slightly medicinal, and warm. This smell is specific and immediate.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
**Hemlock (*Conium maculatum*)** is deadly. It grows on roadsides, riverbanks, and disturbed ground — sometimes the same habitats as yarrow. Hemlock is a large umbelliferous plant (1–2m) with hollow stems carrying distinctive purple-red blotching or spotting. The flower clusters are true umbels — like an inverted umbrella — not flat-topped corymbs of composite heads. Hemlock smells musty and distinctly unpleasant. Yarrow smells strongly aromatic and medicinal. Hemlock leaves are broader and more leaf-like; yarrow leaves are very finely cut and feathery. The purple stem spotting alone identifies hemlock. Crush a leaf: if the smell is musty and unpleasant rather than sharp and aromatic, do not pick.

**Fool's parsley (*Aethusa cynapium*)** is toxic and grows in similar disturbed ground and garden settings. It is an umbelliferous plant with umbel flowers and long, conspicuous bracts hanging beneath each flower head. Yarrow has no such bracts. Fool's parsley has an unpleasant smell when crushed; yarrow smells sharply aromatic.

The smell test rules out both in seconds: yarrow's aromatic scent is strong, immediate, and specific. No dangerous plant smells the same.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Roadsides, old pasture, meadows, chalk downland, rough grassland, and disturbed ground. One of the most common flowering plants in Britain. Found in virtually every county and habitat type except dense woodland and waterlogged ground. Particularly abundant on unimproved chalk and limestone grassland in the south of England.

---

**In Season:**
Leaves from spring; best while young before the plant flowers. Flowers from June through September. Pick young leaves from the base of the plant for culinary use; older stem leaves become tougher and more intensely flavoured. Flowers peak in July and August.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Use sparingly — the flavour is strong. Young leaves: chop finely and stir into a compound butter alongside garlic and lemon zest. Use the butter on grilled lamb chops, chicken, or fish. Add a few finely chopped leaves to a grain salad or a vinaigrette. Infuse in oil: pack a small quantity of leaves into warm (not hot) olive oil, steep for a week, strain. The oil has a subtle aromatic character. Flowers: use fresh as a garnish on savoury dishes or in salads. Infuse in white wine vinegar for two weeks for a mildly flavoured aromatic vinegar. Tea: steep a small quantity of fresh or dried leaves and flowers in boiling water for five to eight minutes. The taste is strongly aromatic and slightly bitter; sweeten if preferred.

**Avoid in pregnancy:** yarrow has a long history of use as an emmenagogue. Do not consume in more than food quantities during pregnancy.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking yarrow from public land, roadsides, and commons for personal use is permitted. The plant is not protected and is extremely common.

---

**SEO Title:** Yarrow — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find yarrow in grassland and roadsides from June. Strong aromatic leaves — use sparingly. Crush and smell before picking: rule out deadly hemlock and toxic fool's parsley first.

---
---

## 170. Silverweed

**Species Name:** Silverweed
**Latin Name:** Potentilla anserina
**Type:** Roots
**Difficulty:** Intermediate
**Slug:** silverweed
**Seasons:** May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Silverweed (*Potentilla anserina*) is a common plant of roadsides, damp grassland, and coastal ground throughout Britain. The silvery underside of the pinnate leaves and yellow five-petalled flowers identify it clearly. The roots are the edible part: small, whitish, and starchy, with a nutty, parsnip-like flavour. Digging roots requires the landowner's permission and is only worth the effort on sandy or loose soil where a useful quantity can be retrieved.

---

**Full Description:**
Silverweed is one of the most recognisable plants on a rough roadside verge or coastal track: the trailing stems, the pinnate leaves with their distinctly silvery-white undersides, and the bright yellow five-petalled flowers make it easy to identify at a glance. What fewer people know is that the roots have a long history as a famine food and a starchy staple in northern Britain, where they were eaten roasted, boiled, or dried and ground into flour in lean times.

The root is small — rarely more than 1cm in diameter, often thinner — and resembles a slender parsnip in colour and texture when cooked. The flavour is pleasant: starchy, slightly sweet, nutty. It requires honest effort to dig enough roots for a meaningful meal, and the digging is most productive on sandy, coastal, or loose soils where the roots run freely and can be retrieved without breaking. On heavy clay or compacted verge soil, the roots are thin, short, and barely worth the attempt.

The rating is Intermediate partly because digging roots always requires the landowner's permission (unlike picking above-ground parts) and partly because the soil conditions must be read correctly to make the effort worthwhile.

Silverweed spreads by runners across the ground surface — the same growth habit as strawberry — and forms dense mats in suitable conditions. It is extremely common and not at any conservation risk from moderate personal picking.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Silverweed is a low-growing, mat-forming perennial spreading by long, slender red runners that root at intervals. Leaves are pinnate with 7–13 pairs of toothed leaflets plus smaller leaflets between each pair, 3–20cm long total. The upper surface is green; the underside is distinctly silvery-white from a dense coating of silky white hairs — the defining visual feature visible from a distance. Flowers are bright yellow, five-petalled, 15–25mm across, single on upright stalks from the runners. No dangerous plant has pinnate leaves with the same silvery underside combined with a yellow five-petalled flower on a trailing runner in this habitat.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Silverweed has no dangerous lookalikes. Creeping cinquefoil (*Potentilla reptans*) also has yellow five-petalled flowers and trails by runners, but its leaves are palmate — five leaflets radiating from a central point — not pinnate. It is also edible. Tormentil (*Potentilla erecta*) grows in similar habitats but has four-petalled yellow flowers rather than five, and upright rather than trailing stems. Also edible.

No toxic plant closely resembles silverweed when the silvery underside of the leaf is observed.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Roadsides, rough grassland, damp meadows, coastal tracks, sand dunes, and the upper edges of saltmarshes. Found throughout Britain on a range of soils, though most productive for root digging on sandy or loose ground. Particularly abundant on coastal paths and dunes in the north and west of Britain. Tolerates periodic flooding, grazing, and trampling; common on farm tracks, field gateways, and any area of regularly disturbed or compacted ground.

---

**In Season:**
Roots can be dug year-round but are best in spring before the plant flowers — the stored starch has not yet been diverted into flowering and growth — and in autumn after the plant dies back. The above-ground growth identifies the plant throughout the growing season (May to September). Dig in spring for the best flavour and starch content; autumn roots are also good.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Dig roots on sandy or coastal ground where retrieval is practical. Rinse thoroughly. Boil in salted water for 10–15 minutes until tender; the skin can be rubbed away after cooking. The flavour is mild and starchy — close to a small parsnip or a mild potato. Season simply with butter and salt. Roast: toss with oil and roast at 200°C for 20–25 minutes. The flavour deepens and sweetens on roasting. Historically, dried and ground silverweed roots were used as a flour substitute — this is labour-intensive but produces a mildly flavoured flour usable in flatbreads. As a side vegetable, the roots need a generous picking session to produce a meaningful quantity.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Digging roots always requires the landowner's permission, regardless of the public access status of the land. The Countryside and Rights of Way Act 2000 permits picking above-ground parts for personal use on access land, but explicitly excludes uprooting. On private farmland, roadsides, and coastal paths, confirm with the landowner before digging. Silverweed is not a protected species and is extremely common; the conservation position is straightforward, but the legal requirement for permission when digging is firm.

---

**SEO Title:** Silverweed — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find silverweed on roadsides and coastal ground. The starchy roots taste of parsnip — best dug from sandy soil in spring. Always get landowner permission before digging.

---
