## 171. Wood Violet

**Species Name:** Wood Violet
**Latin Name:** Viola riviniana
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** wood-violet
**Seasons:** Mar, Apr, May

---

**Short Description:**
Wood violet (*Viola riviniana*) is the most common wild violet in Britain, flowering in woodland, hedgebanks, and grassland from March. The pale to mid-purple flowers are edible raw. The flavour is very mild and mildly sweet. The main use is visual: scattered over a salad or dessert, violet flowers are one of the most elegant wild garnishes available in spring.

---

**Full Description:**
Wood violet is as much a part of the British spring woodland floor as bluebell and wood anemone. It grows throughout Britain — in deciduous woodland, on shaded hedgebanks, along stream sides, and in old grassland — and flowers from March through into May. In a good spring, a shaded bank covered in violet flowers alongside wood anemone and lesser celandine is one of the better arguments for being outdoors in March.

The flowers are the edible part. The flavour is subtle to the point of near-imperceptibility: very mildly sweet, slightly floral. The interest is almost entirely visual. A handful of violet flowers scattered over a pale dish — poached eggs, white bean soup, soft cheese, a lemon tart — provides colour that very few other wild ingredients can match.

The leaves are also edible: mild, slightly mucilaginous when cooked, used historically as a pot herb and a thickener. They are nutritious and inoffensive but not compelling as a vegetable. The flowers are the point.

Wood violet can be distinguished from sweet violet (*Viola odorata*) by its spur: the spur at the back of the wood violet flower is pale, often slightly notched at the tip, and the same colour as or paler than the petals. Sweet violet has a darker, more curved spur and a very distinctive scent that wood violet lacks. Both are edible; the distinction matters more for scent than for culinary use.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Wood violet is a small, clump-forming perennial, 5–15cm tall, spreading by stolons. Leaves are 2–4cm, heart-shaped with a rounded tip and a finely crenated margin, mid-green, on long stalks. Flowers are 1.5–2.5cm across, five-petalled, pale to mid-purple, occasionally lilac or white, with darker veining on the lower petal that acts as a nectar guide. The spur at the back of the flower is pale, blunt-tipped, and slightly notched. Petals are asymmetrical — two upper, two lateral, one lower with the veining. No scent, or only the faintest trace. Common dog violet (*Viola canina*) has similar flowers but grows in grassland and heath rather than woodland; also edible. The heart-shaped leaves, purple flowers, and shaded woodland or hedgebank habitat identify wood violet reliably in spring.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Wood violet has no dangerous lookalikes. All native British *Viola* species are edible. Ground ivy (*Glechoma hederacea*) has small purple flowers in shaded ground at the same season but its flowers are distinctly two-lipped and tubular, quite different from the flat five-petalled violet flower. Ground ivy is also edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Deciduous woodland, shaded hedgebanks, stream sides, and occasionally old grassland with light shade. Found throughout Britain. One of the most common spring-flowering plants in lowland deciduous woodland. Often grows alongside primrose, wood anemone, and lesser celandine on the woodland floor.

---

**In Season:**
Flowers from March, peaking in April. By May the main flowering is over, though some flowers appear into early June in cooler, shadier conditions. The leaves remain through summer and can be picked sparingly at any point. Pick flowers on dry mornings when fully open.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw garnish: scatter flowers over pale dishes immediately before serving — poached eggs, soft white cheese, panna cotta, lemon tarts, or creamy soups. The colour is the reason. Candied: paint petals with beaten egg white, dust with caster sugar, and dry in a very low oven (50°C for 45 minutes). Use to decorate cakes and desserts. Violet syrup: steep a generous handful of flowers in 200ml of boiling water for 24 hours. Strain, add 150g of sugar, and dissolve over low heat. The syrup is faintly purple and delicately floral. Add a squeeze of lemon juice — it turns the syrup from green-grey to bright pink, a useful trick. Use over ice cream or to flavour sparkling water. Frozen in ice cubes: press a flower face-down into each ice cube compartment, fill with water, and freeze. Use in cold drinks.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking wood violet flowers and leaves from public woodland and land for personal use is permitted. Do not uproot the plant. Wood violet is a food plant for several fritillary butterfly species, particularly pearl-bordered and small pearl-bordered fritillaries; avoid stripping flowers from small populations in areas known to support these butterflies.

---

**SEO Title:** Wood Violet — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick wood violet flowers in woodland from March. Very mild flavour — the interest is visual. Scatter over pale dishes, candy for cakes, or make a delicate violet syrup.

---
---

## 172. Sweet Violet

**Species Name:** Sweet Violet
**Latin Name:** Viola odorata
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** sweet-violet
**Seasons:** Feb, Mar, Apr

---

**Short Description:**
Sweet violet (*Viola odorata*) is the fragrant violet of old hedgebanks and woodland edges, flowering from February. The scent is distinctive: intensely floral, sweet, and instantly recognisable. The flowers are edible and the fragrance carries into culinary preparations — violet syrup, candied petals, and infused sugar. Pick it for the scent as much as the flavour.

---

**Full Description:**
Sweet violet flowers earlier than any other British violet — sometimes from late January in a mild year, reliably from February. The scent is one of the most intensely floral of any wild plant in Britain: sweet, heady, distinctly violet, and slightly powdery. It is the scent behind traditional violet perfume, violet sweets, and the flavouring called "parma violet."

The flowers are small — 1.5–2cm — but the scent carries at a distance. On a warm February morning on a sheltered south-facing bank, a patch of sweet violet in full flower makes its presence known before it is seen. Find the scent, then find the plant.

The culinary interest follows the scent. Violet syrup made with sweet violet flowers is genuinely fragrant and floral in a way that wood violet syrup is not — the scent is strong enough to infuse into a syrup and remain detectable in the finished product. Candied sweet violet petals retain the fragrance after drying and are the classic confectionery decoration of French pâtisserie. The flowers can also be used to infuse cream, milk, or white chocolate for desserts that carry a genuine violet character.

The leaves are edible and were used historically as a pot herb and a thickener; the mucilaginous quality of cooked violet leaves was valued in soups and stews in the same way as mallow. The leaves are available for most of the growing season, long after the flowers have finished.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Sweet violet is a small, stoloniferous perennial, 5–15cm. Leaves are 2–5cm, heart-shaped, with a rounded tip and finely toothed margin, darkening and becoming more robust through spring and summer. The key identifier is the scent: the flowers smell intensely and specifically of sweet violet. Flowers are 1.5–2.5cm, five-petalled, deep purple to pale lilac (occasionally white, in the form *Viola odorata* f. *alba*), with a darker, more hooked spur than wood violet. The spur is the same colour as or slightly darker than the petals. Stems are absent — the flower stalks arise directly from the base of the plant. Spreads by runners (stolons) and often forms patches on sheltered south-facing banks. The scent is the definitive identifier: no other early-flowering violet in Britain smells of sweet violet.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Sweet violet has no dangerous lookalikes. All native British *Viola* species are edible. The scent alone distinguishes sweet violet from every other *Viola*. No toxic British plant produces a similar small purple five-petalled flower with the same intense fragrance on a sheltered hedgebank in February.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Sheltered hedgebanks, old woodland edges, ancient churchyards, and scrubby chalk grassland. Prefers south or west-facing exposures on chalk, limestone, or fertile well-drained soils. More common in the south of England, particularly on chalk and limestone; less frequent in the north and Scotland. Often found on old estate walls and at the edges of ancient woodland where the soil has not been disturbed. Patches persist in the same location for decades once established.

---

**In Season:**
February through April, with February and March being the main flowering period in the south. One of the earliest flowering wild plants in the calendar. The flowers are sensitive to hard frost and may temporarily close during cold spells, reopening on warm days. By late April the flowers have largely finished. Leaves are available through summer; they are much larger by June than in February.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Violet syrup: pack flowers loosely into a jar. Pour 200ml of boiling water over them and steep for 24–48 hours, covered. Strain. Dissolve 200g of sugar in the strained liquid over low heat. A squeeze of lemon juice turns the syrup from murky green to a clear pink-purple. Bottle and refrigerate. Use over ice cream, in sparkling water, or to flavour cream and desserts. The fragrance genuinely carries. Candied petals: paint individual petals with beaten egg white, dust with caster sugar, and dry at 50°C for an hour. The fragrance persists in the dried petal. Use on cakes, tarts, and chocolates. Violet infused cream: steep flowers in warm cream for two hours, strain, and chill. Use in panna cotta or as a cream for a spring dessert. Infused sugar: layer flowers between spoonfuls of caster sugar in a sealed jar. Leave for a week. The sugar takes on the scent. Use in shortbread or meringue.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking sweet violet flowers and leaves from public land, roadsides, and commons for personal use is permitted. Do not uproot the plant. Sweet violet is a food plant for dark green fritillary and other fritillary butterfly larvae; avoid excessive picking from any small colony.

---

**SEO Title:** Sweet Violet — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick sweet violets from sheltered banks from February. The most fragrant British violet — the scent infuses into syrups, cream, and candied petals. Flowers early, fades by April.

---
---

## 173. Wild Pansy

**Species Name:** Wild Pansy
**Latin Name:** Viola tricolor
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** wild-pansy
**Seasons:** Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Wild pansy (*Viola tricolor*) is the native ancestor of the cultivated pansy, growing in arable field margins, old grassland, and sand dunes throughout Britain. The flowers are tricoloured — typically purple, yellow, and white — and edible, with a mild, very slightly sweet flavour. Use them fresh as a garnish. The flowering season runs from April through to September, making this one of the longer-season edible flowers available.

---

**Full Description:**
Wild pansy is the plant from which the cultivated garden pansy was bred, and the family likeness is clear in the face-like arrangement of petals. In the wild, the flowers are smaller — typically 1.5–2.5cm across — and more variable in colouring than cultivated varieties. The classic form has purple upper petals, paler lilac side petals, and yellow lower petals, but pure yellow, pure purple, and intermediate forms all occur.

The plant grows in two distinct forms depending on habitat. Heartease (*Viola tricolor* subsp. *tricolor*) grows in arable fields, disturbed ground, and light soils — an annual or short-lived perennial. Field pansy (*Viola arvensis*, sometimes considered a separate species) is found in the same arable habitats and has predominantly cream-yellow flowers. Mountain pansy (*Viola lutea*) grows on upland grassland and limestone, usually with yellow flowers, and is also edible. All are good.

The flowers are the edible part. The flavour is mild and slightly sweet, not strongly distinctive. As with violets, the visual effect is the main culinary reason for picking them — they are colourful, recognisable, and cheerful additions to food. The long season makes them more useful than most edible wild flowers; you can find them in bloom from April right through to September in suitable habitats.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Wild pansy is a slender, branching plant, 10–40cm, often sprawling. Leaves are 1–4cm, oval to lanceolate, with a rounded tip and crenated margins. Stipules at the base of the leaf stalk are distinctive: large, pinnately lobed, leaf-like, with a prominent terminal lobe — these large stipules are a reliable identifier and distinguish the pansies from other violets. Flowers are 1.5–3cm, with five petals: typically two purple upper petals, two lateral petals of varying colour, and one lower petal with a short spur. The lower petal usually has dark veining as a nectar guide. The "face" arrangement of petal colours gives the flower its common name. Flowers throughout the growing season from April.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Wild pansy has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British plant produces a similar five-petalled tricoloured flower with the distinctive large, pinnately lobed stipules. All British *Viola* species are edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Arable field margins, disturbed sandy and chalky soils, old meadows, coastal dunes, and rough grassland. Found throughout Britain, though most common on lighter soils. Particularly abundant on chalk downland and coastal dunes in the south of England; mountain pansy (*Viola lutea*) replaces it on upland limestone and moorland grassland in northern England, Wales, and Scotland.

---

**In Season:**
April through September, with the main flowering from May to July. One of the longer-season edible flowers in the British wild calendar. Pick when flowers are fully open and in good condition. Individual plants continue producing flowers over a long period.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Raw garnish: the most effective use. Scatter fresh flowers over salads, grain dishes, avocado toast, or desserts. The tricoloured face makes each flower visually specific and recognisable. Frozen in ice cubes: press flowers face-down into ice cube trays, fill with water, and freeze. Use in cold drinks for a visual effect. Crystallised: coat with egg white and caster sugar as for violet petals, dry in a very low oven, and use as cake decoration. Infused in oil or vinegar: steep a small quantity of flowers in white wine vinegar or a mild oil for a week; the flavour contribution is mild. Use the vinegar in dressings for summery salads.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking wild pansy flowers from public land, roadsides, and field margins for personal use is permitted. Do not uproot the plant. In arable settings the plant grows at field edges; stay on accessible margins. Mountain pansy populations in upland areas are sometimes in SSSIs; personal flower-picking is generally unproblematic.

---

**SEO Title:** Wild Pansy — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick wild pansy flowers from grassland and field margins from April. Edible tricoloured flowers — the ancestor of the garden pansy. Use fresh as a garnish through to September.

---
---

## 174. Meadowsweet

**Species Name:** Meadowsweet
**Latin Name:** Filipendula ulmaria
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** meadowsweet
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug

---

**Short Description:**
Meadowsweet (*Filipendula ulmaria*) flowers from June in damp meadows, riverbanks, and wet woodland edges. The frothy white flower heads fill the summer air with one of the most distinctive smells in the British countryside: sweet, almond-like, with a clean herbal note underneath. The flowers infuse beautifully into syrups, creams, and drinks. The flavour is as good as the smell.

---

**Full Description:**
Meadowsweet is one of the great aromatic plants of the British summer. Find a river meadow in July, and the scent — sweet, heady, reminiscent of almonds and marzipan with a clean, slightly floral herbal note — carries on the air before the plant is visible. It was one of the most prized strewing herbs of the medieval period, used to scent floors, rooms, and ceremonial occasions. The scent comes from salicylates and related compounds in the flowers and leaves.

The flowers are the culinary target: dense, frothy clusters of tiny white flowers, cream to ivory, with a complex fragrance that infuses easily into liquids. The flavour is aligned with the smell — sweet, almond-like, gently perfumed — and is particularly effective in cream-based preparations, syrups, and drinks.

Meadowsweet has historical significance beyond the kitchen. Salicylic acid, derived from meadowsweet, was the precursor compound from which aspirin was synthesised in the late 19th century — the name "aspirin" derives from the old genus name for meadowsweet, *Spiraea*. People who are sensitive to aspirin should be cautious with meadowsweet in quantity, though culinary use of the flowers in normal amounts is very unlikely to cause problems.

The leaves have a much stronger, more medicinal smell than the flowers — more astringent and less pleasant. They are occasionally used to flavour soups but are not the primary reason to pick this plant. The flowers are what matters.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Meadowsweet is a tall, upright perennial, 60–120cm. Stems are reddish, slightly ridged. Leaves are pinnate with pairs of oval, toothed leaflets 2–8cm, interspersed with smaller leaflets between the main pairs — a distinctive pattern. The upper surface is dark green; the underside is pale and slightly downy. Both surfaces smell strongly of the characteristic meadowsweet scent when crushed, more pleasantly from the flower clusters than the leaves. Flowers are tiny, five-petalled, cream to white, in dense branched clusters (corymbs) 8–20cm across, appearing from June. The combination of the pinnate leaves with their specific pattern of alternating large and small leaflets, the tall reddish stems, the dense white flower clusters, and the overwhelming sweet almond scent is entirely specific to meadowsweet in its damp lowland habitat.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Meadowsweet has no dangerous lookalikes. The scent alone identifies it beyond any doubt. No toxic British plant produces white frothy flower clusters on a tall plant with the same sweet almond fragrance in wet meadow habitat. Elder (*Sambucus nigra*) also produces flat-topped white flower clusters but is a shrub or tree with simple pinnate leaves and a completely different smell.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Wet meadows, riverbanks, fens, wet woodland rides, and the margins of streams and ditches. Found throughout Britain wherever the ground is reliably moist and the vegetation is not too dense. One of the most common flowering plants in lowland wet grassland. Often grows in quantity alongside purple loosestrife, ragged robin, and water mint. Most productive at river meadow sites in July.

---

**In Season:**
June through August, with peak flowering in July. The flower clusters develop over several weeks as the outer flowers open before the inner buds. Pick clusters that are just fully open — the fragrance is strongest at this stage. Over-ripe, browning clusters smell less pleasantly and are not worth using. The window in any given location is about three to four weeks.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Infuse in cream or milk: bring 500ml of cream or milk to a simmer, add a large handful of flower clusters, remove from heat, cover, and steep for two hours. Strain and chill. Use in panna cotta, ice cream, or crème brûlée. The flavour is exceptional. Syrup: steep flowers in 200ml of boiling water for 24 hours; strain, add 200g of sugar, dissolve over low heat. Use over fruit, in sparkling water, or in cocktails. Meadowsweet cordial: make a concentrated syrup and bottle it — it keeps refrigerated for several weeks. Lemonade: add a quantity of meadowsweet syrup to fresh lemon juice and sparkling water for a summer drink that is better than the sum of its parts. Infuse into white wine or gin: pack a small quantity of flowers into a bottle for a week. The flavour infuses well without overwhelming the base drink. Desserts: use meadowsweet-infused cream or milk as the base for any custard, tart, or mousse.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking meadowsweet flowers from public land, wet meadows, riverbanks, and commons for personal use is permitted. The plant is not protected. Do not pick from sites where meadowsweet management is being carried out for conservation purposes — it is an indicator of unimproved wet grassland, which is a priority habitat type.

---

**SEO Title:** Meadowsweet — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick meadowsweet flowers from wet meadows and riverbanks in July. Sweet almond fragrance infuses beautifully into cream, syrup, and drinks. One of Britain's best edible wild flowers.

---
---

## 175. Red Clover

**Species Name:** Red Clover
**Latin Name:** Trifolium pratense
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** red-clover
**Seasons:** May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Red clover (*Trifolium pratense*) grows in grassland and meadows throughout Britain from May. The pink-purple flower heads are edible, with a mild, sweet, slightly beany flavour. Both the flowers and the young leaves can be eaten. The flowers make a pleasant tea and work well scattered over salads. One of the most accessible and abundant edible flowers in Britain.

---

**Full Description:**
Red clover grows almost everywhere in Britain where there is grassland: roadsides, meadows, old pasture, railway banks, and rough ground. The flowering heads appear from May and continue through September, making this one of the longest-season edible flowers available. Abundance is the point: where most edible wild flowers require searching, red clover is simply there.

The flavour is mild and pleasant — slightly sweet, with a faint beany or grassy note that distinguishes it from more intensely flavoured flowers. The petals are more sweet than the calyx (the green base of the flower head); pulling individual petals from the head and eating them avoids the slightly bitter base. Eaten whole, the head is perfectly pleasant but the calyx adds a slight astringency.

The flowers have a long history of use in tea. Dried red clover heads steeped in boiling water produce a mild, sweet, slightly floral tea that is genuinely pleasant. They can be blended with meadowsweet, mint, or lime blossom for a more complex wild herb tea.

Young, tender leaflets can also be eaten: raw in small amounts (the raw leaves are mildly indigestible in quantity), or cooked briefly and used as a pot herb. The young leaflets have a mild, slightly grassy flavour.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Red clover is a sprawling to upright perennial, 20–60cm. Leaves are trifoliate — three leaflets, each 2–4cm, oval with a distinctive pale chevron marking on the upper surface. This chevron or V-mark is a reliable identifier on most plants. Flower heads are 2–3cm across, rounded, made up of many small, tubular pea-like florets, deep pink to purple-red. The heads sit on upright stems above the leaves. No spur. White clover (*Trifolium repens*) has smaller, white flower heads and smaller leaflets, also edible. Zigzag clover (*Trifolium medium*) has similar flowers but slightly different leaves; also edible. The combination of trifoliate leaves with the pale V-mark and the rounded pink flower heads identifies red clover immediately.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Red clover has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British plant produces a similar rounded pink-purple clover flower head on a plant with trifoliate, V-marked leaves. All common British clovers are edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Grassland, meadows, roadsides, hedgebanks, and rough ground throughout Britain. One of the most widespread native wild flowers. Thrives on well-drained, moderately fertile soils. Less common on very acid or very waterlogged ground. Common as a constituent of old meadow and as an agricultural crop; look for it on unimproved grassland where the sward is not heavily managed.

---

**In Season:**
May through September. Most abundant in June and July. Individual plants continue flowering over a long period, producing fresh heads as older ones fade. By September the flowers are finishing, though late-season plants in mild autumns continue into October.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Tea: dry flower heads in a warm oven (50°C, two hours) or in the air for several days. Steep one or two dried heads in boiling water for five to seven minutes. The flavour is mild, sweet, and floral. Blend with dried mint, dried lime blossom, or dried meadowsweet for a more complex wild tea. Fresh garnish: pull individual petals from the flower head and scatter over salads, grain dishes, or desserts. The colour is good and the flavour mild enough not to overpower. Added to bread: incorporate dried clover flowers into soda bread or flatbreads — both the flavour and the colour from the dried petals work well. Infused in honey: pack flower heads into a jar and cover with runny honey. Seal and leave for two weeks. The honey takes on a floral note. Strain if preferred. Young leaves: add very small amounts raw to salads, or cook briefly as a pot herb in soups. Do not eat large quantities of raw leaf.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking red clover flowers from public grassland, roadsides, and commons for personal use is permitted. The plant is not protected. As an important nectar source for bumblebees, particularly long-tongued species, avoid picking from small patches during the peak bee season (June to August). Pick from large stands where the impact is negligible.

---

**SEO Title:** Red Clover — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick red clover flowers from grassland from May. Mild, sweet flavour — scatter over salads or dry for tea. Abundant and long-season. No dangerous lookalikes.

---
---

## 176. Evening Primrose

**Species Name:** Evening Primrose
**Latin Name:** Oenothera biennis
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** evening-primrose
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Evening primrose (*Oenothera biennis*) is a tall, striking plant of disturbed ground, roadsides, and coastal dunes, producing large, bright yellow flowers from June. The flowers open in the evening and are edible, with a mild, slightly sweet flavour. The first-year root is also edible. Not native to Britain, but naturalised across much of the country and freely available.

---

**Full Description:**
Evening primrose is North American in origin and was introduced to Europe in the 17th century, initially as a curiosity in botanical gardens. It has since naturalised extensively, particularly on disturbed sandy soils, roadsides, railway banks, and coastal dunes. It is now a common sight across much of England and Wales.

The name comes from the habit of the flowers: they open in the evening, reaching full size by dusk, and pollinated primarily by moths. By the following midday, most flowers have wilted. This means the picking window is either late evening or very early morning — flowers picked at noon are already past their best.

The flowers are large for a wild plant — 4–6cm across — and a vivid, clean yellow. They are mildly sweet with a faint floral quality. The petals are soft and tender, pleasant in salads or as a garnish on any dish where a yellow note is wanted.

The first-year root — the plant is a biennial, forming a rosette in its first year and flowering in its second — is edible when boiled. The flavour is mild and slightly mucilaginous, compared in some sources to parsnip or salsify. It requires the same digging permission rules as any root plant and is only worth the effort on soft, sandy soils where the root develops well.

The seed oil from evening primrose (*evening primrose oil*) has a commercial market as a dietary supplement; the wild plant's seeds contain the same oil, though in quantities too small to be practically useful from a foraging session.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Evening primrose is a biennial, forming a basal rosette of leaves (10–30cm, lance-shaped, slightly wavy-edged) in its first year and sending up a tall, leafy, flowering stem (60–150cm) in its second. The stem leaves are similar to the rosette leaves but smaller and stalkless. Flowers are 4–6cm across, four-petalled, bright yellow, opening in the evening. The sepals (four, reflexed) and the stigma (X-shaped) are visible when the flower is closely inspected. Seed capsules develop as long, narrow pods up to 4cm. Grows on disturbed ground, roadsides, and dunes. The tall second-year plant with large yellow four-petalled flowers opening in the evening on disturbed sandy ground is unmistakeable.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Evening primrose has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British plant produces a similar large four-petalled yellow flower on a tall stem with the same X-shaped stigma and reflexed sepals. Common ragwort (*Jacobaea vulgaris*) has yellow flowers on disturbed ground but these are daisy-like composite heads, not four-petalled, and the plant has quite different leaves. Ragwort is toxic; the flowers are entirely different in structure from evening primrose.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Disturbed sandy and gravelly ground, roadsides, railway banks, coastal dunes and shingle, and waste ground. Most common in England and Wales. Particularly abundant on the light sandy soils of East Anglia, the south coast dunes, and on recently disturbed urban ground. Less frequent in Scotland and on heavy clay soils.

---

**In Season:**
Flowers from June through September. Individual flowers open in the evening and wilt by the following day; the plant produces new flowers over a period of weeks. Pick flowers in the early morning or late evening for best condition. First-year roots are available from autumn through to the following spring before the plant flowers.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Flowers: scatter petals over salads, grain dishes, or desserts immediately before serving. The yellow is vivid and effective. The petals can also be added to a batter for fritters — four or five flowers briefly dipped in a light batter and fried at 180°C for one minute. Eat immediately. The petals wilt very quickly. Root: dig first-year rosettes in autumn or spring. Scrub thoroughly. Boil in salted water for 15–20 minutes until tender. The texture is soft and slightly mucilaginous; serve with butter and salt or incorporate into a soup. The flavour is mild. Young leaves: the very young leaves of first-year rosettes can be added to salads in small quantities; they are mild and slightly succulent. Seed: the seeds are tiny and oily — edible, but too small to be worth collecting separately.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking evening primrose flowers and leaves from public land, roadsides, and waste ground for personal use is permitted. Digging roots requires the landowner's permission. The plant is not native to Britain and its spread into disturbed ground is not a conservation concern; personal picking has no meaningful impact on the population.

---

**SEO Title:** Evening Primrose — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find evening primrose on roadsides and coastal dunes from June. Large yellow four-petalled flowers open at dusk. Edible petals and first-year root. Naturalised across Britain.

---
---

## 177. Lime Blossom

**Species Name:** Lime Blossom
**Latin Name:** Tilia cordata / Tilia platyphyllos
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** lime-blossom
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul

---

**Short Description:**
Lime blossom from the small-leaved lime (*Tilia cordata*) and large-leaved lime (*Tilia platyphyllos*) flowers in June and July. The small, cream-yellow flowers hang in clusters from distinctive strap-like bracts. The fragrance is mild and honeyed. Lime blossom tea is one of the most widely drunk wild herb teas in Europe. The flowers can also be used to flavour syrups, creams, and desserts.

---

**Full Description:**
Two native lime species grow in Britain: small-leaved lime (*Tilia cordata*) and large-leaved lime (*Tilia platyphyllos*). Both produce edible flowers and are treated identically for foraging purposes. A third, the common lime (*Tilia x europaea*), is a hybrid of the two and is the most widely planted lime tree in British towns and parks; its blossom is also edible, though it has been associated with reports of sedation in bees, and some sources recommend using native species in preference. The native species are the better choice where available.

Small-leaved lime is a native tree of ancient woodland on limestone, most common in parts of the Wye Valley, the Chilterns, and parts of Yorkshire and the Lake District. It is the more restricted of the two native species and often indicates ancient woodland. Large-leaved lime is rarer still. The hybrid common lime is abundant everywhere in parks and streets.

The blossom is small, cream-yellow, with five petals and a prominent cluster of stamens. The key identifier is the distinctive pale, strap-like bract from which the flower stalk hangs. This bract — 4–8cm, attached to the stem of the flower cluster for part of its length — is specific to *Tilia* species and identifies the tree immediately when present.

The flavour of the flowers is mild and honeyed, with a delicate floral quality. Lime blossom tea (*tilleul* in French) is a classic herbal tea across Europe, widely available dried and used as a calming evening drink. Fresh blossom makes a better tea than dried. The flavour also works in syrups, custards, and creams.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Small-leaved lime is a medium to large tree reaching 20–30m, with a rounded crown. Leaves are 3–9cm, heart-shaped, with a pointed tip and finely toothed margin. The underside has small tufts of orange-brown hairs in the vein axils — a useful identifier for *T. cordata* specifically. Flowers are 8–15mm, cream-yellow, in clusters of 4–10 hanging from a pale, strap-like bract 4–8cm long. The bract is partly joined to the flower stalk. Flowers appear in late June and July. The combination of the lime leaf shape and the distinctive bract structure identifies the tree reliably. Flowers in July at the height of the season fill the air with a mild, sweet fragrance.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Lime blossom has no dangerous lookalikes. The strap-like bract from which the flower clusters hang is specific to *Tilia* species and no toxic British tree produces a similar blossom structure. Elder (*Sambucus nigra*) flowers in flat-topped clusters from a different type of shrub; quite different.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Native small-leaved lime in ancient woodland on limestone and calcareous soils in the Wye Valley, Chilterns, Yorkshire Dales, and Lake District; large-leaved lime is rarer and more localised. Common lime is planted throughout Britain in parks, avenues, town squares, and along roadsides. For most foragers, common lime blossom from park and street trees is the most accessible source.

---

**In Season:**
June and July. Small-leaved lime flowers slightly later than common lime — typically early to mid-July. Common lime flowers from late June. The flowering window at any given tree is one to two weeks. The fragrance from a large lime tree in full flower is noticeable from some distance. Collect when flowers are freshly open and the stamens are still bright yellow.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Tea: the primary use. Steep a small handful of fresh flower clusters in boiling water for five to eight minutes. Strain. The tea is mild, honeyed, and slightly floral. Dried blossom can be stored for year-round use: spread on a tray and dry at 50°C for two hours, or air-dry for several days. Store in a sealed jar. Syrup: steep flowers in 200ml of boiling water for 24 hours. Strain, add 200g of sugar, dissolve over low heat. Use in sparkling water, over ice cream, or in cocktails. Lime blossom and strawberry are an excellent pairing. Infused cream: steep a small handful of flowers in warm cream for two hours. Strain and use in any cream-based dessert. The flavour is delicate — do not compete with strong flavours. Honey: add a few fresh flower heads to a jar of runny honey and seal for two weeks; the honey takes on a gentle lime blossom note.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking lime blossom from public park and street trees for personal use is generally unproblematic. For trees on private land, obtain the owner's permission. Do not strip entire branches or climb the tree. A forager's pole or a long reach on lower branches is adequate for a personal quantity. Picking from native lime in ancient woodland sites should be done with restraint — the blossom is an important nectar source for pollinators and ancient woodland trees should not be over-picked.

---

**SEO Title:** Lime Blossom — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick lime blossom from park and street trees in June and July. Mild, honeyed flavour for tea, syrup, and cream. The distinctive strap-like bract identifies the tree.

---
---

## 178. Borage

**Species Name:** Borage
**Latin Name:** Borago officinalis
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** borage
**Seasons:** Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep

---

**Short Description:**
Borage (*Borago officinalis*) is not native to Britain but has naturalised widely from garden escapes and is found on waste ground, roadsides, and disturbed soil throughout England. The vivid blue, star-shaped flowers are among the most striking edible flowers available in summer. The flavour is mild and refreshing — faintly of cucumber. Use them fresh in drinks, salads, and as a garnish. They freeze well in ice cubes.

---

**Full Description:**
Borage is Mediterranean in origin and was introduced to Britain for culinary and medicinal use, probably in the medieval period. It escapes readily from gardens and has established on roadsides, waste ground, and disturbed urban soil across much of England. It is less common in Wales and Scotland, where conditions are cooler and less suitable.

The flowers are the main reason to pick it. The colour is exceptional: a vivid, clear blue — one of very few truly blue edible flowers available in Britain — with a distinctive black anther cone at the centre. The five reflexed petals give the flower a star shape. The flavour is mild, clean, and slightly cooling — genuinely reminiscent of cucumber, which makes the traditional association with summer drinks (particularly Pimm's) a logical one.

Borage contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids in the leaves and stems — compounds that can be harmful to the liver in large quantities and over a long period of regular consumption. The flowers contain very small amounts and are generally considered safe for normal culinary use. The leaves are edible in small quantities but regular, large consumption of the leaves is not recommended. The flowers are the sensible focus.

The plant is a self-seeder and, once established in a garden or on a nearby piece of ground, tends to reappear annually without intervention.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Borage is an upright, branching annual, 30–80cm, covered in stiff white hairs that give the whole plant a rough, bristly texture. Leaves are 5–15cm, oval to lance-shaped, with a coarsely toothed or wavy margin and a wrinkled surface — the hairiness and rough texture are immediately noticeable. Flowers are 2–3cm across, five-petalled, reflexed, vivid sky-blue to occasionally white, with a central cone of black anthers. The flowers are borne in drooping clusters that straighten as they open. The vivid blue colour, star-shaped five-petalled flower, black anther cone, and bristly hairy plant on disturbed ground are together completely specific.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Borage has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British plant produces a similar vivid blue star-shaped flower on a bristly-hairy stem. Green alkanet (*Pentaglottis sempervirens*) has smaller blue flowers on a hairy plant in similar habitats; it is in the same family as borage and is also edible, with a similar mild flavour. Viper's bugloss (*Echium vulgare*) has pink-purple to blue flowers in a different, spike-like arrangement and grows on chalk and coastal grassland; also edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Waste ground, roadsides, disturbed urban soil, field margins, and coastal scrub, particularly on light or sandy soils. Found throughout England, most common in the south and east. Less frequent in Wales and Scotland. Often found near gardens where it has self-seeded. Thrives on poor, well-drained soils in sunny positions.

---

**In Season:**
June through September, sometimes earlier in warm years. A long-season plant that continues producing new flowers over several months. Individual flowers are short-lived but the plant flowers successively. Pick flowers on dry days when fully open.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Ice cubes: the primary visual use. Press a flower face-down into each ice cube compartment, fill with water, and freeze. The blue flower frozen in clear ice is striking in a summer drink. In Pimm's, gin and tonic, or lemonade. Garnish: scatter fresh flowers over salads, cheese boards, or cold fish dishes. The blue is dramatic against green or white. Candied: paint petals with egg white, dust with caster sugar, and dry at 50°C. The blue colour fades slightly to a lavender-blue — still effective. Cucumber soup: add a few fresh flowers to a chilled cucumber soup immediately before serving. The cucumber flavour of the flowers and the soup align. Flavoured butter: mix finely chopped flowers into softened butter with a little salt and lemon zest; use on grilled fish or cold bread.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking borage flowers from public land, waste ground, and roadsides for personal use is permitted. The plant is not native and not protected. Its presence on disturbed ground is common enough that picking has no meaningful impact.

---

**SEO Title:** Borage — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find borage on waste ground and roadsides from June. Vivid blue star-shaped flowers with a mild cucumber flavour. Freeze in ice cubes or use fresh as a summer garnish.

---
---

## 179. Ox-eye Daisy

**Species Name:** Ox-eye Daisy
**Latin Name:** Leucanthemum vulgare
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** ox-eye-daisy
**Seasons:** May, Jun, Jul, Aug

---

**Short Description:**
Ox-eye daisy (*Leucanthemum vulgare*) is one of the most abundant flowering plants in British grassland and roadsides from May. The whole plant is edible: flowers, leaves, and young shoots. The flowers are the most useful — mild, slightly bitter, used as a garnish or in salads. The leaves are more bitter and better cooked or added sparingly. Find it anywhere grassland is not heavily managed and it is usually abundant.

---

**Full Description:**
Ox-eye daisy is one of the defining sights of an unimproved British meadow in June: hundreds of large white daisies above the grass, swaying in the breeze, the classic image of the British summer. It grows on roadsides, old meadows, chalk grassland, railway banks, and any ground where the sward is not too heavily managed or fertilised.

The whole plant is edible, and this is one of the more versatile wild flowers for culinary use given the number of different parts available across the season. The flowers are the mildest and most visually appealing. The petals are white and the disc is yellow; the flavour is mild and slightly bitter, similar to chamomile but without the intensity. The disc florets are slightly more intensely flavoured than the petals.

The young leaves and shoots in spring — before the plant flowers — are the best eating of the non-flower parts: mild, slightly peppery, with a bitterness that is more pleasant raw than later in the season. Older leaves become increasingly bitter and are better cooked in small quantities rather than eaten raw in quantity.

Ox-eye daisy was known as a pot herb in medieval and early modern Britain and was included in the category of "salad herbs" — plants used to provide green bulk, flavour, and variety in cooked dishes. Its mild bitterness works well in the same context as chicory or dandelion — as a counterpoint to richer ingredients.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Ox-eye daisy is an upright perennial, 30–70cm. Lower leaves are 3–8cm, spoon-shaped to oblong with a rounded tip and a toothed or lobed margin, on long stalks. Stem leaves are smaller, stalkless, and clasp the stem. Flowers are 3–5cm across: white ray florets (15–30 per head) surrounding a yellow disc, on upright stems. The familiar daisy structure. No smell or very faint. The combination of large, white-rayed composite flowers on upright stems in grassland from May identifies ox-eye daisy. Scentless mayweed (*Tripleurospermum inodorum*) has similar flowers but a more branched, sprawling habit and finely divided, feathery leaves — quite different foliage. Also edible. Shasta daisy (*Leucanthemum x superbum*), a garden plant that occasionally escapes, is larger and also edible.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Ox-eye daisy has no dangerous lookalikes. No toxic British plant produces a similar large white-rayed composite flower head on an upright stem in unimproved grassland. Corn marigold (*Glebionis segetum*) has entirely yellow flowers on similar ground; it is edible. Chamomile (*Chamaemelum nobile*) has a strong apple-like scent and a domed disc; it grows in closely grazed turf and is also edible.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Old meadows, chalk and limestone grassland, roadsides, railway banks, coastal cliffs, and disturbed ground. Found throughout Britain. Particularly abundant on chalk and limestone downland in the south of England and on unimproved grassland throughout Wales and northern England. Common on road verges wherever the grass is cut after flowering rather than before. One of the most visible flowers of the summer roadside.

---

**In Season:**
May through August, with the main flowering from late May through July. Young shoots and leaves are available from March before the plant flowers. The flowers are at their best in June and July. By August many plants are setting seed, though a second flush of flowers sometimes occurs in early autumn.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Flowers: scatter fresh flower heads or individual petals over salads, grain dishes, or cold plates for a summery garnish. The disc florets, removed from the head, have a more concentrated flavour and can be used as a flavouring in oils and vinegars. Infused oil: steep flower heads in warm olive oil for a week. Strain. The oil has a very mild, slightly bitter floral quality — use in salad dressings. Young leaves (spring): add small amounts raw to mixed salads for a mild, slightly peppery note. Use in the same way as dandelion leaves or young chicory. Cooked leaves: wilt in butter or olive oil for two to three minutes. The bitterness softens on cooking. Use as a side green or stir into risotto or pasta in place of other greens. Tempura flowers: coat heads in a light batter and fry at 180°C for one to two minutes. Drain and serve immediately. The disc holds the batter well.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking ox-eye daisy flowers and leaves from public land, roadsides, and commons for personal use is permitted. The plant is not protected. As a significant nectar plant for a wide range of insects, pick from large stands rather than small patches. Do not uproot the plant.

---

**SEO Title:** Ox-eye Daisy — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Find ox-eye daisies in grassland and roadsides from May. Flowers, young leaves, and shoots all edible. Mild, slightly bitter. Scatter over salads or fry in tempura batter.

---
---

## 180. Dog Violet

**Species Name:** Dog Violet
**Latin Name:** Viola canina
**Type:** Flowers
**Difficulty:** Beginner
**Slug:** dog-violet
**Seasons:** Apr, May, Jun

---

**Short Description:**
Dog violet (*Viola canina*) grows on heathland, chalk grassland, and the edges of scrub and woodland throughout Britain from April. The blue-violet flowers are edible and mild. Unlike sweet violet, it has no scent. Use the flowers as a garnish in the same way as wood violet — for colour rather than flavour. The plant is the food plant of several fritillary butterfly species: pick lightly.

---

**Full Description:**
Dog violet is less celebrated than sweet violet, which got the perfume, and less common than wood violet, which fills the woodland floor. It occupies a different habitat: open heathland, chalk downland, dry grassland, and the scrubby margins of heath and wood where the soil is poor and the competition lower. It flowers a little later than its relatives — mainly April into June — and the flowers are a slightly cleaner, more sky-blue-violet than the richer purple of wood violet.

The name "dog" in British plant naming traditionally indicated a plant considered inferior — the "dog" version of the valued sweet violet, which had the scent that made it worth growing in gardens. Dog violet has no scent at all. This is a practical fact rather than a criticism: the flowers are still edible, still useful as a garnish, and the plant is interesting in its own right.

The primary culinary use is the same as for wood violet and sweet violet: the flowers scattered over pale dishes, frozen in ice cubes, crystallised, or briefly infused in syrup. The flavour is mild and essentially neutral; the visual contribution is the point.

Dog violet is a significant food plant for several fritillary butterfly species, including pearl-bordered fritillary, small pearl-bordered fritillary, high brown fritillary, and silver-washed fritillary. On heathland and chalk downland where these butterflies still occur, pick modestly from large patches and avoid any small, isolated colonies that may be critical breeding habitat.

---

**Identification Notes:**
Dog violet is a small, clump-forming perennial, 5–20cm. Unlike wood violet and sweet violet, which spread by stolons, dog violet does not typically produce long runners. Leaves are 1.5–4cm, broadly heart-shaped to triangular-heart-shaped, with a toothed margin. The leaves are often a slightly more angular heart shape than wood violet. Stipules at the leaf base are lance-shaped and fringed — narrower than the large, leaf-like stipules of wild pansy. Flowers are 1.5–2.5cm, five-petalled, blue-violet to pale blue, with a white or pale spur (the spur distinguishing it from heath dog violet *Viola canina* var., which has a yellowish spur). No scent. Grows on heathland, dry grassland, and chalk downland rather than in shade. The pale, scent-free blue-violet flower on open heath or downland, rather than in woodland, is the consistent identifier alongside the lack of any fragrance.

---

**Lookalikes & Dangers:**
Dog violet has no dangerous lookalikes. All native British *Viola* species are edible. Heath dog violet (*Viola canina* subsp. *canina*) and hairy violet (*Viola hirta*) grow in similar open habitats and are also edible. The distinction between these species is mainly botanical and irrelevant for foraging. No toxic British plant produces a similar small blue five-petalled flower in heathland and chalk grassland habitat.

If in doubt, leave it out.

---

**Habitat:**
Heathland, chalk and limestone grassland, the edges of scrub, old mine workings on poor soils, and the dry, open margins of woodland where the soil is light and not too fertile. Found throughout Britain but most common on the chalk and limestone of southern England, the heathlands of Surrey, Dorset, and Suffolk, and on upland grassland in Wales and northern England. Less shade-tolerant than wood violet.

---

**In Season:**
April through June, with the main flowering in April and May. By June the flowers are largely finished. Pick when fully open on dry mornings. The leaves are available longer but are mild enough to be of limited culinary interest compared to the flowers.

---

**Culinary Uses:**
Use exactly as wood violet. Raw garnish: scatter over pale dishes for colour. The blue-violet is slightly different in tone to wood violet's deeper purple — both are effective. Frozen in ice cubes: press face-down into ice cube compartments, fill with water, and freeze. Crystallised: coat in egg white and caster sugar, dry at 50°C for an hour. Use on cakes and desserts. Violet syrup: steep a handful of flowers in hot water for 24 hours; strain, sweeten, and add lemon juice to bring up the colour. The lack of scent means the flavour is milder than sweet violet syrup, but the colour is useful.

---

**Legal Notes:**
Picking dog violet flowers from public heathland, downland, and commons for personal use is permitted. Do not uproot the plant. Dog violet is a critical food plant for several threatened fritillary butterfly species in Britain; pick very lightly from any site known to support these butterflies and avoid picking from small isolated patches. Pick from large, well-established colonies on productive sites.

---

**SEO Title:** Dog Violet — Foraging Guide UK | The Foragers

**SEO Description:** Pick dog violet flowers on heathland and chalk grassland from April. Mild, scent-free blue-violet flowers — use fresh as a garnish or freeze in ice cubes. Food plant for fritillary butterflies.

---
